The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
0.08s15 GUI tests collect cleanBuild succeeded. 0 Warning(s). 0 Error(s).compiled without the display
Act IILesson 68unit 19 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A lock you cannot run green yet must still verify everything the missing resource does not gate — so its first real run is a confirmation, not a discovery.

The lesson

A lock you cannot run green yet must still verify everything the missing resource does not gate, so its first real run is a confirmation, not a discovery. "Built, not verified" is honest only when the unverified part is precisely the part that needs the hardware — a lock whose first run debugs the lock never locked anything.

Growing from it

The mechanism: an unrun test accumulates two kinds of unknowns — defects in the system and defects in the test itself — and the missing resource only excuses the first kind. Harness bugs, import errors, wrong assertions, and broken setup have nothing to do with the GPU or the display; deferring them until the hardware session means spending scarce hardware time debugging your own scaffolding. The discipline that transfers: when a verification needs a resource you do not have, decompose it — what does the resource actually gate? Run everything else now: compile the artifact, collect the tests, drive the real code paths against a stand-in for only the missing piece, and write down exactly what remains unproven. The deferred run then has one job — confirming the irreducible hardware-bound step — and a failure there is information, not noise.

Act IILesson 68unit 19 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A lock you cannot run green yet must still verify everything the missing resource does not gate — so its first real run is a confirmation, not a discovery.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV): verification vs. validation

TEVV (test, evaluation, verification and validation) under NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function distinguishes verification (built to spec) from validation (performs in the real context). Staged locks exemplify the split deliberately: structural verification — real functions, real order, real assertions — completes without the hardware, so the later hardware run is pure validation of the one resource-bound property. For Domain III.A, governing the building of an AI system includes governing its test assets: an unexercised test is itself an unverified artifact.

AIGP takeaway Stub only what the missing resource gates; verify everything else now, so the first hardware run confirms rather than discovers.
Act IILesson 70unit 20 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A regression lock only locks if the gate that actually runs includes its scope.

The failure

Sprint 16 existed to build locks that actually fire. Its production-parity tests landed green in `tests/integration/` — and the canonical standing-gate command (`shared/ services/ launcher/`) excluded that directory, so a green gate said nothing about whether the lane passed. Wiring the fix surfaced the same gap one layer worse: `tests/security/` — the posture guards that fail loudly if dev-mode ships as the default, if the test-isolation conftest is removed, or if an external-network import appears — was in neither the gate nor `testpaths`. The most consequential locks in the repository were orphaned. The close folded both into the standing gate (2187 green became 2212) and set the default pytest filter (`addopts`) to deselect only the `hardware`, `winui`, and `slow` markers, so the locks fire on the ordinary run, not only when someone remembers a long command.

Evidence: 2026-06-07 — Closing the debt the sprint existed to close
Before you turn the page

The new lock was green and the standing gate was green. What third fact has to be true before those two greens protect anything?

Act IILesson 70unit 20 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A regression lock only locks if the gate that actually runs includes its scope.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
2187→2212gate after folding the lanes
Act IILesson 70unit 20 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A regression lock only locks if the gate that actually runs includes its scope.

The lesson

A regression lock only locks if the gate that actually runs includes its scope. Where a lock lives and where the standing gate looks are two separate facts that must be reconciled, or the lock is decorative — a green gate run proves nothing about a lane it never selects.

Growing from it

The mechanism is an aliasing problem: "the tests pass" silently means "the tests the gate selects pass," and nothing forces the selection to grow when the test estate does. Every suite has an effective scope — directories, markers, default filters — and every lock added outside it inherits a false sense of protection from the green signal beside it. In any project, treat the gate's selection as a configuration artifact to audit: when you add a lock, run the standing command and confirm the new tests appear in its counts; periodically diff what exists on disk against what the gate collects, because orphaned directories accumulate exactly where new kinds of test (integration, security posture) get invented. The strongest position is making the default command the complete one, so protection does not depend on remembering the long version.

Act IILesson 70unit 20 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A regression lock only locks if the gate that actually runs includes its scope.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentISO/IEC 42001 — Statement of Applicability (controls included/excluded as an explicit record)NIST AI RMF — GOVERN

ISO/IEC 42001's management-system discipline asks an organization to know which controls are actually in effect — its Statement of Applicability makes inclusion and exclusion an explicit, justified record rather than an accident of configuration. An orphaned test lane is the anti-pattern: a control documented and built, but excluded from the check that runs, with no record of the exclusion. NIST AI RMF's GOVERN function carries the same idea — policies and accountability structures must make the operative control set visible.

AIGP takeaway Where a lock lives and where the gate looks are separate facts — reconcile them, or the lock is decorative.
Act IILesson 74unit 21 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A `@hardware`/`@slow` tier must SKIP on every axis it needs, not just the headline one.

The failure

The model-loaded production-boot tier was supposed to be safe to run anywhere under its `hardware` marker (a tag letting the standing gate deselect tests that need real hardware): skip if under-provisioned, never fail. Its skip guard checked only `tpm_signer.is_available()`. The dev box has a real TPM 2.0 (Trusted Platform Module security chip), so the guard passed, the tier ran — and FAILED at the Policy Agent's measured-boot weight gate with `PA_CFG_KGM_PATH_NOT_FOUND`, because that worktree had no models on disk. A tier that fails on a TPM-present-but-models-absent machine turns "I ran the hardware suite on an incomplete box" into a red instead of a skip — exactly the noise the marker exists to prevent. The fix widened the guard to the full prerequisite set — TPM reachable AND production model material present AND the audit key provisioned — caught only because the agent actually ran `-m hardware` on its own box.

Evidence: 2026-06-07 — The production boot that two services boot differently
Before you turn the page

The marker said "hardware" and the guard checked the chip. How many other things did that test actually need from its environment — and what checked those?

Act IILesson 74unit 21 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A `@hardware`/`@slow` tier must SKIP on every axis it needs, not just the headline one.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act IILesson 74unit 21 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A `@hardware`/`@slow` tier must SKIP on every axis it needs, not just the headline one.

The lesson

A `@hardware`/`@slow` tier must SKIP on every axis it needs, not just the headline one. The marker's point is that the tier is safe to run anywhere, so the skip guard must cover the full prerequisite set — chip AND keys AND model material — and you only learn it does by running the tier on an under-provisioned box. A hardware tier you never tried to run is as untrustworthy as a stand-in tier you never ran green.

Growing from it

The mechanism: a skip guard encodes the author's mental list of prerequisites, and that list anchors on whatever the tier is named for — "hardware" suggested the chip, so the chip got checked, while models and keys rode along as unexamined assumptions. Conditional tests are themselves code with their own failure modes, and a guard nobody has watched fire in both directions is as unverified as an assertion nobody has watched go red. The transferable habit: enumerate every external precondition a conditional test consumes — devices, files, credentials, network, provisioned state — and make the guard check the complete set, ideally by reusing the same resolution logic production uses. Then prove it empirically: run the tier on a box missing each prerequisite and confirm a clean skip, because the false red you prevent is the credibility of every future red.

Act IILesson 74unit 21 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A `@hardware`/`@slow` tier must SKIP on every axis it needs, not just the headline one.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)NIST AI RMF — trustworthy characteristic: valid and reliable

NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function calls for assessing and tracking risks with methods that stay valid across contexts, and "valid and reliable" heads its trustworthy-AI characteristics. A test tier that reports failure because the measurement environment was incomplete is an unreliable instrument: it manufactures false signal that erodes trust in true signal. Governing testing under Domain III includes governing the test estate's own quality — the conditions under which each check is meaningful must be encoded, and themselves verified.

AIGP takeaway A skip guard must cover every prerequisite — and proving it means running the tier on a box that lacks them.
Act IILesson 77unit 22 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A red test is a claim about your test before it is a claim about the code.

The failure

Walking the whole boot-security chain as one automated cascade, the rotation assertion — "a second per-boot certificate mint must produce distinct cert material" — went red: two PEM certificate files byte-identical, identical serial numbers, even though the minting code calls `x509.random_serial_number()`. For a moment it looked like a real fail-to-rotate defect, escalation-worthy. The actual fault was in the test's own ordering: `provision_per_boot_certs` rotates in place, overwriting the same `certs/` paths, and the test captured the first boot's cert bytes after the second mint had already overwritten the file — diffing the current file against itself. The rotation had been real the whole time. The fix was three lines (capture the first boot's bytes immediately, before the re-mint); the near-miss was reporting a fail-to-rotate bug that did not exist. The real-TPM tier already had the ordering right, which is how the agent knew where to look.

Evidence: 2026-06-07 — Walking the whole trust chain at once, and the rotation check that lied to me
Before you turn the page

Two certificates compared byte-identical. Before concluding the rotation is broken, what question should you ask about when — and what — the test actually read?

Act IILesson 77unit 22 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A red test is a claim about your test before it is a claim about the code.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
byte-identicalthe file diffed against itselfthree linesthe fix after scrutiny
Act IILesson 77unit 22 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A red test is a claim about your test before it is a claim about the code.

The lesson

A red test is a claim about your test before it is a claim about the code. Scrutinise the harness's own timing, ordering, and assumptions before escalating a red as a code defect — the proactive-fix mandate is for confirmed defects, and an unconfirmed red can manufacture one that isn't there.

Growing from it

The mechanism: a test is a second program observing the first, and it can be wrong in all the ways programs are wrong — race its subject, read state at the wrong moment, assert on the wrong object. When a test goes red, two hypotheses are live: the code is broken, or the observation is. The pull toward the first is strong because a defect is the interesting story and the test was "just checking" — but the test is the newer, less-exercised code, which makes it the statistically likelier fault. Build the reflex: before escalating any red, re-derive what the test actually measured at the moment it measured it — what file, what bytes, what ordering — and look for a sibling check that disagrees (here, the hardware tier with correct ordering). Escalate only what survives that scrutiny; false alarms spend credibility you will want later.

Act IILesson 77unit 22 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A red test is a claim about your test before it is a claim about the code.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)NIST AI RMF — trustworthy characteristic: valid and reliable

TEVV (test, evaluation, verification and validation) under NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function is only as good as the measurement's own validity — "valid and reliable" applies to the instrument before it applies to the system. A red produced by the test's own ordering is a measurement artifact; escalating it would have spent governance attention on a defect that did not exist. Domain III practice includes qualifying the evaluation itself: confirm the observation before the finding, because a testing program's credibility is part of its control value.

AIGP takeaway When a test goes red, the test is a suspect too — confirm the observation before escalating the finding.
Act IILesson 78core pathunit 23 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Mechanism-exists is not disaster-tested — the missing catastrophe test is the actual deliverable.

The failure

The offline DEK (data-encryption-key) recovery wrap — the break-glass path for when the TPM security chip dies or the hardware is migrated — had shipped in Sprint 14, and the §5.5 tracker even recorded the mechanism as DONE. Yet every existing test faked "the chip is gone" by monkeypatching a live sealer (patching its unseal method to raise an error mid-test) — the sealing key still one attribute-set away in the same process. That is not hardware migration. What was missing was the fresh-machine lock: provision on machine A, carry only the keystore file and the recovery key to a machine B whose sealer cannot unseal at all, and decrypt real data byte-for-byte out the far side. The new `tests/security/test_key_recovery.py` — 34 tests with `_NoTpmSealer` and `_DeadChipSealer` stand-ins — models the dead machine honestly, including the refusals: wrong key, tampered wrap, foreign ciphertext, all fail closed.

Evidence: 2026-06-07 — The recovery key was already there; what was missing was proof it works on a dead machine
Before you turn the page

Every test "killed" the chip by making a live object raise an error. What does a genuinely dead machine NOT have that every one of those tests still had?

Act IILesson 78core pathunit 23 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Mechanism-exists is not disaster-tested — the missing catastrophe test is the actual deliverable.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
DONE→34 teststracker claim vs disaster proof
Act IILesson 78core pathunit 23 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Mechanism-exists is not disaster-tested — the missing catastrophe test is the actual deliverable.

The lesson

Mechanism-exists is not disaster-tested — the missing catastrophe test is the actual deliverable. When a control's whole reason to exist is a rare catastrophe (dead chip, hardware migration), the load-bearing test reconstructs that catastrophe end to end — fresh environment, only the surviving artifacts, real data out — not a monkeypatch of the failure symptom.

Growing from it

The mechanism: simulating a failure's symptom is cheap, so that is what test suites accumulate — an exception raised here, a return value forced there — while the disaster itself removes everything the convenient simulation keeps: the same process, the same memory, the keys an attribute away. The gap matters most for break-glass paths, because they run for the first time on the worst day, with no second chance and nobody calm. For any recovery control you own — backups, key escrow, failover, restore-from-export — ask what the real catastrophe leaves behind, and build the test that starts from exactly that and nothing more: a fresh environment, only the artifacts that would survive, and success defined as the real data coming out the far side. If that test has never run, the recovery story is a hypothesis with a deadline.

Act IILesson 78core pathunit 23 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Mechanism-exists is not disaster-tested — the missing catastrophe test is the actual deliverable.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — trustworthy characteristic: secure and resilientOECD AI Principles — robustness, security and safety

NIST AI RMF lists secure and resilient among its trustworthy-AI characteristics, and the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety carries the same demand: the system must withstand the adverse event it was designed for, not a simulation of its symptom. Under MEASURE, TEVV (test, evaluation, verification and validation) validation means proving the recovery does its real job in the real context — a fresh machine, surviving artifacts only. A DONE tracker entry for a recovery control never exercised against its disaster is documentation outrunning evidence.

AIGP takeaway Break-glass controls are validated only by reconstructing the catastrophe end to end: fresh environment, surviving artifacts only, real data out.
Act IILesson 79unit 24 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A static config lock and a runtime posture guard are not redundant — they catch different regressions.

The failure

`test_secure_defaults.py` had guarded `dev_mode = false` since Sprint 12 — a static lock that reads the shipped TOML config file and asserts the file still declares production. Real, but only half: it proves the config on disk is secure and stops there. It cannot prove a production boot actually resolves `dev_mode` to False at runtime, nor that production-only fail-closed behaviours engage — an inverted override default, a stray `BLARAI_DEV_MODE` environment leak into the resolver, or a service quietly ignoring the override would sail past the file lock and surface only at the operator's terminal. The Sprint-16 coverage audit logged the gap as GAP-12 (#600); the Sprint-17 fix added the dynamic complement — twelve gate-tier assertions through the exact resolution chain the launcher runs, the sharpest being that in production posture with no keystore configured, the session store refuses to start, where dev mode happily builds a software-backed one.

Evidence: 2026-06-07 — The posture the file swears to versus the posture the boot resolves
Before you turn the page

The file on disk says `dev_mode = false`, and a test proves it. List the steps between that file and the running process — which of them does the test see?

Act IILesson 79unit 24 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A static config lock and a runtime posture guard are not redundant — they catch different regressions.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
twelveruntime assertions, zero skips
Act IILesson 79unit 24 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A static config lock and a runtime posture guard are not redundant — they catch different regressions.

The lesson

A static config lock and a runtime posture guard are not redundant — they catch different regressions. When a security flag has a resolution path (environment, override, mode default) and behaviours that branch on it, lock BOTH ends: the declared default and the resolved runtime value, each paired with a dev-mode control so neither assertion can pass vacuously.

Growing from it

The mechanism: between a configuration file and the behaviour it governs runs a resolution pipeline — environment variables, overrides, mode defaults, code that consults the flag — and every stage is a place where the file's truth can fail to become the system's truth. A static check pins only the first link; the regressions that hurt live in the links after it. When you protect any security-relevant setting, trace its full path from declaration to the behaviour that branches on it, and assert at both ends: the artifact says the right thing, and the running system acts the right way. Pair every positive assertion with a control case proving the opposite posture behaves differently — otherwise a bug that makes resolution always return the "safe" value would render your production assertions vacuously green, guarding nothing.

Act IILesson 79unit 24 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A static config lock and a runtime posture guard are not redundant — they catch different regressions.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV): verification vs. validationEU AI Act — accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity (high-risk obligation, concept level)

The verification-versus-validation distinction inside NIST AI RMF's TEVV (test, evaluation, verification and validation) maps exactly onto the two locks: the static check verifies the configuration artifact matches spec; the runtime guard validates that the deployed system actually resolves and enforces the secure posture. EU AI Act high-risk obligations pair accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity with record-keeping for the same reason — a documented posture and a demonstrated one are different evidence. Domain III release governance wants both ends locked.

AIGP takeaway Lock the declared default AND the resolved runtime posture — a file that swears production cannot prove the boot agrees.
Act IILesson 81unit 25 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A posture test must own the outcome it asserts on, not borrow it from the environment.

The failure

Stream J's production-posture guard was twelve green tests in its isolated worktree (a separate working copy of the repository). The merge gate, re-run on the real main tree, turned one red within nine seconds: `test_assistant_orchestrator_production_security_gate_fires` — DID NOT RAISE. The test proved the security-material gate fail-closes "because the manifest isn't there" — true in a `git worktree`, which has no `models/` directory (the multi-gigabyte weights are not in git), false on the dev machine, where the real manifest sits at `models/qwen3-14b/openvino-int4-gpu/manifest.json`. It was measuring whether the box happened to have models installed. The first fix repeated the mistake one layer down: pinning the specific failure code re-introduced host-dependence, since a provisioned box trips on the JWT certificate-authority key (`AO_CFG_JWT_CA_PATH_MISSING`) and a bare worktree on the manifest. The final fix forces the precondition and asserts the invariant — the gate fires — accepting either code.

Evidence: 2026-06-07 — The posture test that passed because the worktree was empty · fix on branch sprint17/j-posture-fix (merge-gate catch, stream J / C5)
Before you turn the page

The test passed twelve-for-twelve in the worktree and failed in nine seconds on the main tree. What was different — and which of the two environments was the test actually written against?

Act IILesson 81unit 25 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A posture test must own the outcome it asserts on, not borrow it from the environment.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
twelve tests→nine secondsworktree green; main-tree red
Act IILesson 81unit 25 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A posture test must own the outcome it asserts on, not borrow it from the environment.

The lesson

A posture test must own the outcome it asserts on, not borrow it from the environment. Force the precondition explicitly AND assert the invariant (the gate is engaged, the control fail-closes), never the environment-decided detail — and never trust a builder's in-worktree green, because the builder may have run on a tree that structurally could not show the failure.

Growing from it

The mechanism: every test runs inside an environment, and any precondition the test does not set explicitly is being set implicitly by whatever machine it runs on — absent files, installed models, provisioned keys. A test that passes because of those accidents is measuring the host, not the code, and it will flip the moment it runs somewhere else. Worse, the dependency can hide a layer down: even after forcing the right precondition, asserting an environment-decided detail (which specific error fires first) re-imports the same fragility. The transferable discipline has two halves. Construct, in the test, every condition your assertion depends on — never lean on "the test tree happens to lack X." And assert at the level of the invariant you actually care about, not the incidental path the environment chose to reach it. Then run the test on a second, differently-provisioned machine before trusting it.

Act IILesson 81unit 25 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A posture test must own the outcome it asserts on, not borrow it from the environment.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)NIST AI RMF — trustworthy characteristic: valid and reliable

For TEVV (test, evaluation, verification and validation) under NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function to produce valid and reliable evidence, results must be reproducible across environments — a check whose verdict depends on the host's provisioning state is not a measurement of the system. The merge-gate practice of re-running the suite on the integrated tree is the process control that catches it, the kind of procedural rigor Domain I.C (policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle) expects a testing regime to encode.

AIGP takeaway Force every precondition and assert the invariant — a test that borrows its outcome from the host measures the host.
Act IILesson 87unit 26 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A process leak that makes tests defensively skip looks like correct behaviour — verify the resource was released by a separate mechanism, because a stable skip count is not a safety net.

The failure

The standing gate had reported a clean 2342 passed / 0 skipped for weeks — then occasionally ~2333 / 9, with no code change. The natural read: someone had BlarAI open, so the boot-cascade tests defensively skipped. The truth: the WinUI harness's bare `proc.terminate()` killed `BlarAI.Desktop.exe` but orphaned the Python backend child it spawns, which kept holding loopback port 5001. The next gate run saw a held port and skipped — correctly, defensively, silently. The "live instance" was a ghost: a leaked child with no parent, no window, serving no one, and the suite reported the failure as a perfectly reasonable skip. The fix: a `terminate_process_tree` helper (`tests/harness/process_tree.py`) reaps the full descendant tree, plus a session-scoped fail-loud detector in the root `conftest.py` that fires only on a free-to-held port delta — the one transition meaning "this session leaked."

Evidence: 2026-06-08 — The skip that wasn't a skip: when a process-leak masquerades as a coverage decision · commits 5698936 + 2c1f373 merged f6193d1
Before you turn the page

The gate sometimes said 9 skips instead of 0, and every skip was individually correct. If the skip logic is right, where else can the defect be?

Act IILesson 87unit 26 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A process leak that makes tests defensively skip looks like correct behaviour — verify the resource was released by a separate mechanism, because a stable skip count is not a safety net.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
2342 passed / 0 skipped→~2333 passed / 9 skippedthe drift with no code change
Act IILesson 87unit 26 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A process leak that makes tests defensively skip looks like correct behaviour — verify the resource was released by a separate mechanism, because a stable skip count is not a safety net.

The lesson

A process leak that makes tests defensively skip looks like correct behaviour — no assertion error, no exception, just a reasonable-sounding skip, with the defect upstream in the teardown that should have released the resource. A skip-guard on a shared resource needs its release verified by a separate fail-loud mechanism; never trust the skip count to stay put.

Growing from it

The mechanism: defensive skips translate an environmental anomaly into a non-failure, so any upstream defect that creates the anomaly gets laundered into "expected behaviour" — the suite's politeness becomes the bug's camouflage. And cleanup code is the least-tested code you own: teardown runs after the assertions, its failures rarely fail anything, and a terminate that kills the parent while orphaning children looks identical to one that worked. The transferable habits: treat skip counts as a monitored signal with an expected value, where any drift is a defect until explained; and for every guard that skips on a held shared resource, add a separate detector that distinguishes "the resource was busy when we arrived" from "we are the ones who left it busy." Choosing fail-loud over silent cleanup is the difference between a regression you see and one you absorb.

Act IILesson 87unit 26 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A process leak that makes tests defensively skip looks like correct behaviour — verify the resource was released by a separate mechanism, because a stable skip count is not a safety net.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)NIST AI RMF — MANAGE (residual-risk monitoring)

NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function includes tracking risk signals over time, and MANAGE includes monitoring residual risk after responses — a skip count is exactly such a signal, and its silent drift was a monitoring failure dressed as test hygiene. The fix exemplifies the accountable and transparent characteristic: the fail-loud detector makes a leaked resource a named, attributable failure rather than an ambient condition. Domain III.C's release-and-monitoring lens treats a drifting baseline as a finding, not noise.

AIGP takeaway Defensive skips can launder an upstream leak into expected behaviour — monitor the skip count and fail loud on resource leaks.
Act IILesson 88unit 27 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A `dev_mode` round-trip proves the routing, not the posture — and the gap between them is the seam production burns on.

The failure

Two existing tests touched the gateway-to-Orchestrator path, and both quietly stepped around the hard part. `test_prompt_round_trip_host_mode` stood up the real listener and a real gateway — with the GPU stubbed and `dev_mode=True`, so no model and no mTLS (mutual TLS, where both ends present certificates). `test_sprint12_real_model` drove the real Qwen3-14B model — through an in-process `_FakeTransport`, so no socket, no handshake. Each was honest about its coverage; neither exercised the composed production walk: real socket, real `CERT_REQUIRED` mTLS, the signature-verified manifest boot, real generation, real policy check. `dev_mode=True` is the single line that made both tests easy and both incomplete. When the new production-posture tier (`dev_mode=False`) first ran on the chip, the boot fail-closed on a test config whose relative model paths resolved under a tmp dir — `AO_CFG_KGM_PATH_NOT_FOUND` — the signed-manifest gate proving it works before the round-trip did.

Evidence: 2026-06-08 — The two ways `dev_mode` lies, and the worktree that wasn't where I wrote · commit e51119d merged b1d7c96 (C1+C2 build) · commit e3dbee0 merged dedf341 (path fix)
Before you turn the page

Both existing tests were honest about their coverage, and both set `dev_mode=True`. What, exactly, does that one line remove from what the tests can prove?

Act IILesson 88unit 27 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A `dev_mode` round-trip proves the routing, not the posture — and the gap between them is the seam production burns on.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…

Act IILesson 88unit 27 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A `dev_mode` round-trip proves the routing, not the posture — and the gap between them is the seam production burns on.

The lesson

A `dev_mode` round-trip proves the routing, not the posture — and the gap between them is the seam production burns on. The production-posture tier (`dev_mode=False`, real mTLS, signed manifest required) is a separate lock: build it explicitly, and mark it `@hardware` so the gate is honest about what has actually run on the chip.

Growing from it

The mechanism: a dev-mode flag exists to make development convenient, which means it disables exactly the expensive parts — handshakes, signature checks, hardware-bound material — and those are the security posture. Tests gravitate toward the convenient configuration for the same reason developers do, so a suite can accumulate genuine end-to-end coverage of the routed path while the enforced path stays unexercised, every test individually honest and the composition untested. The transferable questions: for each flag that simplifies your test setup, what does it turn off, and where does that turned-off behaviour get proven? Routing (does a request travel the path?) and posture (are the controls enforced along it?) are separate claims needing separate locks. And when the posture tier first fail-closes on its own scaffolding, read it as the control working — then fix the scaffolding, not the gate.

Act IILesson 88unit 27 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A `dev_mode` round-trip proves the routing, not the posture — and the gap between them is the seam production burns on.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV): validationEU AI Act — accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity (high-risk obligation, concept level)

Validation in the TEVV sense (test, evaluation, verification and validation, under NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function) means demonstrating the system does its real job in its real context — for a security posture, that context is the production configuration: mutual TLS enforced, the model's signed manifest verified at boot. This maps to the EU AI Act's high-risk concept of accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity, which attaches to the system as deployed, not as conveniently tested. Domain III.C release governance asks that the gate name honestly which posture has actually run.

AIGP takeaway Routing coverage and posture coverage are separate claims — the production configuration needs its own lock, run on real hardware.
Act IILesson 90unit 28 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A mocked seam plus a standalone test of the seamed component do not cover the path that connects them — only a test that drives both in sequence does.

The failure

Two Sprint-18 streams surfaced the same blind spot independently. C3: the Orchestrator's unit tests mock `SemanticRouter.classify()` (replace it with a stand-in), and the one real-router test exercises the router standalone — so the cross-service router-to-Orchestrator path had zero coverage. Grepping the Orchestrator turn revealed worse: the router is called nowhere in the 750-line `_handle_connection` — the seam was not merely untested but unbuilt, ticketed as Vikunja #632 for the built-ahead-versus-gap call. C4: `BlarAIApp`, the terminal user interface, had comprehensive coverage — and every test drove it against a `MagicMock` or a hand-rolled echo server, so `BlarAIApp` to `TransportGateway` to Orchestrator had never run as a system in any automated test. Both gaps closed the same way: a single test driving both real components in sequence on the real path.

Evidence: 2026-06-08 — The mocked seam that hid the wiring gap · commit a57a634 merged be75335 (C3) · 2026-06-08 — The TUI was only ever tested against a mock · commit b59c246 merged 798c8a1 (C4)
Before you turn the page

The router had a real test and the Orchestrator had hundreds. What did the project actually know about the path between them — and how would it learn the path didn't exist?

Act IILesson 90unit 28 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A mocked seam plus a standalone test of the seamed component do not cover the path that connects them — only a test that drives both in sequence does.

Act IILesson 90unit 28 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A mocked seam plus a standalone test of the seamed component do not cover the path that connects them — only a test that drives both in sequence does.

The lesson

A mocked seam plus a standalone test of the seamed component do not cover the path that connects them — only a test that drives both in sequence does. "Component covered" and "seam covered" are different claims, and the gap between them is silent until one side changes — or until you discover the connection was never built at all.

Growing from it

The mechanism: coverage composes the way you wish it would only if something exercises the composition. A mock on one side and a standalone test on the other each verify a half against an assumption about the other half — and the two assumptions are never tested against each other, so the connecting path can drift, break, or (as here) simply not exist while both halves stay green. Notice the deeper tell: a mocked seam can hide not just a defect but an absence, because the mock answers whether or not anything real ever would. The transferable audit: list each boundary in your architecture and ask not "is each side tested?" but "which single test drives both real sides in sequence?" If the answer is none, you hold two component claims and zero system claims about that path — and grep for the call before assuming the path even exists.

Act IILesson 90unit 28 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A mocked seam plus a standalone test of the seamed component do not cover the path that connects them — only a test that drives both in sequence does.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)NIST AI RMF — MAP

NIST AI RMF's MAP function establishes the system's context — its components and how they connect — and MEASURE's TEVV (test, evaluation, verification and validation) must then exercise those connections, not only the parts. Two component greens with a mocked seam between them is a coverage claim that overstates itself; here it concealed an unbuilt integration. The Body of Knowledge v2.1's shift from "AI model" to "AI model and system" points the same way: Domain III governance attaches to the assembled system, and its seams are first-class objects of testing.

AIGP takeaway Two tested components with a mocked seam between them is zero system coverage — one test must drive both in sequence.
Act IILesson 92unit 29 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

The bug lives in the seam, not the parts.

The failure

#638 handed the builder three capability-token containment fixes and named the mechanism for one: add a 'jti' spent-set to enforce single-use of the short-lived signed permission tokens (JWTs — JSON Web Tokens) the Policy Agent mints. The store was built — class, wiring, validation stage, tests — before the reviewing guide course-corrected: single-use was already enforced by the nonce, every token's unique 128-bit number; a replay presents the same nonce and is rejected. The ticket had described the property and guessed at the mechanism. Tearing the redundant store out led to the real bug, subtler than a missing control: the nonce store forgets seen values after 5.0 seconds, while tokens stayed valid for 30 — two defaults set independently, in different files, never made to agree. A token replayed in the 5-to-30-second window passed both checks. The single-use guarantee had a 25-second hole precisely because each component was individually correct.

Evidence: 2026-06-09 — The replay window between two correct components
Before you turn the page

The nonce store is correct, the token lifetime is correct, and every component test is green — where can a 25-second replay window possibly be living?

Act IILesson 92unit 29 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

The bug lives in the seam, not the parts.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
5s→30snonce memory vs token life25-secondthe replay hole between them
Act IILesson 92unit 29 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

The bug lives in the seam, not the parts.

The lesson

Two components that are each individually correct can still leave a hole where they meet, if the contract between them was never specified: a 5-second nonce window and a 30-second token were both reasonable alone and together left a 25-second replay window. When a control depends on two independently-defaulted values agreeing, tie them to one source of truth rather than trusting them to stay in step.

Growing from it

Two mechanisms to carry forward. First: tickets inherited from decks and briefs describe properties but guess mechanisms — verify the named mechanism against the code before building it, or you will duplicate a working control and walk past the real defect (pairs with lesson 76's verify-the-enforcement-point-on-disk). Second, the seam: each component's defaults are chosen locally, reviewed locally, tested locally, so nobody owns the invariant that spans them — and it silently fails to hold. Hunt deliberately for pairs of values that must agree (a cache lifetime versus a token validity, a timeout versus a retry budget, a buffer versus a message size) and ask where the agreement is enforced. The durable fix is structural: derive one value from the other, promote the constant to a single source of truth, and keep a contrast test that documents the exact bug the alignment removes — so any future drift fails loudly.

Act IILesson 92unit 29 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

The bug lives in the seam, not the parts.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)OECD AI Principles — robustness, security and safety

NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function assesses the AI system, not only its parts — and a replay window that exists only in the interaction of two correct components is precisely what component-level evaluation misses. 'Secure and resilient,' the trustworthy characteristic at stake, is a property of the assembled system. Verifying the ticket's claimed mechanism against the code, then pinning the seam with a single source of truth and a regression lock, exemplifies Domain III.A — interface contracts specified, not assumed.

AIGP takeaway Component-correct is not system-correct: specify the contract at every seam, and pin values that must agree to one source of truth.
Act IILesson 114unit 30 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A test double must preserve the variance the assertion depends on

The failure

Stage A of the UC-002/003 ingest program (#655) fixed an overdue defect: the memory substrate had been embedding 2048-character chunks through a 128-token window — an embedding is the numeric vector a model assigns to text — so roughly three quarters of every stored chunk never informed its vector. New knowledge chunks embed at 512 tokens, and a regression test was written to lock the untouched 128-token path. The first stub session — a stand-in for the real model — emitted hidden[s,d] = ids[s] * scale[d], and L2 normalisation, which rescales every vector to length one, cancelled the token dimension entirely: the 128-vs-512 test could not fail no matter what the code did. The stub had to become non-separable before the property was even visible. Within hours an adversarial review returned the favour: the 'vector' retrieval tests were all satisfiable by BM25 keyword scoring alone — the suite passed with the cosine limb deleted.

Evidence: 2026-06-10 — A third failure posture, and the index that lives only in RAM · commits 8efbffc, f5e8bfc
Before you turn the page

Your new regression test is green — what experiment would prove it is even capable of failing, and what structural property of your stub could make that impossible?

Act IILesson 114unit 30 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A test double must preserve the variance the assertion depends on

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
128 tokens→512 tokensembedding window — the numeric property the regression lock guardsthree quartersof every stored chunk never informed its vector under the 128-token window (roughly)
Act IILesson 114unit 30 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A test double must preserve the variance the assertion depends on

The lesson

A test double must preserve the variance the assertion depends on. A stub whose output was mathematically separable in token and dimension let normalisation cancel exactly the truncation effect the regression lock existed to detect — green against a broken property. When a lock guards a numeric property, prove the lock can fail — break the guarded limb on purpose and watch it go red — before trusting that it passes.

Growing from it

A test is a difference detector, and it can only detect differences its inputs express. Doubles built for convenience often collapse the input space — constant outputs, symmetric data, mathematically separable structure — and every collapsed dimension is a property your suite silently stops checking, while staying green. The countermeasure is a mutation-style self-check: before trusting any new lock, make the guarded property false on purpose — comment out the limb, flip the constant, truncate the input — and confirm the test actually fails; if it stays green, the test or its double is blind. Be especially suspicious when assertions sit downstream of transformations like normalisation, rounding, sorting, or aggregation, because each one can absorb the variance you meant to measure. This project proved the recursion within hours: the author's own retrieval tests failed the same standard — the suite passed with the vector-similarity limb deleted — and only deliberate breakage exposed it.

Act IILesson 114unit 30 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A test double must preserve the variance the assertion depends on

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)NIST AI RMF — trustworthy characteristics (valid and reliable)

Test, evaluation, verification, and validation — TEVV, inside NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function — depends on instruments that can actually register the risk they monitor, and an assertion that cannot fail is a measurement that cannot measure. The 'valid and reliable' trustworthy characteristic applies to the evidence-generating apparatus, not only the model. Proving each lock can fail before trusting its green exemplifies Domain III.B — governing the data used in testing, where a convenience stub quietly decided what the suite could see.

AIGP takeaway A test that cannot fail measures nothing: break the guarded property on purpose and watch the lock go red before trusting its green.
Act IILesson 118unit 31 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When an exception class subclasses a builtin, every `except` of the subclass is a claim that the base class cannot reach that spot

The failure

The Stage C guest parser merged green at 131 new tests, and an adversarial review still found a one-frame way to kill it. The chain: ParseChannelError subclasses ValueError, Python's built-in invalid-value error — deliberate, so callers catching the channel error also catch malformed-envelope errors. The parser's _send_error echoed the peer-supplied request_id — a correlation tag — into its error reply, and caught only ParseChannelError around the encode, behind a comment reading '# pragma: no cover — tiny body, cannot overflow.' The premise was false: the id had no length cap, so a request_id of ~65,200 characters fit the incoming 64 KB frame, the echoed reply exceeded the 65,536-byte limit, and the message framer — the component that packs replies into size-capped frames — raised plain ValueError: the base class. The subclass-only catch let it escape all the way up and take down the whole listener. One hostile frame, a third of the supervisor's three-crash budget.

Evidence: 2026-06-11 — Catching the subclass while the base class escaped · commit 3091d98
Before you turn the page

A try block's guarded call can raise the base class, and the except names only the subclass — what claim is that catch silently making, and who ever tests it?

Act IILesson 118unit 31 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When an exception class subclasses a builtin, every `except` of the subclass is a claim that the base class cannot reach that spot

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
131tests green at merge — the adversarial review still found a one-frame listener kill65658the escaped error: 'Message size 65658 exceeds limit 65536' — reproduced exactly before fixing256PARSE_REQUEST_ID_MAX_CHARS — over-long ids now die on the first frame, never recorded
Act IILesson 118unit 31 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When an exception class subclasses a builtin, every `except` of the subclass is a claim that the base class cannot reach that spot

The lesson

Catching the subclass while the framer raises the base class made the catch narrower than the raise surface it guarded — the inversion that stings, because catching ValueError would have subsumed the subclass and been safe. And the pragma comment asserting the path unreachable is precisely what kept coverage tooling from ever asking. A 'cannot happen' claim on an error path deserves adversarial pricing, not exemption from it.

Growing from it

Exception hierarchies are asymmetric: a base-class catch subsumes its children, but a subclass catch excludes its parent — so every narrow except clause embeds a claim about what the guarded call can raise, and the claim rots as code changes. Audit yours: list the raise surface of the call inside the try and confirm the catch is at least as wide; where a 'cannot happen' comment excuses a path from coverage, read it as an invitation to adversarial review, not a settled fact. Then fix in layers: restore the contract structurally so nothing escapes, kill the bad input at the earliest frame so the overflow becomes unreachable from the wire, and add an outermost catch-all that turns 'one bad connection costs at most that connection' into an invariant rather than an inventory of today's raisers. Last, prove the locks fire — check the broken code back out and watch the reproducer escape.

Act IILesson 118unit 31 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When an exception class subclasses a builtin, every `except` of the subclass is a claim that the base class cannot reach that spot

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)OECD AI Principles — robustness, security and safety

Adversarial review after a green merge is NIST AI RMF MEASURE work — TEVV includes evaluating what the test suite itself failed to ask, and here it priced a 'cannot happen' claim and found a one-frame denial of service. The layered fix — structural containment, earliest-frame rejection, an invariant catch-all that logs only the exception class so no page content leaks — serves the 'secure and resilient' trustworthy characteristic and the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety, exemplifying Domain III.A's governance of how the system is built.

AIGP takeaway A subclass-only catch claims the base class cannot occur; price every 'cannot happen' adversarially — the catch must cover the raise surface.
Act IILesson 119unit 32 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A passing test suite proves the code does what the tests ask, never that the tests ask the right question

The failure

The Stage C parse channel had a guest that listened and a host that could not reach it — and every test was green. The guest parser was up on the Alpine VM, bound to a plaintext AF_VSOCK listener (the Linux VM-side socket family) on port 50001. The host smoke harness got connection refused: it was knocking at 127.0.0.1:50001 — the loopback address, the host's way of talking to itself — where nothing listened. The conflation had sat in shared/ipc/vsock.py since the transport was first written. It could express three postures — dev (loopback, no mTLS, the mutual certificate authentication), host-mode (loopback plus mTLS), guest-mode (AF_HYPERV, the host-to-VM socket family, plus mTLS) — so the only way to get 'no mTLS' was dev_mode=True, which also forced loopback. Two independent properties, one switch; no fourth posture for AF_HYPERV without encryption. Three call sites used dev_mode = not has_mtls, quietly routing a guest-bound, certificate-less connection to the host's doorstep.

Evidence: 2026-06-11 — The door the loopback tests never knocked on
Before you turn the page

Every test is green and the live connection is refused — what dimension of behavior could the suite not even express, and what welded that dimension shut?

Act IILesson 119unit 32 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A passing test suite proves the code does what the tests ask, never that the tests ask the right question

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
3→4transport postures the API can name — the missing fourth was the untestable blind spot15new mocked-socket tests pinning family, protocol, and SSL selection across all four modes
Act IILesson 119unit 32 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A passing test suite proves the code does what the tests ask, never that the tests ask the right question

The lesson

The loopback tests were green because they exercised the loopback path; the boundary hop had no test because the API had no way to name it. When two independent properties — transport family and encryption on or off — are welded to one switch, the suite can be exhaustive over that switch and still carry a blind spot the size of the missing fourth combination. Build the explicit opt-in before you need it, so the thing you must test has a name.

Growing from it

A test suite explores the states its API can express; a configuration the API cannot name is one no test can construct, so coverage silently stops at the design's own vocabulary. That is why the live round-trip was not redundant with a fully green suite — it was the only probe that could fail in the dimension the unit tests could not address. When you find two independent properties welded to one switch, decouple them and enumerate the full cross-product: four combinations here, of which the suite had been exhaustive over three. Make the dangerous one an explicit, default-off opt-in that a stronger setting always overrides — certificate material present means the plaintext flag is inert — and make it log loudly, because a plaintext boundary crossing should never be silent. Then put the whole truth table behind one predicate, so it is one place to read and one place to test.

Act IILesson 119unit 32 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A passing test suite proves the code does what the tests ask, never that the tests ask the right question

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV: verification vs validation)NIST AI RMF — MANAGE

NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function houses TEVV — test, evaluation, verification, and validation — and this is its core distinction lived: a green suite verified the code against the questions the tests could ask, while only the live boundary round-trip could validate the real job in the real context. Shipping the bring-up with mTLS as an explicit, tracked residual — flagged, default-off, loudly logged — is MANAGE's residual-risk communication. Both exemplify Domain III.A, governing how the system and its test vocabulary are designed.

AIGP takeaway Coverage stops at the API's vocabulary: name every meaningful configuration explicitly, or the suite is exhaustive over the wrong space.
Act III of 8 · 14 lessons · lessons 4–122 (thematic, not contiguous) · 2 on the core path

Scope and restraint — build the smallest true thing

Constraints, rules and right-sizing as first-class engineering inputs

Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceDomain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development

This act trains right-sizing as a governance skill: building the smallest capability that genuinely runs, treating written constraints as outranking individual task instructions, and matching every fix, dependency, and feature envelope to its actual purpose and proportionate risk. Its center of gravity is Domain I.C — establishing policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle — exercised through NIST AI RMF's MAP function (establish a system's context, purpose, and capabilities before building on them) and MANAGE function (respond to risks in proportion, with the treatment on the record). Several lessons reach into Domain III, where the same restraint governs design and build, and one into Domain IV, naming capability limits before deployment decisions build on them.

Act IIILesson 4unit 33 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Build the smallest thing that genuinely runs, then grow.

The failure

The project began as a 'factory' — a fleet of AI agents meant to wake on a schedule and build BlarAI autonomously. Months of time and real money went in, and what came out was process: design documents, reports, audits of the reports. The honest count was the turning point: of around 259 automated sessions that committed anything, only 15 committed real code; the other 244 committed only markdown (plain-text documentation files). The grand plan had collapsed under its own weight while feeling like work. The replacement approach shipped the same day: in a single session, four small fixes — in-conversation memory wired in (commit aacb127), cross-restart persistence (b751147), a rigid answer template removed (9baa2d8), leaked internal reasoning hidden (13c235e) — each verified live on the real system before commit, together turning rough chat into something that genuinely works.

Evidence: 2026-05-21 — The pivot, and the first real shipping · commits aacb127, b751147, 9baa2d8, 13c235e
Before you turn the page

Of roughly 259 automated sessions that committed something, what made only 15 of them count as progress — and what were the other 244 producing instead?

Act IIILesson 4unit 33 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Build the smallest thing that genuinely runs, then grow.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
259automated sessions committed something15committed real code244committed only markdown
Act IIILesson 4unit 33 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Build the smallest thing that genuinely runs, then grow.

The lesson

Every grand plan collapsed under its own weight. Small, verified increments — one real capability at a time, each proven running on the real system before the next begins — are what compounded into a working product. Scope restraint is not a limit on ambition; it is the mechanism by which ambition accumulates.

Growing from it

Why does the grand plan fail? Because feedback is the scarcest resource in any build, and a plan's size sets how long you go without it. A small increment returns a verdict — runs or doesn't — within hours; a sweeping architecture defers every verdict for months, so errors compound invisibly and activity substitutes for evidence. The 'factory' felt productive precisely because producing documents has no failure signal. You can recognize the pattern early: ask what the smallest unit of your plan could be demonstrated running, and how soon. If the honest answer is 'nothing for weeks,' re-cut the plan. In any future project, measure progress in verified capabilities — things shown working — never in artifacts produced about the work; the ratio between those two counts is the health metric.

Act IIILesson 4unit 33 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Build the smallest thing that genuinely runs, then grow.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceISO/IEC 42001 — AIMS plan-do-check-act

The pivot exemplifies risk-proportionate development under Domain I.C — establishing policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle. The procedure that replaced the factory (small increment, review, live verification, commit only on survival) is a life-cycle control: it bounds how much unverified work can exist at any moment. It is also the plan-do-check-act pattern ISO/IEC 42001 builds its AI management system on — short cycles of doing and checking with continual improvement, rather than one long unchecked plan.

AIGP takeaway Govern the life cycle in small verified cycles — plan-do-check-act — so unverified work never outgrows your evidence.
Act IIILesson 5core pathunit 34 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Read the rules, not just the task.

The failure

While making conversations survive restarts during the pivot session, the task as written instructed building a second database to hold the history. The project's own governance rules said the opposite: only one component may own the session store (the single home of saved conversations). The instruction and the constraint were in direct conflict, and the task was simply wrong — following it faithfully would have shipped a second, competing source of truth for session data, the exact architecture the rule existed to prevent. The conflict was caught before any code was built, and the feature was done the correct way instead: conversation history is replayed to the assistant over the channel that already exists, with the single session store untouched as owner (commit b751147). The capability shipped the same day, and the governance boundary held.

Evidence: 2026-05-21 — The pivot, and the first real shipping · commit b751147
Before you turn the page

When a written task and the project's standing rules directly contradict each other, which one is 'doing your job' — and who should decide?

Act IIILesson 5core pathunit 34 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Read the rules, not just the task.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act IIILesson 5core pathunit 34 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Read the rules, not just the task.

The lesson

A task once instructed building a second database; the project's own governance rules forbade exactly that. The task was wrong. Doing what you are told is not the same as doing the right thing — the constraints deserve as much attention as the request, and when they conflict, the constraint outranks the instruction until someone with authority says otherwise.

Growing from it

The mechanism underneath: instructions are written by someone holding a local picture — one feature, one deadline — while constraints encode the global picture, the accumulated decisions about what must stay true everywhere. A task author can forget a rule; the rule does not stop applying because it was forgotten. If you execute instructions without loading the constraints first, you become the laundering step that converts one person's oversight into the system's architecture. The habit to build: before starting any task, read the standing rules of the system you are touching — its decision records, its stated invariants — and actively check the task against them. When they collide, stop and surface the conflict rather than silently obeying either side. In delegated and AI-agent work this matters double: an agent that obeys tasks but not policy scales the mistake.

Act IIILesson 5core pathunit 34 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Read the rules, not just the task.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceNIST AI RMF — GOVERN

This is Domain I.C made concrete: policies and procedures established throughout the AI life cycle exist precisely to outrank any single instruction. NIST AI RMF's GOVERN function — the cross-cutting one — calls for policies and accountability structures that shape every downstream activity; a developer (or an AI agent) checking each task against standing policy, and escalating conflicts rather than obeying the louder voice, is that function operating at the level of a single commit.

AIGP takeaway Policy outranks instruction: GOVERN means every task is checked against standing rules, and conflicts escalate instead of executing.
Act IIILesson 12unit 35 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Build the surface to the shape of the capability you are about to add, and the capability becomes a backend swap instead of a UI rewrite.

The failure

Photos were where the assistant was heading, but it could not see — and the tempting plan was to build nothing multimodal until a vision model existed. Instead, Phase 4 of the windowed-app work built the entire experience against a blind backend: a paperclip picker, drag-and-drop, a screenshot button that genuinely captures, inline chips with image thumbnails — every path ending at the same store-only seam placed in Phase 1, where the file is saved and the model receives only a sentence saying an image is present and unreadable (Python suite untouched at 1,364 passed, 2 skipped). On 2026-06-03 the bet paid off in cash: wiring real image understanding (Qwen3-VL-8B, a vision-language model, loaded on the Arc 140V GPU in thirteen seconds) was a backend swap — one function in the document loader, where the placeholder text was. Nothing in the window changed.

Evidence: 2026-06-02 — Phase 4: attachments you can see, and a model that still can't · 2026-06-03 — Vision, in one swap: the envelope I built in May paid for itself
Before you turn the page

When a vision model finally landed, why did nothing in the window need to change — and which decisions had already been made months earlier, while the feature was still inert?

Act IIILesson 12unit 35 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Build the surface to the shape of the capability you are about to add, and the capability becomes a backend swap instead of a UI rewrite.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
one functionthe whole backend swapthirteen secondsQwen3-VL-8B load on Arc 140V
Act IIILesson 12unit 35 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Build the surface to the shape of the capability you are about to add, and the capability becomes a backend swap instead of a UI rewrite.

The lesson

A capability's envelope — where it plugs in, what it is allowed to touch, how the user invokes it — can and should be built and reasoned about before the capability itself exists. Build the surface to the shape of the capability you are about to add, and the capability becomes a backend swap: turning it on later is a single, auditable change rather than a scramble.

Growing from it

The mechanism: a feature's risk questions — what it may read, where its output goes, how a user invokes and revokes it — are decided at the envelope, not inside the model. If you build the envelope only when the capability arrives, you decide its blast radius under launch pressure, when every answer that slows shipping loses. Building it while the capability is inert separates the two problems: the contract gets debated when changing it costs nothing, and activation later becomes one small, reviewable change. You can spot the opportunity in any project by asking: what capability is clearly coming, and what would its seam look like if it landed tomorrow? Build that seam now, with an honest placeholder behind it. The screenshot button is the sharp version: the privacy question — this app can grab your screen — was asked and answered while nothing could yet interpret pixels.

Act IIILesson 12unit 35 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Build the surface to the shape of the capability you are about to add, and the capability becomes a backend swap instead of a UI rewrite.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MAP

This exemplifies NIST AI RMF's MAP function: establishing a system's context — its purpose, capabilities, and the parties its features touch — before and independently of building the capability itself. Mapping the envelope while the capability is inert means its boundaries are set in advance, so activation under Domain III.A (governing the designing and building of the AI model and system) is one deliberate, auditable change rather than a bundle of unexamined decisions shipped with a launch.

AIGP takeaway MAP the capability's envelope — scope, touchpoints, invocation — before the capability exists; activation then becomes one auditable change.
Act IIILesson 15unit 36 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Routing around a broken tool beats fixing it, when the tool is not on your path.

The failure

Probing the voice plan on the real hardware flushed out a hazard the handoff brief never mentioned: optimum-cli export openvino — the tool that converts a Whisper speech model for the runtime — was broken in the project's Python environment, a three-way version mismatch between transformers 5.3.0, optimum 2.1.0.dev0, and the editable optimum-intel checkout kept for upstream work. The reflex, the operator's first instinct too, was to fix it: a broken tool in the tree feels like a hazard you must not build over. The discipline was to ask whether voice actually stood on it — and it did not: the runtime never imports optimum, and a pre-converted Whisper model could simply be downloaded, more reproducible than a local conversion anyway. Fixing it meant re-pinning transformers, which the working 14B inference path and the Substrate embedder both ride. The breakage was filed as tracked hardening ticket #541 instead.

Evidence: 2026-06-02 — Voice, the backend half: measuring the terrain before laying track
Before you turn the page

A conversion tool in the tree is provably broken. Before fixing it, what one question decides whether the fix belongs on this feature's critical path at all?

Act IIILesson 15unit 36 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Routing around a broken tool beats fixing it, when the tool is not on your path.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act IIILesson 15unit 36 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Routing around a broken tool beats fixing it, when the tool is not on your path.

The lesson

'It's broken' is not the same as 'it's in my way.' When a broken tool is not on your path, the mature move is blast-radius judgment: inventory the hazard, prove your path avoids it, and schedule the repair for when it can be done with the right regression net — not yak-shave a dependency tree on the critical path of an unrelated feature.

Growing from it

The mechanism is a risk transfer you don't notice making. Fixing a broken shared dependency mid-feature swaps a known, contained hazard (a tool you do not use is broken, and it is written down) for an unknown, wide one (re-pinning a library that working systems depend on, with no test plan, under feature deadline). The instinct to fix comes from treating 'broken' as a moral state instead of a risk to be priced. Train the replacement question: am I actually building over this? Trace the dependency — does my path import it, invoke it, trust its output? If not, the cheap and honest controls are a written hazard inventory and a tracked ticket with the repair scheduled for when its blast radius can be tested. Guard against the opposite failure too: routing around without recording, which converts a known hazard into a forgotten one.

Act IIILesson 15unit 36 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Routing around a broken tool beats fixing it, when the tool is not on your path.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceNIST AI RMF — MANAGE

This is NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function in miniature: risks prioritized and responded to in proportion — a treatment plan (the tracked ticket), a response decision (route around, using a more reproducible pre-converted artifact), and residual-risk visibility rather than silent deferral. Under Domain I.C, the procedure matters as much as the call: the hazard was documented and scheduled, not ignored, so the life-cycle record shows a deliberate, reviewable risk decision instead of an invisible one.

AIGP takeaway MANAGE means proportionate response: prove your path avoids the hazard, document it, schedule the repair — don't fix off-path tools under deadline.
Act IIILesson 19core pathunit 37 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Sell the capability you have, not the one they hope for — name the limits before they build on them.

The failure

Vision (Qwen3-VL) was being wired for the operator's sister's landscape business, and the goals included precise measurements from photos and generating proposal renderings. A vision-language model does neither: it understands images — describe, recognize, answer, draft text — but it guesses at dimensions, and false precision is worse than none for a bid; and it cannot generate images at all, which is a different kind of model's job (a diffusion model). The valuable move was not to quietly wire what could be wired and let the gaps surface mid-sales-call, but to say plainly, before a line of code: measurement is photogrammetry, a separate pipeline that needs the photo capture itself to change; rendering is image generation, a separate model; both honest future tracks. The description prompt explicitly forbids the model from giving measurements. The operator took the limits well, and vision was scoped to what it genuinely delivers now: understanding.

Evidence: 2026-06-03 — Vision, in one swap: the envelope I built in May paid for itself
Before you turn the page

The user's plan assumed the new vision feature could measure a yard from a photo. When is the cheapest possible moment for him to learn it cannot — and whose job is it to make that moment happen?

Act IIILesson 19core pathunit 37 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Sell the capability you have, not the one they hope for — name the limits before they build on them.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act IIILesson 19core pathunit 37 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Sell the capability you have, not the one they hope for — name the limits before they build on them.

The lesson

Capability communication is a governance act. A user building a business on an assistant needs the limits named as clearly as the features, before they plan around them — an over-promised capability discovered in production costs far more than a limit stated in conversation. Sell the capability you have, not the one they hope for.

Growing from it

The mechanism: hope fills any gap your silence leaves. A user with a goal will read an undescribed capability as whatever their plan needs it to be, then commit money and reputation to that reading — and the gap surfaces at the worst possible moment, in front of their customer, as your system 'failing.' The asymmetry is the thing to internalize: a limit stated up front costs one slightly disappointing conversation; the same limit discovered in production costs trust, rework, and possibly a bad bid someone signed. Build the habit of capability triage before wiring anything: for each goal the user names, classify it as delivered-now, different-technology, or not-possible — and say which, in their language, with the honest path for each. Engineering the false hope out of the plan is part of shipping the feature.

Act IIILesson 19core pathunit 37 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Sell the capability you have, not the one they hope for — name the limits before they build on them.

The AIGP connection
Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and UseOECD AI Principles — transparency and explainabilityNIST AI RMF — MAP

This exemplifies the OECD AI Principle of transparency and explainability — disclosing what an AI system can and cannot do to the people relying on it. It is also MAP work in NIST AI RMF terms: the system's capabilities and limits, and the parties affected by them, are established as context before plans get built on top of them. Under Domain IV.A, naming limits before someone builds a business process on the system is part of evaluating the factors relevant to the decision to deploy.

AIGP takeaway Name capabilities and limits before users plan around them — transparency up front beats an over-promise discovered in production.
Act IIILesson 20unit 38 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

The privilege-lowering fix and the capability fix can be the same change.

The failure

File attach was broken — OneDrive cloud-only files unselectable, drag-and-drop showing the 'not allowed' cursor — and the obvious-looking fix, broadening the dialog's file-type filters, had already shipped (commit 855d857) and changed nothing, because formats were never the cause. The tell was which inputs failed: a local copy of a photo attached fine; the same photo as a cloud-only file failed through the same dialog. Everything that broke crossed a trust boundary — the shell's per-user cloud-hydration service, the integrity check Windows runs between programs of different trust levels — because the window had inherited the launcher's High integrity by accident; its own manifest asked for nothing. The fix kept the launcher elevated (Hyper-V needs it) and spawned the UI at a filtered Medium token — the Administrators group set deny-only, privileges stripped, integrity stamped down. That one change restored attach and shrank the attack surface (1442 tests passed; ADR-019).

Evidence: 2026-06-03 — The privilege boundary, a fourth time: this time I moved the wall · commit 855d857 (the filter fix that changed nothing)
Before you turn the page

A local copy of a photo attaches fine; the identical photo, as a cloud-only file, fails through the very same dialog. What does the difference between those two inputs tell you about where the bug lives?

Act IIILesson 20unit 38 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

The privilege-lowering fix and the capability fix can be the same change.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
High→Mediumthe window's integrity level
Act IIILesson 20unit 38 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

The privilege-lowering fix and the capability fix can be the same change.

The lesson

Least privilege is usually filed as a security tax paid on top of features; here it was the feature. When a capability breaks only for the inputs that cross a trust boundary, suspect the privilege level before the feature code — reach for the token, not the dialog. The privilege-lowering fix and the capability fix can be the same change.

Growing from it

The mechanism: excess privilege isolates.
