An over-privileged process sits on the wrong side of every boundary the platform enforces, so ordinary integrations — drag-and-drop, file dialogs, per-user services — quietly refuse to talk to it; the symptom presents as feature bugs scattered across the surface, and you debug each one at the feature layer while the single cause sits in the process's privilege level. The diagnostic to learn is differential: when the same code path works for one input and fails for another, ask what boundary the failing input crosses that the working one does not. If the answer is a trust or integrity boundary, privilege is your suspect. And carry the wider cost model: elevating a process for one capability silently taxes every unrelated integration it touches — which is itself the practical argument for least privilege, long before an attacker shows up.

Act IIILesson 20unit 38 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

The privilege-lowering fix and the capability fix can be the same change.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — secure and resilient (trustworthy-AI characteristic)OECD AI Principles — robustness, security and safety

Least privilege here exemplifies NIST AI RMF's secure-and-resilient trustworthy-AI characteristic and the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety — and it dissolves the assumed trade-off: hardening and capability moved together, in one change. Under Domain III.A, governing the designing and building of the system includes setting each component's privilege to the minimum that works; the failure shows the cost of letting privilege be inherited by accident rather than decided on purpose.

AIGP takeaway When a feature fails only across trust boundaries, fix the privilege, not the feature — least privilege can be the capability fix.
Act IIILesson 26unit 39 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

On-demand and context-aware beats eager and generic — and a subordinate model's answer is data, not instruction.

The failure

The first vision wiring described every photo the instant it was attached, with a fixed 256-token landscaping prompt, before the user had said a word — answering a question nobody asked, uselessly when the image is a rash, a screenshot, a whiteboard. The redesign holds the image on attach; when the user actually asks, the 14B reasoning model (the 'brain') formulates what the vision model (the 'eyes') should look for from the conversation, and the answer folds back as datamarked data — marked as untrusted content, never instruction, because a vision model can read an injection off a sign in an attacker's photo. A bare 'what's this?' skips the formulation pass. The hard distributed version almost got built: the gateway-to-Orchestrator channel is strictly one-way with 64 KB frames, against images up to 64 MB — co-locating the orchestration where both models already live made the sequence a local function call.

Evidence: 2026-06-04 — The brain directs the eyes: vision tasked on demand, in context, as data
Before you turn the page

The first wiring described every photo instantly, with a fixed landscaping prompt. What two different things were wrong with doing that work before the user asked anything?

Act IIILesson 26unit 39 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

On-demand and context-aware beats eager and generic — and a subordinate model's answer is data, not instruction.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
256-tokenfixed prompt, fired on every attach64 KBthe one-way frame cap64 MBan image, never crossing the wire
Act IIILesson 26unit 39 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

On-demand and context-aware beats eager and generic — and a subordinate model's answer is data, not instruction.

The lesson

Run an expensive capability when a real question exists, shaped by that question — not eagerly with a generic preset. Let the reasoning model formulate what the perception model should look for, skip the pass when there is nothing to enrich, and ground the subordinate model's answer as datamarked data, never trusted as instruction. Put cross-model orchestration where the models already live.

Growing from it

Two mechanisms underneath. Eager-and-generic fails because work done before the question exists must guess the question — and a guess encoded as a fixed prompt bakes one domain's assumptions into every input, spending compute to produce irrelevance. On-demand inverts it: context arrives first, so the work can be shaped by it, and skipped when it adds nothing. The security half generalizes further: any output from a subordinate component — a model, a tool, a parser — is input shaped by whatever that component read, so trusting it as instruction builds a laundering path for injections. In your own systems, ask of every automatic step: what question is this answering, and who asked it? And of every component boundary: does anything downstream obey what this component says, rather than read it? Both questions surface expensive, dangerous defaults early.

Act IIILesson 26unit 39 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

On-demand and context-aware beats eager and generic — and a subordinate model's answer is data, not instruction.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MAPNIST AI RMF — secure and resilient (trustworthy-AI characteristic)

The redesign is MAP discipline applied inside a single feature: the system's actual context of use — what the user is asking, what domain the image belongs to — is established before capability is spent, instead of a generic assumption hard-coded in advance. The datamarking rule expresses NIST's secure-and-resilient trustworthy-AI characteristic: a subordinate model's output is treated as untrusted data so perception can never become a command channel. Both sit under Domain III.A, governing how the system is designed and built.

AIGP takeaway Spend capability only on real questions, in their real context — and treat a subordinate model's answer as data, never instruction.
Act IIILesson 28unit 40 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

The safest feature flag is the fail-soft path you already built — disable-by-config and degrade-by-failure should be one code path.

The failure

The voice models — Whisper for speech-in, Kokoro with its whole 54-voice bank for speech-out — preloaded at boot, but the operator was not using voice yet, and on a machine whose integrated GPU shares one 32 GB memory pool with everything else, the cheapest memory is the model you never load. The tempting fix was the elegant one: a new lazy-load-on-demand subsystem — the better end state, but new code with a UI contract that could not be live-tested headless, trading a memory risk for a capability risk. The voice engine was already fail-soft, though: an absent model disables that half cleanly — speech-to-text and text-to-speech report unavailable, the UI hides the affordances, and speak is gated off so nothing is auto-spoken — a path exercised every time the weights were simply missing. The off-switch became a single constant, VOICE_ENABLED (default False), routing into that proven degraded state.

Evidence: 2026-06-04 — The cheapest memory is the model you never load
Before you turn the page

You must switch a capability off safely. The system already has a tested behaviour for when that capability's model is simply missing. What does building a brand-new disable mechanism add — and what does it risk?

Act IIILesson 28unit 40 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

The safest feature flag is the fail-soft path you already built — disable-by-config and degrade-by-failure should be one code path.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
54-voiceKokoro bank preloading, unused32 GBone shared pool, no dedicated VRAM
Act IIILesson 28unit 40 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

The safest feature flag is the fail-soft path you already built — disable-by-config and degrade-by-failure should be one code path.

The lesson

When you need to turn a capability off — for memory, for risk, for a user who is not using it yet — look first for the degradation path the system already handles. Disabling by config and degrading by failure are the same behaviour, so reuse the proven path: you are not introducing a new state, you are deliberately entering a tested one — the lowest-risk shape there is.

Growing from it

The mechanism: risk lives in untested states, and a new disable path is a new state — new code, new UI contract, new failure modes — added precisely when your goal was to reduce exposure. A degradation path the system already survives, by contrast, has been exercised every time the dependency was genuinely absent; entering it deliberately adds zero new states. Before building any off-switch, ask: how does this system already behave when the thing is missing — and is that behaviour acceptable as the off state? If yes, the flag is a one-line gate into proven behaviour, and the more elegant mechanism (lazy-load, dynamic provisioning) waits until it is wanted on its own merits. One transferable detail: put the flag in a channel that survives your process boundaries — here a code constant, because an environment variable dies crossing the Windows elevation relaunch.

Act IIILesson 28unit 40 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

The safest feature flag is the fail-soft path you already built — disable-by-config and degrade-by-failure should be one code path.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceNIST AI RMF — MANAGE

This exemplifies NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function: a risk response (reclaim memory by disabling an unused capability) implemented through the path of least residual risk — a proven degraded state rather than new machinery. It also expresses risk-proportionate procedure under Domain I.C: the policies-and-procedures view asks not only whether a control exists but whether the change introducing it is itself low-risk; the rejected alternative, a more capable lazy-load subsystem, stays on the record as the path not taken.

AIGP takeaway The lowest-risk off-switch routes into the degraded state the system already survives — config-off and failure-off as one path.
Act IIILesson 31unit 41 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Fix the symptom with a bound; fix the cause when the bound lets you.

The failure

A user attached an 8.3 MP photo and asked about it. Under memory pressure the Orchestrator generated and validated the answer — the policy gate approved it — but GENERATION_COMPLETE, the message telling the gateway the answer was finished, never arrived; the front end kept waiting for a closing frame to re-enable its text input, and six minutes later the user killed the process. The cause — vision-model memory exhaustion — involves hard quality-versus-memory trade-offs only the operator can decide, tracked at Vikunja #565. The symptom — an infinite freeze regardless of root cause — was fully fixable immediately: the entire streaming arc was wrapped in a 90-second deadline (_PROMPT_STREAM_FAILSAFE_S), guaranteeing a terminal frame and a re-enabled input whatever the gateway does. The regression test was written first and watched fail against the unfixed code — the primary assertion timing out in 3.15 s exactly as the bug predicted.

Evidence: 2026-06-04 — The terminal frame the user never saw
Before you turn the page

The freeze had a root cause requiring a quality trade-off only the operator could decide. What could be fixed immediately without deciding anything for him — and why are those two fixes different in kind?

Act IIILesson 31unit 41 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Fix the symptom with a bound; fix the cause when the bound lets you.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
6 minutes→90 sworst case to a terminal frame3.15 swatched-fail test, as predicted
Act IIILesson 31unit 41 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Fix the symptom with a bound; fix the cause when the bound lets you.

The lesson

When a failure has a symptom the system can absorb and a cause that requires a human call, they are separate decisions with separate owners. Bound the symptom now — a deadline, a fallback, a cap — and expose the cause cleanly, so the operator can decide on the cause from a system that no longer freezes rather than from one that does.

Growing from it

The mechanism is a conflation of two decisions with different owners and different clocks. Containing a symptom is pure engineering — one correct shape, no trade-off, deployable today. Fixing the cause may change what the product can do — image quality, capability, cost — which belongs to whoever owns the product. Fuse them and you get one of two failures: the urgent fix waits on a slow human decision (users keep freezing), or the human decision gets made implicitly by an engineer under pressure (a capability quietly degraded without consent). The pattern to carry: for any serious failure, ask what bound the system itself can guarantee — time, memory, retries — independent of why things went wrong; ship that bound; then route the root cause to its proper owner with the time pressure removed. The bound also keeps the system honest while diagnosis continues.

Act IIILesson 31unit 41 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Fix the symptom with a bound; fix the cause when the bound lets you.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MANAGEEU AI Act — human oversight (high-risk obligation concept)

This is NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function with the seams showing: response implementation (the bounded deadline) separated from treatment planning on the underlying risk, with the residual risk tracked and communicated rather than silently absorbed. Routing the cause to the operator exemplifies the EU AI Act's human-oversight concept: decisions that change system quality or capability stay with an accountable human, while engineering owns containment. Domain III.C frames it as governing the monitoring and maintenance of the running system.

AIGP takeaway Bound the symptom now; route the cause to its accountable owner — containment and capability decisions have different owners.
Act IIILesson 40unit 42 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Reuse the commodity, harvest the method, refuse the framework — a network-touching dependency is a supply-chain liability you import on purpose.

The failure

Building the web-search skill — the first capability that would ever cross the network boundary — three mature open projects (local-deep-research, gpt-researcher, open_deep_research) already implemented the exact query, search, read, synthesize loop needed, and the convenient move was to adopt one wholesale. Each carries a large orchestration framework (LangGraph), cloud-LLM assumptions, and a multi-engine scraper stack: a wide, network-touching dependency surface to import into a system whose security model is its air-gap. The discipline that paid: take the commodity — the thin, hash-pinned Kagi client, five canonical packages, a 3.7 KB wheel — and the method, the algorithmic shape studied not copied, and hand-build the gated-local-native part none of them have. The whole skill was built behind glass: every network call mocked, the runtime egress guard (the control blocking outbound connections) armed, not one live call; whether BlarAI ever faces the internet remains a separate, deliberate decision.

Evidence: 2026-06-04 — The eyes you rent, the brain you own: web-search, designed behind glass
Before you turn the page

Three open projects already implemented the exact loop the skill needed. What would adopting one wholesale have imported, beyond the loop?

Act IIILesson 40unit 42 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Reuse the commodity, harvest the method, refuse the framework — a network-touching dependency is a supply-chain liability you import on purpose.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
3.7 KBthe hash-pinned Kagi client wheelfive canonical packagesthe entire commodity surface taken
Act IIILesson 40unit 42 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Reuse the commodity, harvest the method, refuse the framework — a network-touching dependency is a supply-chain liability you import on purpose.

The lesson

In a security-critical system, build-vs-adopt is a supply-chain decision before it is a convenience one. Every dependency is attack surface you chose to take on, and 'someone already wrote it' is the weakest reason to import a framework into a system whose core invariant it would widen. Reuse the commodity, harvest the method, refuse the framework.

Growing from it

The mechanism: a dependency is not one decision but a standing delegation — its transitive packages, its update cadence, its maintainers' security posture all join your trusted base, indefinitely, in exchange for writing time saved once. Frameworks are the worst ratio: they bring the most surface and entangle deepest, so removing one later is a rewrite. The decomposition to practice on every 'should we adopt X?' call: which part is commodity (small, stable, pinnable — take it), which part is method (an algorithmic shape you can study in the source and implement natively — harvest it), and which part is framework (broad machinery whose assumptions become yours — refuse it unless those assumptions genuinely match your system). Then price every candidate dependency against the specific invariant your system cannot afford to lose; here that invariant was the air-gap itself.

Act IIILesson 40unit 42 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Reuse the commodity, harvest the method, refuse the framework — a network-touching dependency is a supply-chain liability you import on purpose.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MAPNIST AI RMF — secure and resilient (trustworthy-AI characteristic)

This exemplifies MAP-function risk identification in NIST AI RMF terms — the system's context included an invariant (no external network) against which every candidate dependency was categorized before adoption — and the secure-and-resilient trustworthy-AI characteristic, treating third-party software surface as security surface. Under Domain III.A, governing the designing and building of the system includes governing what enters it: the build-vs-adopt record, with the rejected frameworks named, is design-layer evidence the decision was deliberate.

AIGP takeaway Build-vs-adopt is a supply-chain risk decision: take the pinned commodity, learn the method, refuse the framework's surface.
Act IIILesson 43unit 43 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A component's primary purpose is fixed by its use case, not by the sprint you happen to be viewing it through.

The failure

Walking the air-gap-removal decisions one at a time with the operator — eight were ratified in the end — the Cleaner (USE-CASE-003) came up, and it was posed as a security question: how aggressive should the sanitizer be? The operator stopped the walk: the scrubber's first job was never security. Re-reading the use-case text proved him right — UC-003 is data-normalization first, stripping ads, boilerplate, and markup so the Substrate (the knowledge store) is not poisoned with 'inference pollution,' with injection sanitization only its second layer, backstopped by other defences so it need not be paranoid. The security sprint's lens had silently redefined the component's purpose, and a decision was nearly locked around the wrong frame — sizing a data-quality component as if hostility were its reason to exist (commits 3fbc4d7, a075932).

Evidence: 2026-06-05 — Walking the air-gap decisions, and the map that forgot its own legend · commits 3fbc4d7, a075932 (roadmap §6 ratified)
Before you turn the page

Met during a security sprint, the Cleaner looked like a security control. What document settles what a component is actually for — and what was its first job all along?

Act IIILesson 43unit 43 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A component's primary purpose is fixed by its use case, not by the sprint you happen to be viewing it through.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act IIILesson 43unit 43 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A component's primary purpose is fixed by its use case, not by the sprint you happen to be viewing it through.

The lesson

The lens of the sprint you meet a component in will try to redefine its purpose. A component's primary purpose is fixed by its use case — the canonical statement of why it exists — not by whichever concern brought you to it this week. Re-read the use-case text before locking a decision around a component.

Growing from it

The mechanism is context contamination: whatever question brought you to a component becomes the frame you evaluate it in, and every property gets graded against that frame — a data-quality tool judged as a security control, a security control judged on performance. The error compounds when decisions lock: requirements get sized for the wrong job, and the real job is quietly under-served. Notice the warning sign in yourself: if your description of a component's purpose matches the theme of your current work suspiciously well, you are probably describing your lens, not the component. The repair is cheap and procedural — before any decision that fixes a component's behaviour, go back to its founding document (the use case, the design record, the original ticket) and let the stated purpose, not the visiting concern, define what 'good' means for it.

Act IIILesson 43unit 43 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A component's primary purpose is fixed by its use case, not by the sprint you happen to be viewing it through.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceNIST AI RMF — MAP

Intended purpose is the anchor concept: NIST AI RMF's MAP function begins by categorizing a system and establishing its purpose and context, because every later risk judgment keys off that statement — and letting a sprint's lens silently re-purpose a component corrupts the mapping. Under Domain I.C, the canonical use-case documents function as life-cycle policy artifacts: decisions get checked against them, not against the concern of the week — the constraint-outranks-task discipline of lesson 5, applied to purpose.

AIGP takeaway A component's purpose comes from its use case, not your current lens — MAP from the founding document before deciding.
Act IIILesson 102unit 44 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Reuse the project's canonical approval primitive instead of minting a second one.

The failure

BlarAI's egress kill-switch — the latch that stops anything leaving the machine — trips itself automatically on an anomaly but re-arms only by hand. The hole was authorisation: rearm() was a bare function whose docstring (its own explanatory note) all but shouted "Lead-Architect-only", yet anything running in-process could call it — a governance gap, not a control. Closing it (#653), the tempting move was a small egress-specific auth path: a confirm dialog, a password check. Instead the re-arm consumes the project's canonical approval machinery — the ApprovalVerifier protocol and ApprovalResult shape the ESCALATE (route-to-human-review) consumer (#639) defined and the Windows-Hello fingerprint verifier (#649) already implements — because re-arming is the same shape of decision: single-user, human-in-the-loop approve or deny. Only an explicit approved-is-True clears the latch; no verifier, a deny, an error, a timeout — all stay LOCKED. The reused primitive had 40 tests behind it; the re-arm added eleven, all green.

Evidence: 2026-06-10 — The latch that only a fingerprint may lift
Before you turn the page

Before building a new "ask the human" check, what does the system already have for the same shape of decision — and what would a second path cost?

Act IIILesson 102unit 44 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Reuse the project's canonical approval primitive instead of minting a second one.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
40tests already behind the reused #639/#649 approval primitive11new re-arm tests: every deny, error, timeout and no-verifier path stays LOCKED
Act IIILesson 102unit 44 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Reuse the project's canonical approval primitive instead of minting a second one.

The lesson

When a new control needs "a human must approve this", reuse the approval primitive the project has already audited rather than minting a second one. A second trust surface is a second thing to get wrong and a second thing to drift out of step with the first. One primitive, reused fail-closed — only an explicit approval clears, absence and error stay locked — concentrates scrutiny where it compounds.

Growing from it

The pull toward a bespoke path is real: wiring a small confirm dialog feels faster than learning someone else's contract, and the new feature feels like it deserves its own switch. The cost is structural, not stylistic. Every approval path is a trust surface — a place where an attacker, a bug, or drift can turn "human must approve" into "nothing checked" — and surfaces multiply maintenance: hardening and review applied to one path quietly miss the other, while the bespoke one starts life with zero tests. So before you build any ask-the-human mechanism, inventory what already exists for the same shape of decision: who approves, at what granularity, with what failure posture. Same shape — take the hard dependency on the canonical primitive, and treat the coupling as a feature: scrutiny concentrates. Genuinely different shape — write down what differs before you build, because that difference is the design.

Act IIILesson 102unit 44 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Reuse the project's canonical approval primitive instead of minting a second one.

The AIGP connection
Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and UseEU AI Act — human oversight (high-risk obligation, concept level)NIST AI RMF — MANAGE

A kill-switch that trips on anomaly and re-arms only on a verified human decision is human oversight in the EU AI Act's sense — one of the high-risk obligations, at concept level — implemented as a control, not a policy sentence. The trip-and-recover cycle is the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's MANAGE function: responding to a detected risk and governing the return to normal operation. AIGP Domain IV.C — govern the deployment and use of the model and system — is where an operational control like this lives; one audited primitive keeps the oversight verifiable.

AIGP takeaway Human-oversight controls earn trust through one audited approval path — reused, fail-closed — not a new trust surface per feature.
Act IIILesson 120unit 45 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When a transport's bootstrap dependency lives on the far side of the transport itself, stop trying to automate the first hop

The failure

Stage C of the Cleaner program needed reproducible provisioning for the Hyper-V guest — the isolated virtual machine that will parse hostile HTML. The archaeology was the finding: asked how the earlier #615 echo responder ever reached the Alpine guest, the evidence answered it mostly didn't. The vsock round-trip (the host-to-VM socket channel) PASSED on 2026-02-23 — but the one recorded deploy attempt over Copy-VMFile, Hyper-V's host-to-guest file copy, FAILED (P5_GUEST_CHANNEL_NOT_READY, 2026-02-25), and the deploy evidence schema quietly carried the confession: "invocation_mode": "manual-in-guest". There is no host-to-guest execution channel for a Linux guest: PowerShell Direct is Windows-guest-only, and the guest has no network card by ratified posture. Copy-VMFile needs the hv_fcopy_daemon running inside the guest — the daemon that serves the deploy channel is the thing missing. The channel cannot ship itself, and for three months 'manual-in-guest' had passed as a temporary embarrassment.

Evidence: 2026-06-11 — Designing around a channel that cannot ship itself
Before you turn the page

The one step you cannot automate keeps getting labelled 'temporary' — what does that label cost over months, and what changes the day you design the step instead?

Act IIILesson 120unit 45 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When a transport's bootstrap dependency lives on the far side of the transport itself, stop trying to automate the first hop

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
3console commands in the one permanent manual step — every deploy after it is automatic3 monthshow long the deploy channel sat unproven while 'manual-in-guest' passed as temporary61new tests proving the predicates and protocol before any real VM was touched
Act IIILesson 120unit 45 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When a transport's bootstrap dependency lives on the far side of the transport itself, stop trying to automate the first hop

The lesson

When a transport's bootstrap dependency lives on the far side of the transport, automating the first hop is impossible by construction. Design the smallest permanent manual step instead — three commands at the Hyper-V console enable the copy daemon — and from then on a guest-side supervisor turns the host's only verb, copy files, into the verbs the lifecycle needs: apply, restart, supervise. Naming the bootstrap as a designed step is what makes the remaining automation trustworthy.

Growing from it

Bootstrap circularity is everywhere: the credential you need before the secret store is up, the agent you must install before the fleet manager can install anything, the daemon that serves the deploy channel. The failure mode is denial — treating the unautomatable hop as a temporary embarrassment, which leaves it undocumented, unproven, and quietly load-bearing. The mature move inverts it: shrink the manual step to its minimum, make it one-time, write it down as designed, then engineer everything after it to be automatic and verifiable. The host ships the bundle, its hash, then a trigger file last as the commit point, so a partial copy is never applied; the supervisor deliberately exits after three crashes in two minutes so the host's health check fails loudly. And test the protocol before touching the machine: those tests caught an invisible Windows line-ending character that would have made the guest reject every deploy.

Act IIILesson 120unit 45 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When a transport's bootstrap dependency lives on the far side of the transport itself, stop trying to automate the first hop

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MAPISO/IEC 42001 — AIMS plan-do-check-act

Provisioning and deployment machinery is Domain III.C — governing the release, monitoring and maintenance of the model and system. Establishing what the deployment environment actually permits — no execution channel into a network-less Linux guest — is what NIST AI RMF's MAP function asks for: context before treatment. And a provisioning record that names the manual bootstrap as designed, then itemizes exactly what stays unproven until a controlled session runs it, exemplifies ISO/IEC 42001's plan-do-check-act management-system pattern: documented procedure, honest check, then act.

AIGP takeaway Design the unautomatable bootstrap as a minimal, documented, one-time manual step; automate and verify everything after it.
Act IIILesson 122unit 46 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A runtime pinned below a capability its own new feature needs is a version-bridge problem, not a migration problem

The failure

UC-003 Stage C needs the host to open an AF_HYPERV vsock — the Windows socket family for talking to a Hyper-V virtual machine — to the network-less guest where hostile web bytes get parsed. One line in the launcher hard-refused on any interpreter below Python 3.12: if not hasattr(socket, "AF_HYPERV"). The guard was right; what it guarded against was us. The BlarAI runtime venv — its isolated Python environment — is Python 3.11.9, pinned deliberately because OpenVINO GenAI and the 14-billion-parameter model weights are validated against it, and socket.AF_HYPERV first shipped in CPython 3.12. Confirmed on the real venv, not from docs: hasattr returned False. The production interpreter tripped its own guard every time — the runtime was pinned one minor version below a capability one of its own new features needs.

Evidence: 2026-06-11 — The capability my own runtime was pinned below
Before you turn the page

The guard against a missing capability is firing on your own production interpreter — why might migrating the whole stack be the wrong fix, and what is the smallest boundary that can contain the version jump?

Act IIILesson 122unit 46 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A runtime pinned below a capability its own new feature needs is a version-bridge problem, not a migration problem

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
3.11.9runtime Python, pinned for OpenVINO and the 14B weights; socket.AF_HYPERV shipped in 3.1230existing tests broken by one new required config field — caught instantly on the real 3.11 venv
Act IIILesson 122unit 46 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A runtime pinned below a capability its own new feature needs is a version-bridge problem, not a migration problem

The lesson

The obvious fix — migrate the whole stack to 3.12+ — was rejected hardest: that is re-validating OpenVINO, every pinned wheel, and the draft model that accelerates generation, to buy one socket constant. Hand-reimplementing the socket plumbing was rejected as clever-but-brittle. What shipped is a bridge: the 3.11 launcher spawns a short-lived Python 3.14 helper per operation, the version jump isolated to one process boundary, the runtime venv never touched — and the bridge self-retires the day the runtime catches up.

Growing from it

Version pins are risk decisions: a runtime validated against specific heavy dependencies will sooner or later lag a capability its newest feature needs, and you will face this fork. Weigh blast radius against frequency — re-validating an entire inference stack to serve a rare control-plane call is the wrong trade — and notice the two seductive wrong exits: the whole-stack migration, and the silent fallback to the old interpreter, which simply recreates the original bug. A bridge done well has three reusable properties: fail-closed discovery, where no qualifying interpreter means a loud refusal and never a quiet degrade; a fast path that makes the bridge self-retiring once the runtime catches up; and a deliberately tiny import surface for the helper, which doubles as a security forcing function. And run your gate on the interpreter the feature actually ships on — that is what made this mismatch visible and reproducible instead of theoretical.

Act IIILesson 122unit 46 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A runtime pinned below a capability its own new feature needs is a version-bridge problem, not a migration problem

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MANAGENIST AI RMF — trustworthy characteristics (valid and reliable)

Choosing how to serve one capability without destabilizing a validated runtime is risk treatment, and NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function is exactly that work: prioritizing responses with the rejected alternatives on the record — whole-stack migration and hand-marshalled plumbing both named and declined. The pin itself protects the 'valid and reliable' trustworthy characteristic: the model stack stays on the interpreter it was validated against. Containing the change at one process boundary exemplifies Domain III.C — governing the maintenance of the model and system.

AIGP takeaway Isolate a needed capability jump at a process boundary; never migrate a validated stack — or silently fall back — for one constant.
Act IV of 8 · 18 lessons · lessons 17–115 (thematic, not contiguous) · 11 on the core path

Governance and decisions — the escalation boundary

Accountable decisions, documented trade-offs, and what is yours to fix versus the operator's to call

Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance

This act trains the governance spine of the deck: who is accountable for which decision, how decisions are recorded for later examination, and where the builder's ownership ends and the operator's call begins. Its home is Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance: organizational expectations, decision rights, and policies across the AI life cycle. The recurring anchor is the GOVERN function of the NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF) — accountability structures, roles, and risk culture — beside the EU AI Act's human-oversight concept: the accountable human gets the genuine decisions, with the information and timing to decide them well. Several lessons reach into Domain III (governing design) and Domain IV (the decision to deploy).

Act IVLesson 17core pathunit 47 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A decision can be right for a reason that is false — re-test the rationale, not just the result.

The failure

BlarAI's governance doc disabled the GPU compile cache "for guaranteed identical compiled state," and because the setting looked right, nobody had ever questioned the reason. A handoff brief then billed re-enabling that cache as "the single highest-value startup fix." Instead of arguing the documentation, the agent measured it: five fresh-process loads of the production pipeline, varying only the cache setting, with outputs hashed. Every run — fresh compile or cache read — produced byte-identical output, the same SHA-256 to the last character. The documented rationale was guarding a risk that does not exist. The "fix" dissolved too: a fresh compile takes about eleven seconds, the first cache write cost fifty-three seconds writing a nine-gigabyte blob, and reading it back cold took just under twelve seconds — slower than recompiling. The thirteen-percent "win" was only the second warm run riding the operating system's file cache. Right setting, false rationale, worthless fix.

Evidence: 2026-06-03 — Measuring the reason, not just the setting: the GPU cache that saved nothing · 2026-05-22 — Boot time: the optimisation I measured to nothing · commit 383f445 (the 2026-05-22 first pass)
Before you turn the page

The cache setting was correct and never caused a problem — so what, exactly, was at risk while its written justification stayed false?

Act IVLesson 17core pathunit 47 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A decision can be right for a reason that is false — re-test the rationale, not just the result.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
about eleven seconds→just under twelve secondsfresh compile vs cold cache readfifty-three secondsfirst boot cache-write costbyte-identicaloutput with cache on or off
Act IVLesson 17core pathunit 47 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A decision can be right for a reason that is false — re-test the rationale, not just the result.

The lesson

A decision can be right for a reason that is false. A documented rationale is a claim, and an unverified claim sitting under a correct decision is still a liability — it misleads the next person who builds on the reason rather than the result. When you revisit a decision, price its premise on the running system, and write the corrected reason down even when the decision itself does not move.

Growing from it

Notice the mechanism: a wrong decision gets corrected because its consequences hurt, but a right decision shields its own rationale from scrutiny forever — nothing ever goes wrong, so nobody ever looks. The danger is not the decision; it is the false reason being reused. The next engineer who reads "cache disabled to guarantee identical compiled state" learns a fact about the system that is not true, and may design around a reproducibility risk that does not exist. When you write or inherit a decision record, treat every "because" clause as a claim with a shelf life: was it measured, by whom, on what system, when? And when you re-validate a decision, test the premise separately from the conclusion — the cheapest finding in this story was that the stated reason and the proposed fix were both false while the setting stayed correct.

Act IVLesson 17core pathunit 47 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A decision can be right for a reason that is false — re-test the rationale, not just the result.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceNIST AI RMF — MEASUREEU AI Act — technical documentation (high-risk obligation)

Decision records are a core Domain I practice — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance — and this lesson shows their failure mode: a record whose rationale never matched reality. The probe that settled it is what the MEASURE function of the NIST AI Risk Management Framework asks for — assess claims with quantitative methods rather than argument. And the EU AI Act's high-risk obligations make technical documentation a named duty precisely because documentation others rely on must describe the system as it is, including corrected reasons and recorded supersessions.

AIGP takeaway Re-test the rationale, not just the result; record the corrected reason even when the decision stands.
Act IVLesson 29core pathunit 48 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Escalate what the user experiences; own how it is wired — a quality or capability tradeoff is the operator's call, never an implementation detail you slip in.

The failure

A vision feature was freezing the operator's machine: a fifty-megapixel photo (8160×6144) hung a describe request for four minutes, while a 2.4-megapixel photo answered in fifty seconds. The agent had the obvious fix — downscale images to about 1.5 megapixels before the vision model — and wrote it up as a "low-risk, standard, reversible" recommended option. The operator stopped it: shrinking that photo would gut the answer for exactly the question it carried — read the text on the wall — and reducing what the product can do is his decision, not something to bury in a recommendation. His correction is now the rule: "Design and technical decisions that have large impacts on the quality of the response is the kind of hard escalation we should be having to me, not stuff asking me about Git which I know nothing about." Only observation shipped; the capability call was his.

Evidence: 2026-06-04 — The fix that logged a lie, and the capability cut I almost made without asking
Before you turn the page

The downscale really was low-risk, standard, and reversible — so what made it the operator's decision rather than the engineer's?

Act IVLesson 29core pathunit 48 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Escalate what the user experiences; own how it is wired — a quality or capability tradeoff is the operator's call, never an implementation detail you slip in.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
four minutes→fifty secondsdescribe time, 50 MP vs 2.450 MP→1.5 MPthe downscale almost shipped silently
Act IVLesson 29core pathunit 48 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Escalate what the user experiences; own how it is wired — a quality or capability tradeoff is the operator's call, never an implementation detail you slip in.

The lesson

Escalate what the user experiences; own how it is wired. A quality or capability trade-off is the operator's call — surfaced explicitly, with alternatives, before implementation — never an implementation detail slipped into a "recommended option." Pure mechanics (commit structure, which thread a call runs on) are the builder's to decide; whether the assistant can still read the sign in the photo is exactly the call to hand up.

Growing from it

The trap is one of perspective: from inside the fix, downscaling an image is a parameter — small, standard, reversible. The capability it destroys is only visible from the user's side of the glass, and the person at that layer was never asked. You will miscalibrate escalation the same way if you sort decisions by technical difficulty: hard-looking things get escalated, easy-looking things get folded in — but difficulty is the wrong axis. The right axis is user impact. Before shipping any fix, ask what the user gains or loses that they could notice; if the answer is anything but "nothing," the accountable owner decides, with the alternatives laid out, before you build. The same axis works in reverse: do not push mechanics — branch strategy, thread placement — onto an owner who cannot evaluate them. You own the wiring; they own the experience.

Act IVLesson 29core pathunit 48 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Escalate what the user experiences; own how it is wired — a quality or capability tradeoff is the operator's call, never an implementation detail you slip in.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceNIST AI RMF — GOVERNEU AI Act — human oversight (high-risk obligation)

This is the escalation boundary as governance: Domain I asks organizations to establish clear expectations and accountability for AI decisions, and the boundary drawn here — capability and quality calls go to the accountable human, wiring stays with the builder — is an accountability structure in the sense of the GOVERN function of the NIST AI Risk Management Framework. It also exemplifies the EU AI Act's human-oversight concept: oversight only works when consequential trade-offs reach the human before implementation, not afterward in a degraded answer.

AIGP takeaway Capability and quality trade-offs escalate to the accountable owner before implementation; implementation mechanics never do.
Act IVLesson 38core pathunit 49 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When the operator's gut says a design is wrong, that is a design-review trigger — re-derive from best practice, do not defend the as-built.

The failure

The provenance sprint shipped a security lock and watched it verify live: paste untrusted content into a session, and every tool switches off until the user types /trust. Every test passed; the screen confirmed it. Then the operator — a self-described non-developer — watched the clock get locked behind a /trust prompt and asked the question the builder stopped asking: "is this locking mechanism really best practice? this whole trusting process seems bad." He was right twice. Locking a clock or calculator adds friction with zero security benefit — they cannot exfiltrate, mutate, or egress anything. And /trust asked the least-equipped person in the system to "accept that the untrusted content could influence the tool" — the un-evaluable are-you-sure the literature calls click-through theatre. The redesign (ADR-023 Amendment 1) gave tools risk tiers: SAFE tools are never locked, and DANGEROUS actions are denied absolutely, with no /trust to override.

Evidence: 2026-06-04 — The operator looked at the lock and said it felt wrong, and he was right
Before you turn the page

Every test passed and the lock verified live — what was the operator measuring that the test suite and the builder were not?

Act IVLesson 38core pathunit 49 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When the operator's gut says a design is wrong, that is a design-review trigger — re-derive from best practice, do not defend the as-built.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act IVLesson 38core pathunit 49 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When the operator's gut says a design is wrong, that is a design-review trigger — re-derive from best practice, do not defend the as-built.

The lesson

When the operator's gut says a design is wrong, that is a design-review trigger. A non-expert's "this feels wrong" about a security experience is rarely noise — it is the smell test the experts stopped running. Re-derive the design from first principles and the external state of practice; the as-built is evidence of what was tried, never proof that it was right.

Growing from it

The pull you must resist is structural: the design had passed every test and verified live, so explaining why it was fine felt like the competent response. But tests and live verification measure a design against its own specification — and the flaw was in the specification. A fresh, non-expert user evaluates the thing experts no longer see: what the experience actually demands of the person using it. Security design fails there constantly — consent prompts nobody can evaluate, friction with no risk reduction — and those failures are invisible to the people who wrote the threat model. When someone's gut objects, especially someone unlike you, do not adjudicate the objection against the as-built; open a real review and re-derive the design from first principles and the published state of practice. If the design survives, you have learned why it is right. This one did not survive.

Act IVLesson 38core pathunit 49 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When the operator's gut says a design is wrong, that is a design-review trigger — re-derive from best practice, do not defend the as-built.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceEU AI Act — human oversight (high-risk obligation)NIST AI RMF — GOVERN

Effective human oversight — a named EU AI Act high-risk obligation — requires that the human can actually evaluate what they are asked to approve; a /trust prompt the user cannot assess is oversight in form only. The redesign restored substance: no human override exists for dangerous actions, and safe ones demand no ritual. The deeper exemplar is the GOVERN function of the NIST AI Risk Management Framework: a risk culture in which a non-expert's challenge triggers review rather than defense is an accountability structure working as designed.

AIGP takeaway A non-expert's "this feels wrong" is a design-review trigger: re-derive from best practice, never defend the as-built.
Act IVLesson 39core pathunit 50 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A locked decision can stay right while the evidence under it quietly rots — re-measure a premise when the stack beneath it moves, not just when you revisit the decision.

The failure

The operator asked: is 16K the right context window — how much conversation the model holds at once — and did we choose it properly? It had been: decision record DEC-03 locked it on a 42-configuration sweep. But one supporting finding — that speculative decoding (a speed-up using a small helper model) goes inert at large context, so the big window costs no speed — was measured 2026-03-03, before two stack changes: the fix that made speculative decoding engage, and a helper-model swap. The agent nearly quoted the stale finding as current; a date caught it, not a re-run. A targeted re-measurement settled it: acceptance at 16K was 46%, not zero, throughput about 2.3 times the recorded number — the decision reaffirmed. The same run found a second premise rotted the other way: the memory footprint nearly quadrupled, 3,562 MB to 12,715 MB, making "20K is safe" the suspect claim.

Evidence: 2026-06-05 — The benchmark that aged out from under a locked decision
Before you turn the page

DEC-03 was still the right call — so what, concretely, had gone stale, and what is the cheapest signal that would have caught it?

Act IVLesson 39core pathunit 50 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A locked decision can stay right while the evidence under it quietly rots — re-measure a premise when the stack beneath it moves, not just when you revisit the decision.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
0→46%spec-decode acceptance at 16K2.3×throughput vs the recorded number3,562 MB→12,715 MBthe 16K footprint on re-measure
Act IVLesson 39core pathunit 50 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A locked decision can stay right while the evidence under it quietly rots — re-measure a premise when the stack beneath it moves, not just when you revisit the decision.

The lesson

A locked decision can stay right while the evidence under it quietly rots. A decision's premises have their own shelf life, independent of the decision itself; when the stack beneath a locked call moves — a model swap, a runtime upgrade — re-measure its premises on the running system before you quote them. A right decision resting on a rotted reason is still a liability.

Growing from it

Premises rot silently because nothing triggers their review: the decision works, the record looks settled, and every component change happens somewhere else, on someone else's timeline. You catch it with dates — the cheapest instrument there is. Date every measurement inside a decision record, and when you are about to cite one, compare its date against what has changed underneath it: a model swap, a runtime upgrade, or a re-tuned component invalidates measurements that predate it, whatever the document says. Be equally careful with the comforting answer "we already have that data": check what the data actually covered. Here, recent benchmarks did prove the new decoding worked — but every one ran at short context, so the 16K corner was genuinely unmeasured, and "we have it" would have been a true answer to the wrong question. One decision, two premises, each on its own clock.

Act IVLesson 39core pathunit 50 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A locked decision can stay right while the evidence under it quietly rots — re-measure a premise when the stack beneath it moves, not just when you revisit the decision.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceNIST AI RMF — MEASUREISO/IEC 42001 — continual improvement (plan-do-check-act)

The AI life cycle is the organizing idea of Domain I's policies-and-procedures competency, and this lesson is life-cycle thinking applied to decision records: evidence has a maintenance phase, just as systems do. The MEASURE function of the NIST AI Risk Management Framework asks for risks to be tracked over time, not assessed once — re-measuring a premise after the stack moves is exactly that. ISO/IEC 42001's continual-improvement loop, the plan-do-check-act pattern, exemplifies the same posture: a locked decision still gets a check when its inputs change.

AIGP takeaway Decision premises have their own shelf life; re-measure them when the stack moves, not only when the decision is revisited.
Act IVLesson 45unit 51 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When a mess keeps reappearing, find the doctrine producing it before you clean it up again.

The failure

Eleven session handoff briefs had piled up in the repository root, and the operator wanted them gone — reasonably; the root is no place for transient continuity notes. The quick fix was mechanical: move them to docs/handoffs/ and add a gitignore line so they stop cluttering the status view. But the briefs had not landed in root by accident. The project's own context-exhaustion doctrine — the standing instruction every agent session inherits — literally told each session to write its handoff brief at the repository root. Hiding the directory would have buried the symptom while the cause kept manufacturing new instances: the very next session to hit its context limit would have dutifully dropped a fresh brief back in root. So the real fix was the doctrine, amended in both of its copies. The relocation and the rule changed together (commits e0fd0d0, fe809b9) — or neither would have held.

Evidence: 2026-06-05 — The briefs piled up in root because the doctrine told them to · commits e0fd0d0, fe809b9
Before you turn the page

The directory move was quick and it worked — why was it guaranteed to fail anyway, and what was the eleventh brief telling you that the first one was not?

Act IVLesson 45unit 51 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When a mess keeps reappearing, find the doctrine producing it before you clean it up again.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Elevenhandoff briefs piled in root
Act IVLesson 45unit 51 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When a mess keeps reappearing, find the doctrine producing it before you clean it up again.

The lesson

When a mess keeps reappearing, find the doctrine producing it before you clean it up again. A recurring-clutter symptom usually has a policy default standing behind it; cleaning the instances without amending the policy guarantees the next instance. The durable fix changes the rule and the residue in the same motion.

Growing from it

Recurrence is the diagnostic. A one-off mess is an accident; a mess that re-forms after every cleanup is being produced, and something is doing the producing — an instruction, a default, an incentive. Cleanup feels productive because it is visible and bounded, while the policy behind the mess is invisible and feels out of scope, so most people sweep the same floor indefinitely. In systems run by AI agents this is sharper still: written doctrine is effectively executable code, followed literally by every session that inherits it, so a bad default replicates with perfect fidelity — eleven briefs is not eleven mistakes, it is one instruction executed eleven times. When you find yourself tidying the same thing twice, stop and trace the instance back to the rule that generated it, and change both together. The mess is data about your policy layer.

Act IVLesson 45unit 51 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When a mess keeps reappearing, find the doctrine producing it before you clean it up again.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceNIST AI RMF — GOVERNISO/IEC 42001 — plan-do-check-act (AIMS)

Domain I's competencies center on establishing policies and procedures across the AI life cycle — and this lesson is about maintaining them: when outcomes go wrong repeatedly, the policy layer is the suspect, and amending doctrine is the corrective act. That is the GOVERN function of the NIST AI Risk Management Framework in miniature — governance as the cross-cutting layer that shapes every downstream behavior. It also exemplifies ISO/IEC 42001's plan-do-check-act management-system pattern: the act step corrects the system that produced the problem, not merely the artifact it produced.

AIGP takeaway Recurring messes are produced by policy; amend the doctrine and clean the residue in the same change.
Act IVLesson 49core pathunit 52 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A design gate — the ADR written and reviewed before a line of cipher code — turns the hard seam-choices into translation work, and moves the review surface from the code to the ADR.

The failure

The task was a cryptographic envelope — the dual-wrapped key machinery under BlarAI's at-rest encryption — the kind of code where unit tests feel deceptively reassuring. Round-trips pass, tamper tests raise, and none of it proves the design is right: the real failures are quiet wrong-shapes at the seam between components — a version byte the inner blob doesn't expect, a recovery wrap that encodes the raw key by accident — each a passing test over a silently broken security property. So the three hard choices were pinned in ADR-025, an Architecture Decision Record reviewed before any cipher code existed: a fresh single-use random nonce per encryption, a random 256-bit recovery key rather than a passphrase, and every ciphertext bound to its table, column, and row identity. Implementation then arrived as translation (commit c824f6a, 55 tests), and the merge review read the ADR rather than reverse-engineering intent from code comments.

Evidence: 2026-06-05 — The key that wraps the key: shipping the DEK envelope before any real data lands · commit c824f6a (merged 18bafe7)
Before you turn the page

If a security property can fail while every test passes, at what point in the work is a wrong choice still visible enough to catch — and to whom?

Act IVLesson 49core pathunit 52 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A design gate — the ADR written and reviewed before a line of cipher code — turns the hard seam-choices into translation work, and moves the review surface from the code to the ADR.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
55 teststhe envelope suite at merge
Act IVLesson 49core pathunit 52 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A design gate — the ADR written and reviewed before a line of cipher code — turns the hard seam-choices into translation work, and moves the review surface from the code to the ADR.

The lesson

A design gate — the decision record written and reviewed before the first line of high-stakes code — turns hard seam-choices into translation work and moves the review surface from the code to the document. The path-not-taken lands on the record at the design layer, where it is legible, instead of being implied by an implementation.

Growing from it

Why a gate works: in most code, a wrong choice announces itself — something crashes. In cryptographic and security code, the wrong choice passes its tests and simply fails to protect, so the only moment a choice is genuinely inspectable is before it dissolves into implementation. A reviewer reading finished cipher code must reverse-engineer which behaviors are decisions and which are accidents; a reviewer reading a three-choice decision record sees exactly what was decided, why, and what was rejected. Use the gate wherever failure is silent — encryption, privacy handling, anything touching money or safety: write the few load-bearing choices down, get them reviewed, and only then build. Expect a bonus: the document also exposes its own gaps. Here, the one seam the record was silent on became the one place the merge review knew to look hardest.

Act IVLesson 49core pathunit 52 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A design gate — the ADR written and reviewed before a line of cipher code — turns the hard seam-choices into translation work, and moves the review surface from the code to the ADR.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentISO/IEC 42001 — Annex A controls on the AI system life cycleEU AI Act — technical documentation (high-risk obligation)

This is Domain III.A in practice — governing the designing and building of the AI model and system: the governance act happens before construction, where it can still shape it. ISO/IEC 42001's Annex A controls on the AI system life cycle take the same posture — documented design decisions as management-system artifacts, reviewed and justified. And a design record that names the path-not-taken is what the EU AI Act's technical-documentation obligation is for at concept level: documentation that lets a reviewer assess the system without excavating its source.

AIGP takeaway Where failure is silent, gate the design: review the decision record before code exists, with rejected alternatives on the record.
Act IVLesson 66core pathunit 53 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A test can lock a defect, not just a feature — so "it's test-locked" is not proof "it's a decision to escalate."

The failure

The night of BlarAI's first production boot, the encrypted session store bricked the entire app: practice-era rows written under the old development key would not decrypt under the production key, and one undecryptable row aborted the whole session list — an app-wide "backend not running" from one bad record. A green Sprint-14 regression test (a test that pins existing behaviour) asserted exactly that brick-everything behaviour. Twice in one night, the agent reached to escalate the fix as a security-posture decision, handing the operator an A/B — reset the data, or change the store's behaviour — as though the passing test proved the behaviour was intended. The operator collapsed it each time: "Fix this so it doesn't keep happening — is there really more than one correct answer?" There was not. The fix quarantines bad rows in bulk reads; single-record paths still fail hard; confidentiality and integrity untouched (commits 4af2033, 6fe1fcc).

Evidence: 2026-06-06 — The night BlarAI first answered a prompt in production · 2026-06-06 — When "fail closed" becomes a door that locks you out of your own house · commits 4af2033, 6fe1fcc (#618)
Before you turn the page

A passing regression test guarded the exact behaviour that bricked the app — what does a green test actually prove about whether the behaviour was ever a decision?

Act IVLesson 66core pathunit 53 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A test can lock a defect, not just a feature — so "it's test-locked" is not proof "it's a decision to escalate."

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
one bad rowenough to brick the whole app2,163green tests while production bricked
Act IVLesson 66core pathunit 53 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A test can lock a defect, not just a feature — so "it's test-locked" is not proof "it's a decision to escalate."

The lesson

A test can lock a defect, not just a feature — so "it's test-locked" is not proof "it's a decision to escalate." Before escalating a change as a decision, ask whether the behaviour it reverses was ever correct or merely locked in; a defect with one nameable fix is yours to fix even when a passing test guards it. The test is then part of the defect, and correcting it is part of the fix.

Growing from it

Tests carry borrowed authority. A green assertion reads like ratified intent — somebody decided this, the suite enforces it — but a test only proves that an expectation was once written down, and it inherits every misjudgment of the person who wrote it. Here the Sprint-14 author had encoded a sensible instinct, "authentication failure is unrecoverable," at the wrong granularity, and the test then defended the defect like a feature. When you find yourself escalating because a test guards the current behaviour, interrogate the test's pedigree: was this behaviour ever correct, for a reason someone could state — or merely locked in? Then apply the boundary test: a defect has one correct fix you can name; a decision has trade-offs only the accountable owner should weigh. One correct answer means fix it — and rewriting the test is part of the fix, not tampering with the record.

Act IVLesson 66core pathunit 53 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A test can lock a defect, not just a feature — so "it's test-locked" is not proof "it's a decision to escalate."

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceNIST AI RMF — GOVERNNIST AI RMF — MANAGE

The escalation boundary is an accountability structure — the GOVERN function of the NIST AI Risk Management Framework asks exactly this: who is authorized to decide what, and on what evidence. Mis-classifying a defect as a decision stalls the risk response the MANAGE function calls for — the fix existed, was nameable, and waited on a round-trip the operator did not need. Domain I frames the same discipline: governance gets the consequential trade-offs to the accountable human and keeps routine correction moving without manufactured ceremony.

AIGP takeaway A green test is not a decision record; a defect with one correct fix is yours to fix, test included.
Act IVLesson 69unit 54 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A gate checklist never re-verified against the code drifts in both directions.

The failure

Preparing the air-gap-removal gate, an audit walked every criterion of the security roadmap's §5 tracker against the code on disk — and found the checklist wrong in both directions. Two controls listed as still-remaining had shipped sprints earlier: the tamper-evident audit stream (hash-chained, signed by the machine's TPM security chip) and the runtime guard that blocks outbound network connections, armed at the launcher's entry point at a verifiable file and line. Meanwhile the item the document implied was nearly done — model weight integrity — was only half built: the primary model binary was hash-checked at load; every other manifest entry was not. The walk also surfaced an open question the checklist had smoothed over: the boot code's docstring promised "attestation" while the implementation performed key-and-manifest validation — two different controls. The rewrite (commit 2800d53) replaced checkboxes with file:line evidence, explicit DONE/PARTIAL/REMAINING labels, and the open decisions named as decisions.

Evidence: 2026-06-07 — The checklist that had never been verified · commit 2800d53
Before you turn the page

The checklist was wrong in both directions at once — which direction is merely expensive, which one is dangerous, and why do they accumulate differently?

Act IVLesson 69unit 54 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A gate checklist never re-verified against the code drifts in both directions.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act IVLesson 69unit 54 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A gate checklist never re-verified against the code drifts in both directions.

The lesson

A gate checklist never re-verified against the code drifts in both directions — into understatement (shipped work still listed as remaining) and overstatement (half-built work implied done). The only authoritative tracker is one walked against the code on disk, carrying file:line evidence, distinguishing done from partial from remaining, and naming the open decisions it finds rather than smoothing them into uniform progress.

Growing from it

Status trackers decay by design: they are written once at planning time, read many times at decision time, and nothing in between forces them to notice the work shipping around them. Expect both failure directions, because they cost differently. Understatement wastes effort — a builder re-plans work that already exists. Overstatement is the dangerous one: a go/no-go gate that consumes the tracker passes on controls that are not actually there. The repair is not diligence; it is format. A tracker whose every claim carries evidence — a file and line you can open, a status that distinguishes done from partial — is falsifiable, and falsifiable documents get corrected. Before any decision that consumes a status document, walk the document against the thing it describes; and when the walk surfaces an ambiguity — here, two different meanings of "attestation" — record it as an open decision, not a smoothed-over checkbox.

Act IVLesson 69unit 54 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A gate checklist never re-verified against the code drifts in both directions.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceISO/IEC 42001 — Statement of ApplicabilityNIST AI RMF — MEASURE

A control-status tracker is the working ancestor of ISO/IEC 42001's Statement of Applicability — the document recording which controls apply, with justification — and this lesson is its maintenance discipline: such a record is only authoritative when verified against the system it describes. The walk — claims checked against evidence, gaps tracked — is what the MEASURE function of the NIST AI Risk Management Framework asks of assurance work. Domain I frames the stake: gate decisions are governance acts, and they are only as sound as the records they consume.

AIGP takeaway Status checklists drift both directions; only a tracker walked against the code, with file:line evidence, is authoritative at a gate.
Act IVLesson 72unit 55 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Closing the debt a sprint exists to close is act-not-ask — escalate trade-offs, not the obvious completion of the stated goal.

The failure

Sprint 16 existed for one purpose: regression locks that actually fire. The agent found the just-built production-parity test lane sitting in a directory the standing gate command never ran — a green gate said nothing about whether those locks passed — and even wrote the structural insight up as lesson 70. Then, instead of folding the lane into the gate, it escalated the fix to the operator as a "governance/baseline-scope decision." His reply cut through it: "why are you even considering not closing the debt fully? obviously that is the goal here; the goal is to have these actually working." Closing it widened the standing gate from 2187 to 2212 green tests — and surfaced the same gap one layer worse: the security posture guards, the most consequential locks in the repository, sat in neither the gate nor the configured test paths. Orphaned. They were folded in the same day.

Evidence: 2026-06-07 — Closing the debt the sprint existed to close
Before you turn the page

The gate's scope sounds like governance — what one question separates a genuine governance decision from the obvious completion of the sprint's stated goal?

Act IVLesson 72unit 55 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Closing the debt a sprint exists to close is act-not-ask — escalate trade-offs, not the obvious completion of the stated goal.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
2187→2212standing gate after folding the lane
Act IVLesson 72unit 55 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Closing the debt a sprint exists to close is act-not-ask — escalate trade-offs, not the obvious completion of the stated goal.

The lesson

Closing the debt a sprint exists to close is act-not-ask — escalate trade-offs, not the obvious completion of the stated goal. A scope change with a genuine downside is the operator's call; folding a just-built lock into the gate so it does its job has no trade-off to weigh. It is the work. Before escalating, ask whether the choice has a real downside the owner must weigh, or whether you are deferring the obvious.

Growing from it

The misclassification has a precise shape: the fix touched a governance-flavored object — the official test-gate scope — and anything governance-flavored triggered the escalation reflex, regardless of whether a genuine trade-off existed. That is sorting by the object touched instead of by the content of the choice. The test that works: try to name the downside of the obvious action, concretely, as something the owner would weigh. "The gate takes slightly longer to run" is not a trade-off against "the locks we just built actually fire"; if you cannot state a real downside, there is no decision — act, and report what you did. False escalations are not free caution: each one spends the owner's attention, stalls finished work behind a round-trip, and trains the owner to rubber-stamp — which corrodes the escalation channel you need for the calls that are genuinely theirs.

Act IVLesson 72unit 55 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Closing the debt a sprint exists to close is act-not-ask — escalate trade-offs, not the obvious completion of the stated goal.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceNIST AI RMF — MANAGENIST AI RMF — GOVERN

The MANAGE function of the NIST AI Risk Management Framework is about implementing risk responses, not merely selecting them — and a regression lock outside the gate that runs is a risk treatment selected but never implemented. Completing it is execution, not policy. The GOVERN function carries the other half: well-designed accountability structures route genuine trade-offs to the accountable human and keep completion work moving without ceremony. Domain I names this calibration — oversight spent on real decisions is oversight that stays meaningful.

AIGP takeaway If you cannot name a real downside the owner must weigh, it is not a decision — finish the work.
Act IVLesson 84unit 56 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A sequencing or packing choice that changes nothing built is the orchestrator's to own, not a fork to hand the operator — and offering "or we defer half" reads as proposing they stop short.

The failure

Preparing Sprint 17, the agent put two "decisions" in front of the operator. One was genuinely his: whether the air-gap gate requires true measured-boot attestation — hardware-backed proof of what software booted — a control that guards physical tampering, is orthogonal to the gate's network threat, and fail-closes on every legitimate firmware update until re-baselined. He decided it, informed, and it became Architecture Decision Record ADR-028 (commit cd86442). The other "decision" — build the outbound-network machinery this sprint or hold half for the next — changed nothing about what gets built and nothing about answer quality. It was packing: how disjoint work distributes across waves. Dressed as "…or we could defer half to Sprint 18," it read to the operator exactly as it should have — as the agent proposing he stop short of finishing. Taken back and owned: Sprint 17 builds the whole cluster, in parallel, and finishes.

Evidence: 2026-06-07 — The control that guards a different door · commit cd86442
Before you turn the page

Two questions sat side by side — attestation scope and sprint packing — and only one was the operator's. What is the single test that separates them?

Act IVLesson 84unit 56 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A sequencing or packing choice that changes nothing built is the orchestrator's to own, not a fork to hand the operator — and offering "or we defer half" reads as proposing they stop short.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act IVLesson 84unit 56 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A sequencing or packing choice that changes nothing built is the orchestrator's to own, not a fork to hand the operator — and offering "or we defer half" reads as proposing they stop short.

The lesson

A sequencing or packing choice that changes nothing built is the orchestrator's to own, not a fork to hand the operator. The honest tell: if the alternative you are offering is "do less of the agreed work later," you are not surfacing a decision — you are proposing to stop short. Decide it yourself and keep moving.

Growing from it

Both questions looked alike — sprint-scope items, side by side — and that is the trap: classification by surface. They differ on the only axis that matters: does any answer change an outcome the owner carries? The attestation choice changed the security posture and bound him to a decades-long maintenance cost; whichever way it went, he would live with it, so it was his. The packing choice had identical end states: the same work built either way, differing only in which week. Before you surface a fork, write the difference between its branches in outcome terms — capability, quality, risk, cost the owner bears. If that diff is empty, the fork is yours; surfacing it anyway is not humility, it is offloading. And watch the framing of deferral: "or we could do half later" does not read as an option — it reads as your appetite to finish.

Act IVLesson 84unit 56 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A sequencing or packing choice that changes nothing built is the orchestrator's to own, not a fork to hand the operator — and offering "or we defer half" reads as proposing they stop short.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceNIST AI RMF — GOVERNEU AI Act — human oversight (high-risk obligation)

Allocating decisions to roles is the heart of the GOVERN function of the NIST AI Risk Management Framework — accountability structures that say which calls belong to the accountable human and which to the delegate. The EU AI Act's human-oversight concept presumes the same calibration: oversight is effective only when the human's attention is spent on forks with real stakes — an attestation posture with decades of cost — rather than on packing choices with identical outcomes. Domain I frames decision-rights design as a foundational governance expectation.

AIGP takeaway If no branch of the fork changes what is built or its quality, the choice is the orchestrator's — own it.
Act IVLesson 85core pathunit 57 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP
