Schedule the human's understanding pass BEFORE the irreversible decision, not after — with a remediation loop so what they learn can still change the outcome.

The failure

The capstone security presentation (ticket #612) — the full review of what risks remain once BlarAI's air-gap, its total disconnection from the network, comes off — was sequenced after the air-gap-removal sign-off, as its ticket read: an after-the-fact "verified posture" bookend. The operator moved it. That presentation is his real deep-dive into the residual risks of facing the network — the single biggest threat-model shift in the project, the one irreversible-feeling door — and it belongs before he signs, not after: he wants the thorough briefing, the chance to ask questions, and any work the questions surface tasked and resolved before the gap closes behind the decision. A briefing scheduled after a gate can only justify a decision already taken; before it, with a remediation loop, it can still stop or reshape the outcome. The sequence was rewritten into the gate itself as a sign-off precondition (commits 452ac98, 021ffda).

Evidence: 2026-06-07 — The briefing before the irreversible door · commits 452ac98, 021ffda
Before you turn the page

The same presentation, the same content, the same audience — what changes about its governance value when it moves from after the sign-off to before it?

Act IVLesson 85core pathunit 57 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Schedule the human's understanding pass BEFORE the irreversible decision, not after — with a remediation loop so what they learn can still change the outcome.

rendering diagram…
Act IVLesson 85core pathunit 57 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Schedule the human's understanding pass BEFORE the irreversible decision, not after — with a remediation loop so what they learn can still change the outcome.

The lesson

Schedule the human's understanding pass before the irreversible decision, not after — with a remediation loop so what they learn can still change the outcome. A briefing after the gate can only explain a decision already made; before it, it makes the sign-off an informed act rather than a ceremony. Signed is not safe until understood.

Growing from it

Briefings drift to the wrong side of decisions for a mundane reason: from the builder's seat, the presentation is a deliverable — a summary of finished work — so it gets scheduled where deliverables go, at the end. But an approval is only worth the information and authority the approver held at the moment of signing; everything they learn afterward improves nothing about the decision. So sort your reviews by what they can still change. Before the gate, with a loop that lets surfaced questions become tasked work, a briefing has power: it can reshape or stop the outcome. After the gate it is a ceremony, and the signer's questions become regrets. Reserve this ordering especially for the irreversible calls — the doors that do not reopen — and check who the presentation is for: if it exists so the decision-maker understands, it must precede the decision by definition.

Act IVLesson 85core pathunit 57 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Schedule the human's understanding pass BEFORE the irreversible decision, not after — with a remediation loop so what they learn can still change the outcome.

The AIGP connection
Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and UseEU AI Act — human oversight (high-risk obligation)NIST AI RMF — MAP

Domain IV.A is the competency this lesson trains: evaluating the key factors and risks relevant to the decision to deploy — here, the decision to let a hardened local system face the network. The EU AI Act's human-oversight concept is sharpest at such gates: oversight means the accountable human understands the residual risks before the irreversible act, not after. Sequencing the briefing ahead of the sign-off — the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's MAP function, establishing context — makes the approval an exercise of oversight rather than its record.

AIGP takeaway Signed is not safe until understood: the understanding pass precedes the irreversible decision, with remediation in the loop.
Act IVLesson 86core pathunit 58 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Automate by default; a human step must be proven necessary, not inherited from a runbook's wording.

The failure

Sprint 17's hardware tiers needed the real chip; the verification plan scripted them as an operator-driven session — one command at a time, the human pasting outputs back. That framing went verbatim to the execution agent — running on the dev box with full shell access — and the operator got a "give me the first command" kickoff draft. He stopped it: "It's having me do testing! Why would it do that? All testing should be automated. My time is being wasted, again." Root cause: the script fits exactly one situation — a guiding session with no path to the hardware. The "operator runs X, pastes Y" phrasing carried through four layers — runbooks, the verification plan, the execution brief, the kickoff message — and no layer asked whether the executing agent could simply do the work. It could — and ran the entire on-chip session itself, surfacing only results.

Evidence: 2026-06-08 — The on-chip session I told the agent to make the operator run
Before you turn the page

The runbook said "the operator runs each command" — what was that sentence actually evidence of, and what question should each layer that copied it have asked?

Act IVLesson 86core pathunit 58 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Automate by default; a human step must be proven necessary, not inherited from a runbook's wording.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
four layerscarried the operator-runs-it phrasing
Act IVLesson 86core pathunit 58 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Automate by default; a human step must be proven necessary, not inherited from a runbook's wording.

The lesson

Automate by default; a human step must be proven necessary, not inherited from a runbook's wording. An agent on hardware it can reach runs every step itself; a step routes to the human only when proven to need human hands — an elevation the shell cannot obtain, a physical action, a presence assertion. The burden of proof sits on routing a step out to the human, never on automating it.

Growing from it

Runbooks smuggle their author's constraints into your present as if they were the task's requirements. "The operator runs X" was a fact about whoever first wrote the procedure — someone with no path to the hardware — not a property of X; and because each layer copied the phrasing instead of asking what it was for, the assumption survived four hand-offs into a context where it was false. The durable repair is to flip the burden of proof. If human-in-the-loop is the default, automation must be argued step by step, and it never is; if automation is the default, every human step must carry its proof — a privilege the agent genuinely cannot obtain, a physical act, a required presence. Treat one-time blockers as one-time: solve access once rather than routing every command through a person. The human's real contribution is judgment; every rote step removed protects it.

Act IVLesson 86core pathunit 58 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Automate by default; a human step must be proven necessary, not inherited from a runbook's wording.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceNIST AI RMF — GOVERNNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)

Role design is GOVERN-function territory in the NIST AI Risk Management Framework — accountability structures and workforce: deciding what genuinely needs the human. The verification work itself belongs to MEASURE, the home of TEVV (test, evaluation, verification, and validation) — and TEVV done by hand at human speed is TEVV that gets skipped; automating it is what makes assurance sustainable for decades. Domain I carries the principle: human attention is the scarcest governance resource, and a mature system spends it on oversight and judgment, not on pasting commands.

AIGP takeaway Automate by default; route a step to the human only with proof it needs human hands.
Act IVLesson 99unit 59 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A decision is only a decision if the options differ on security, capability, or quality — otherwise it is mechanics the builder owns.

The failure

The #639 decision record always named the destination: inline operator confirmation, with escalations approved by fingerprint via Windows Hello, Windows' built-in biometric sign-in. Scoping that follow-on (#649), the builder asked the operator to choose the implementation route — a small C# helper subprocess versus an in-process Python binding. He pushed back: 'Why are you asking me this? I am a novice. Does this impact security, capability, or performance such that I need to make a decision?' It didn't. The only fork with a real posture delta — adding a new Python dependency to the runtime — had an obvious answer under the project's supply-chain posture: don't. Mechanics had been dressed as governance, costing the operator exactly the involvement he is trying to minimize. The builder made the call, said so, and built; the operator's own live-verify ran both directions — three real fingerprint matches APPROVED, three cancels DENIED (merge c1f51e9).

Evidence: 2026-06-10 — The fingerprint, and the decision I tried to hand back · commit b65f1c5 · commit c1f51e9
Before you turn the page

Before handing the decision-maker a choice between two implementation routes, what must differ between the routes for the question to be legitimate at all?

Act IVLesson 99unit 59 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A decision is only a decision if the options differ on security, capability, or quality — otherwise it is mechanics the builder owns.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
~145-lineC# helper, zero new dependencies3 APPROVEDreal fingerprint matches, live-verified3 DENIEDcancels through the same dialog
Act IVLesson 99unit 59 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A decision is only a decision if the options differ on security, capability, or quality — otherwise it is mechanics the builder owns.

The lesson

Handing a novice operator an implementation choice with no governance delta inverts the responsibility the builder is paid to carry, and costs the operator exactly the involvement he is trying to minimize. The check is cheap: name what each option changes for the operator; if the answer is 'nothing he governs,' decide, state the call, and keep moving.

Growing from it

Asking feels safe because it transfers accountability — so escalation drifts upward until the decision-maker is adjudicating wiring he cannot evaluate, which is itself a governance failure: oversight bandwidth is finite, and spending it on mechanics starves the calls only the human can make. Run a two-column test before every ask: what does each option change for the person you are asking (security, capability, quality), versus only for the builder (effort, structure, style)? An empty first column means it is your call — make it, state it with the named trade-off (here: a few hundred milliseconds of subprocess spawn accepted over a forever-pinned supply-chain dependency). The inverse duty stands: a real posture delta, like the new-dependency fork, still gets surfaced even inside a 'mechanics' decision. Sharpens lessons 29, 37 and 84 — and this is the project's cleanest statement of the boundary, articulated by the operator himself.

Act IVLesson 99unit 59 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A decision is only a decision if the options differ on security, capability, or quality — otherwise it is mechanics the builder owns.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceNIST AI RMF — GOVERNEU AI Act — human oversight (concept level)

NIST AI RMF's GOVERN function establishes roles and accountability structures — who decides what — and calibrated escalation is that structure working: forks with governance weight (a new supply-chain dependency) surface; mechanics do not. The EU AI Act's human-oversight concept is meaningful oversight, which over-asking dilutes as surely as under-asking evades. Fittingly, the artifact built here makes the operator's fingerprint the consent ceremony itself — Domain I.B: establishing and communicating expectations for who approves what, enforced fail-closed.

AIGP takeaway Escalate only forks that change what the overseer governs; deciding mechanics yourself is part of keeping human oversight meaningful.
Act IVLesson 100unit 60 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

An "unbounded by default" comment is often an unclosed gap wearing a decision's clothes.

The failure

The audit log — the system's tamper-evident record of its own governance decisions — carried a docstring (code's own explanatory note) that read like a settled decision: append-only, unbounded by default, forensic completeness for a decades-use system. Half the claim was sound: the records are metadata only (decisions and hashes, no content), a decades-long history is a rounding error on disk, and that history is the governance evidence. The literal half was deferred work in a decision's clothes: every record loaded into RAM at construction, the active file re-read whole every boot, memory growing with total history forever. #607 made the decision actually open — rotate-and-retain: the active audit.jsonl seals into a compressed segment at 64 MiB or 100k records, and a signed segment index pins each file's SHA-256 fingerprint so tamper-evidence spans files. ADR-029, an architecture decision record, names the rejected paths: time-based purge, first-in-first-out size cap, the status-quo single file.

Evidence: 2026-06-10 — Keeping everything without keeping it all in RAM
Before you turn the page

When a comment says a behavior was chosen on purpose, what evidence would tell you a decision was actually made — rather than work never done?

Act IVLesson 100unit 60 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

An "unbounded by default" comment is often an unclosed gap wearing a decision's clothes.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
38→62audit-log tests after #607 rotate-and-retain64 MiB or 100k recordsthreshold at which the active segment seals
Act IVLesson 100unit 60 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

An "unbounded by default" comment is often an unclosed gap wearing a decision's clothes.

The lesson

When a comment asserts a design choice that is also the path of least resistance, check whether the valuable part of the claim and the convenient part are the same claim. Here they were not: keep-everything was sound policy; keep-everything-in-one-file-in-RAM was just work nobody had done. Separate the two, make the real decision, and put the rejected alternatives on the record.

Growing from it

The mechanism is that deferral likes to dress itself as intent. Writing "unbounded by default" is cheaper than building rotation, and once written it reads as a settled stance to every later reader — including you. The trick is that the claim bundles two things: a value judgment (keep all the evidence) and an implementation accident (one file, everything in memory), and the second rides in on the credibility of the first. The tell is coincidence: whenever the documented "choice" is exactly the zero-work option, suspect a gap in costume. The test is cheap: split the claim, ask whether the valuable half actually requires the convenient half, and look for named rejected alternatives — a real decision leaves them behind; a default leaves none. When you find none, the decision is still open, and it is yours to make explicitly and record.

Act IVLesson 100unit 60 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

An "unbounded by default" comment is often an unclosed gap wearing a decision's clothes.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceNIST AI RMF — GOVERNEU AI Act — record-keeping and logging (high-risk obligation, concept level)

An audit trail is the record-keeping function the EU AI Act names among high-risk obligations, at concept level — and retention is part of that obligation: decided, not defaulted. The move #607 exemplifies is the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's GOVERN function — converting an implicit default into explicit policy, with the trade-off and the rejected alternatives documented in ADR-029. AIGP Domain I.C — establish policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle — is precisely this: a retention stance that exists because someone weighed it, on the record.

AIGP takeaway Record-keeping obligations include retention; a documented retention policy with rejected alternatives on the record is governance — an inherited default is not.
Act IVLesson 103core pathunit 61 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Verify the vocabulary you teach against the current source before you teach it.

The failure

Building the lessons-teaching deck (#650) — 103 study units drafted from this very journal — the agent building it "knew" the IAPP AIGP (Artificial Intelligence Governance Professional) exam as seven domains. Live-source verification, run before a single mapping was drafted, showed the Body of Knowledge had been restructured to four domains in v2.0.1 (February 2025), with v2.1 effective 2026-02-02 adding ISO/IEC 42005 and agentic architectures. Every framework claim in the deck would have taught a one-restructure-stale exam, at the level the certification actually tests. The catch happened only because verification was made a gate, not a courtesy: a pinned aigp_vocabulary.md became the only vocabulary the content may use, and validate_acts.py enforces it mechanically — beside an anti-hallucination check that every cited journal entry and commit SHA (the unique fingerprint of a saved change) must exist verbatim in BUILD_JOURNAL.md. Eight drafting agents wrote in parallel against that contract; the validator came back 103 units, zero failures.

Evidence: 2026-06-10 — Teaching the journal its own lessons, and the vocabulary that had rotted under me
Before you turn the page

Before teaching a framework's structure from memory, what tells you the structure has not changed since you learned it?

Act IVLesson 103core pathunit 61 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Verify the vocabulary you teach against the current source before you teach it.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
seven domains, as remembered→four domains — BoK v2.0.1, February 2025the AIGP exam structure on live-source check103 units, zero failuresvalidator verdict across eight parallel drafting agents
Act IVLesson 103core pathunit 61 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Verify the vocabulary you teach against the current source before you teach it.

The lesson

Domain knowledge in your head — or an agent's — has a shelf life: a certification syllabus, a framework, or an API can restructure under you between the day you learned it and the day you use it. Teaching from memory propagates the rot to a student. The staleness gets caught only when verification is a gate the content must pass, not a courtesy someone may remember to extend.

Growing from it

Knowledge is a cache with no expiry tag. The day you learned a fact it was true, and nothing about how your head — or a model's training — stores it marks the date or watches the source; fluency even works against you, because the facts you can recite most confidently are usually the oldest. Anything versioned is the high-risk class: syllabi, standards, APIs, laws all restructure on their own schedule, silently invalidating whoever memorized the previous shape. The defence is structural, because "remember to double-check" is exactly what fluency defeats. When your output will teach — documentation, training material, a curriculum, a compliance mapping — pin the vocabulary first: one sheet of claims verified against named, current sources on a stated date, and a mechanical validator that gates publication on using only that sheet. Then point the same suspicion at yourself that you point at any stale document: you are one.

Act IVLesson 103core pathunit 61 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Verify the vocabulary you teach against the current source before you teach it.

The AIGP connection
Domain II — Understanding How Laws, Standards and Frameworks Apply to AIISO/IEC 42001 — plan-do-check-act continual improvement

AIGP Domain II — understanding how laws, standards and frameworks apply to AI — presumes that knowledge of the frameworks is current, and this failure shows the competency decaying in place: the Body of Knowledge itself moved from seven domains to four (v2.0.1, February 2025), and v2.1, effective 2026-02-02, added ISO/IEC 42005 and agentic architectures. Re-verifying framework claims against live sources before relying on them exemplifies the plan-do-check-act continual-improvement pattern at the heart of ISO/IEC 42001's AI management system — the check applied to the governing knowledge, not only the governed system.

AIGP takeaway Framework knowledge expires without notice — verify the current structure against live sources before mapping or teaching anything to it.
Act IVLesson 105core pathunit 62 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Demonstrating a capability is not deciding to deploy it — a proof-of-concept that ends in a deliberate "no" is a success, not a waste.

The failure

A proof-of-concept on 2026-06-09 closed a long-standing 'feasible but unproven' caveat: it sealed a secret under a TPM PolicyPCR (the TPM is the Trusted Platform Module, a dedicated security chip; PCRs are Platform Configuration Registers recording the machine's boot state) and watched the silicon refuse to unseal when the bound PCR value differed — measured-boot key-binding enforces on this exact chip, it does not merely decorate. The natural reflex on a green capability proof is to reach for it. The operator did the opposite: on 2026-06-10 he declined to PCR-bind the production trust-root keys. PCR values move on every legitimate firmware and OS update, so a PCR-bound trust root would brick itself after a routine BIOS or Windows update until a recovery ceremony re-sealed it — and the threat bought down is physical, boot-local, and already mitigated in depth by non-exportable, chip-bound keys on an air-gapped, physically held box.

Evidence: 2026-06-10 — The lock I proved, then declined to fit
Before you turn the page

Your proof-of-concept just went green — which question has that actually answered, and what would deploying it cost on an ordinary day, repeated across years?

Act IVLesson 105core pathunit 62 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Demonstrating a capability is not deciding to deploy it — a proof-of-concept that ends in a deliberate "no" is a success, not a waste.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act IVLesson 105core pathunit 62 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Demonstrating a capability is not deciding to deploy it — a proof-of-concept that ends in a deliberate "no" is a success, not a waste.

The lesson

Demonstrating a capability is not deciding to deploy it. Feasibility and desirability are separate questions, and a proof-of-concept's highest governance value is often the informed negative it enables: 'we proved it works and chose not to use it' is a stronger position than either 'we couldn't' or 'we did it because we could.' Record the decline with the path-not-taken visible — here as ADR-028 Amendment 1.

Growing from it

Proofs convert themselves into deployments through momentum: effort was spent, the demo is green, and 'it works' quietly answers a question nobody asked — should we run it here? Those are different questions with different inputs. The proof answers capability; the deployment decision needs the threat model (what attack does this buy down, and is it already mitigated?), the operational cost (what does this break in normal life — here, every routine firmware update), and the fit with your recovery posture (the strongest lock is poison if you must survive losing the key). When one of your experiments succeeds, treat the green result as the start of the deployment question, not its answer: write down what the capability would cost on an ordinary Tuesday across years of operation, and let a recorded 'no' stand as a finished, successful outcome.

Act IVLesson 105core pathunit 62 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Demonstrating a capability is not deciding to deploy it — a proof-of-concept that ends in a deliberate "no" is a success, not a waste.

The AIGP connection
Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and UseNIST AI RMF — MANAGEEU AI Act — human oversight (high-risk obligation concept)

AIGP Domain IV opens with exactly this competency: evaluating the key factors and risks relevant to the decision to deploy — a capability proof is one input to that evaluation, never its verdict. The decline exemplifies NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function, prioritizing and responding to risk: marginal reduction of an already-mitigated, boot-local physical threat was weighed against bricking the trust root on routine updates, and the documented treatment was 'do not deploy.' The EU AI Act's human-oversight concept shows in who decided: the accountable human, with the evidence in hand.

AIGP takeaway Feasibility and desirability are separate questions; a capability proof's highest value can be the informed, recorded decision not to deploy it.
Act IVLesson 107core pathunit 63 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A "gate-blocking residual" must be reconciled against disk before it drives the plan.

The failure

The #612 deep-dive's residual register named one item as the gate's last technical blocker: A.3, the controlled sandboxed egress exercise (egress: outbound network traffic) — prove the armed egress stack denies by default, auto-trips off its allowlist, and blocks a planted secret, against real non-loopback traffic. The register graded it 'BUILT-DORMANT, gate-blocking, the one remaining proof.' Told to complete it, the agent went to build it and found it already built: tests/security/test_egress_sandbox_proving.py — the #643 work, merged the day before — does exactly that, sits in the standing gate, and passes. Run deliberately as the formal exercise anyway, it produced 11 proofs, 11 passed: real same-host, non-loopback round-trips on the box's own LAN IP, air-gap-safe by construction. The register had drifted — and it had folded a genuinely unfinished item into A.3: the full tool-driven egress chain, un-exercisable until an egress tool exists at all.

Evidence: 2026-06-10 — The gate-blocker that was already green
Before you turn the page

The register says one proof remains before the gate — when was that claim last checked against what the tests on disk actually prove?

Act IVLesson 107core pathunit 63 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A "gate-blocking residual" must be reconciled against disk before it drives the plan.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
11/11egress proofs passed when the register's 'one remaining blocker' was run as the formal exercise
Act IVLesson 107core pathunit 63 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A "gate-blocking residual" must be reconciled against disk before it drives the plan.

The lesson

Decision-support inventories — a residual register, a finish-list, a deep-dive's 'one remaining proof' — drift from the code the moment work lands faster than the document is reconciled. Reconcile the claim against disk before it drives the plan: here that turned 'the gate's last blocker' into 'already done, plus a genuine residual the register had folded into it.' And when document and tree disagree, the drift is itself gate-relevant.

Growing from it

Registers drift because they are snapshots dressed as current truth: each item was accurate when graded, the codebase kept moving, and nothing forces the document to notice a merge. The more momentum a project has, the faster its decision documents rot — and the more authority they carry, because everyone is too busy shipping to re-derive them. Before any inventory item drives your plan, reconcile first: check the system of record — the tree, the test suite, the gate run — for what the item claims is missing. Unbundle composite items while you are there; a stale register often hides a real residual inside an already-met one. And watch for circular prerequisites: a proof that can only run after the decision it supposedly blocks is mis-sequenced, and naming that beats forcing it. When document and disk disagree, the disagreement is a finding in its own right.

Act IVLesson 107core pathunit 63 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A "gate-blocking residual" must be reconciled against disk before it drives the plan.

The AIGP connection
Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and UseNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)EU AI Act — human oversight (high-risk obligation concept)

Gate evidence is assessment, and NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function with TEVV (test, evaluation, verification, and validation) assumes assessments describe the system as it currently is — a residual register that lags the tree feeds the deployment decision stale risk information. Running the existing proof as a formal, recorded exercise re-grounded the evidence, and routing the unsettled scope question — component-level bar versus full chain — to the accountable human rather than self-certifying exemplifies the EU AI Act's human-oversight concept: the overseer decides with reconciled facts.

AIGP takeaway Reconcile decision inventories against the actual system before acting on them; stale registers rebuild green proofs and misdirect gates.
Act IVLesson 115unit 64 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When an ADR rejects an alternative that might come back, *hold* it explicitly

The failure

BlarAI's egress policy — egress meaning outbound network traffic — was ratified as ADR-027, an Architecture Decision Record: the dated document that fixes a design decision with its rejected alternatives on the record. Rule 1: named endpoints, one vetted at a time. But ADR-027 had explicitly held its hybrid per-action-consent refinement 'if a feature warrants it (e.g. fetching arbitrary user-directed URLs).' Three days later the exact feature the parenthetical predicted showed up: the UC-002/003 ingest program needed an arbitrary pasted news URL to clear an egress door built for named endpoints, and a re-litigation of the whole posture loomed. Instead, adopting the held option took one amendment — ADR-027 Amendment 1: the paste is the consent for that ONE URL, the Policy Agent adjudicates a new INGEST_FETCH action class per fetch and logs it, GET-only with no user-content echo outbound, kill-switch and exfil screen untouched.

Evidence: 2026-06-10 — The option we held three days ago found its feature · commits 63f3736, df51b8d
Before you turn the page

Of the alternatives your decision record rejects, which are dead on principle and which are merely dormant on current facts — and where did you write the condition that would revive the dormant ones?

Act IVLesson 115unit 64 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When an ADR rejects an alternative that might come back, *hold* it explicitly

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
3 daysfrom the option held in ADR-027 to the predicted feature arriving1 amendmenttotal cost of the posture change, reasoning pre-built
Act IVLesson 115unit 64 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When an ADR rejects an alternative that might come back, *hold* it explicitly

The lesson

When an Architecture Decision Record rejects an alternative that might come back, hold it explicitly — name the option and the condition that would warrant it. The held option carried its reasoning pre-built, so when the predicted feature arrived the posture change cost one amendment instead of a fresh debate over a policy that looked closed. A held option is a designed seam in the decision record, the way a private method is a designed seam in code.

Growing from it

Decision records ossify by design: once ratified, reopening one reads as relitigating, so teams either avoid the reopening — and the policy quietly blocks a legitimate feature — or bypass the record, and the policy silently stops describing reality. A held option is a pre-authorized hinge. Naming the condition at ratification time separates 'is this change legitimate?' (answered in advance) from 'has the condition arrived?' (cheap to check on the day). When you write any decision — an ADR, a policy, a contract — sort the rejected alternatives into dead, rejected on principle, and dormant, rejected on current facts; for each dormant one, write the condition that would revive it. Future-you inherits an amendment path instead of a credibility fight. One caution from the same arc: held options drift like everything else when sessions draft in parallel — a reconciliation pass must update the preconditions ratified under the old mechanism, not just the mechanism.

Act IVLesson 115unit 64 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When an ADR rejects an alternative that might come back, *hold* it explicitly

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceISO/IEC 42001 — AIMS plan-do-check-actNIST AI RMF — GOVERN

ISO/IEC 42001 builds AI governance as a management system on the plan-do-check-act pattern — policies that are expected to be revisited and improved, not carved once. A held option with a named reviving condition is that pattern designed into a single decision record: the check is cheap because the criteria were written at ratification. NIST AI RMF's GOVERN function, which owns policies and accountability structures, is exercised here as Domain I.C describes — establishing policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle that can evolve without losing their authority.

AIGP takeaway Hold revivable alternatives in the record with their reviving condition; policy then evolves by amendment, not re-litigation.
Act V of 8 · 32 lessons · lessons 13–121 (thematic, not contiguous) · 8 on the core path

Fail-closed — security as posture, not feature

Safe-state defaults, controls that actually fire, and the gap between built and armed

Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentDomain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and UseDomain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance

Security is this project's center of gravity, and this act trains its core posture: fail-closed — default to the safe state, refuse to start rather than run weak, and prove every control armed, precise, and recoverable. The recurring failure shape is the gap between built and armed: mechanisms proven but never invoked, key material never issued, defaults that silently downgrade, deny rules that would starve the system they protect. In AIGP terms this is the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety lived end to end; NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function (risk treatment carried to completion) and MEASURE function with TEVV (test, evaluation, verification, and validation); ISO/IEC 42001's discipline of controls that operate, not merely exist; and Body of Knowledge Domains III and IV, with one excursion into Domain I.

Act VLesson 13core pathunit 65 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Provenance is not trust: your own stored data can still carry an attack.

The failure

Building the Substrate — BlarAI's persistent semantic memory, which chunks loaded documents and approved conversation turns into an on-disk vector store and retrieves them by meaning on every new prompt — the easy assumption sat right there: this content is the user's own, so it can be waved through as trusted. The journal's design entry names why that is wrong in a way that matters. A document loaded a week ago may have carried a prompt injection (text crafted so a model reads it as instructions) that nobody noticed at the time; a past turn may quote something hostile the user pasted from a page. Stored-by-us is not authored-by-us, and even authored-by-us is not safe-to-obey. So every retrieved chunk runs through the exact Layer 1+2 defences a freshly loaded document gets — forged-delimiter neutralisation and per-load datamarking — and is grounded as data, never as instruction.

Evidence: 2026-06-02 — Phase 5: BlarAI remembers now
Before you turn the page

If a poisoned document was stored a week ago and retrieved today, which of your defences actually run on it today?

Act VLesson 13core pathunit 65 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Provenance is not trust: your own stored data can still carry an attack.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VLesson 13core pathunit 65 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Provenance is not trust: your own stored data can still carry an attack.

The lesson

The provenance of data — where it came from, who stored it — is independent of its trustworthiness — whether it is safe to act on. A system that conflates the two builds a laundering path: yesterday's unnoticed injection becomes today's trusted instruction. Retrieved memory therefore gets the same defences fresh input gets, and is grounded as data, never obeyed as instruction, no matter that it came from the system's own database.

Growing from it

The mechanism underneath is a trust shortcut: 'inside the boundary' quietly becomes 'safe,' because the check you remember running was the one at the door. But storage launders origin — once content sits in your own database, nothing about its bytes records that it arrived from a hostile page, and a system that trusts its own store re-delivers the attack with the store's authority attached. Watch for any pipeline where data crosses from external to internal and the security treatment drops away after the crossing: caches, message queues, memory systems, data lakes. Ask of every consumer, not just every ingress: what happens if this record is malicious? If the answer is 'it cannot be, it is ours,' you have found the laundering path. Apply the same defences at retrieval that you apply at ingestion, because retrieval is just ingestion from your past.

Act VLesson 13core pathunit 65 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Provenance is not trust: your own stored data can still carry an attack.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentOECD AI Principles — robustness, security and safetyEU AI Act — data governance (high-risk obligation concept)

This is data governance in the AIGP Body of Knowledge's sense — Domain III's competency for governing the collection and use of data extends to data the system re-consumes from its own stores. The lived near-miss maps to the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety: a system is robust only if content cannot escalate from data to instruction by virtue of where it is stored. It also exemplifies the data-governance obligation concept the EU AI Act attaches to high-risk systems: provenance tracking helps only if trust decisions do not collapse into it.

AIGP takeaway Provenance and trustworthiness are independent properties; apply ingestion-grade defences at every retrieval, including from your own store.
Act VLesson 18unit 66 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A security design is only as real as the hardware it assumes — verify the silicon, not the spec sheet or your own inference.

The failure

BlarAI's trust root was specified for months on Intel SGX: the canonical use-case document had the Policy Agent completing 'SGX local attestation' before deriving its certificate-authority key. But Intel removed SGX from its client CPUs in 2021, and the target Lunar Lake laptop has none — the design described hardware that was never there, and no code had ever run it. Querying the actual device replaced the phantom: tpmtool reported a TPM 2.0 (Trusted Platform Module, a dedicated security chip), manufacturer STMicroelectronics. Two smaller versions of the same error bracketed the rebuild. The agent declared the chip 'not Pluton' from that one manufacturer string and was corrected by the machine's owner — Pluton is present, just not serving as the active TPM. And the signing primitive's NCryptDeleteKey flag was wrong in a way no documentation reading revealed; only the real chip returning NTE_BAD_FLAGS did.

Evidence: 2026-06-03 — A trust root built on hardware nobody had checked
Before you turn the page

What evidence do you actually hold that the security hardware your design names exists on the machine that will run it?

Act VLesson 18unit 66 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A security design is only as real as the hardware it assumes — verify the silicon, not the spec sheet or your own inference.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VLesson 18unit 66 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A security design is only as real as the hardware it assumes — verify the silicon, not the spec sheet or your own inference.

The lesson

Spec sheets, inherited designs, and API documentation describe an intended device; a trust root must be built on the measured one. Query the silicon — tpmtool, Device Manager, a key round-trip on the actual chip — before writing a line of trust code. And when the hardware's owner says your description is wrong, believe the owner over your inference.

Growing from it

Designs rot silently because the document and the hardware version on different schedules: the spec was written against a product line that later changed, and nothing forces the paper to notice. The failure mechanism is inheritance without re-verification — each reader assumes the writer checked, so a claim that was never true anywhere can stay load-bearing for months. You prevent it by treating every hardware assumption in a security design as a claim with an expiry date: before building on it, run one cheap probe on the real device — list its capabilities, exercise the exact API call, round-trip a key. The probe costs hours; building a trust chain on absent silicon costs the chain. And calibrate your evidence sources: a single tool's output string is weaker than the device owner's direct knowledge plus a second instrument, and a returned error code from the real chip outranks any manual.

Act VLesson 18unit 66 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A security design is only as real as the hardware it assumes — verify the silicon, not the spec sheet or your own inference.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MAPOECD AI Principles — robustness, security and safety

NIST AI RMF's MAP function asks an organization to establish context — the system, its capabilities, and its deployment environment — before risks can be measured or managed; a trust root specified for hardware the deployment environment does not contain is a MAP failure in miniature. The correction — characterize the real device, then re-derive the design, recorded in ADR-018 — exemplifies the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety: security properties must hold on the system as deployed, not as imagined.

AIGP takeaway Verify security hardware on the actual device before designing on it; a spec-sheet trust root can be a phantom.
Act VLesson 22core pathunit 67 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Fail-closed is not fail-shut: a deny-by-default control must still enumerate — and prove — the legitimate-allow set.

The failure

BlarAI's 'no external network' guarantee was never enforced in code; it held only because the machine was air-gapped and the source happened not to call out — both of which evaporate the day the system faces the internet. Turning the habit into an egress kill-switch (a control that blocks outbound network traffic), the handoff brief framed it as 'refuse any outbound socket' — and that version would have bricked the app on launch, because the runtime's own inter-process communication is sockets: the Hyper-V vsock channel to the virtual machine, the dev-mode loopback TCP substitute, the launcher's 127.0.0.1:5001 handshake. The operator caught it before it shipped. The control became an allowlist — permit loopback and Hyper-V, deny everything else — and sixteen tests assert both halves: external connects, binds, DNS lookups, and UDP sends refused; a real loopback round-trip, the socketpair the event loop is built on, and loopback DNS still working.

Evidence: 2026-06-03 — Fail-closed is not fail-shut: the egress switch that had to stay open
Before you turn the page

Your new deny-by-default control blocks everything it should — what proves it still permits everything the system itself needs to run?

Act VLesson 22core pathunit 67 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Fail-closed is not fail-shut: a deny-by-default control must still enumerate — and prove — the legitimate-allow set.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
16 testsasserting both halves at once
Act VLesson 22core pathunit 67 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Fail-closed is not fail-shut: a deny-by-default control must still enumerate — and prove — the legitimate-allow set.

The lesson

A deny-by-default control is only safe if the legitimate-allow set is enumerated and proven. The test must assert the positive path — the legitimate channels still pass — as rigorously as the negative one, because a fail-closed control whose allow-set nobody verified is one launch away from a self-inflicted outage that no headless test would catch.

Growing from it

Deny-by-default feels finished the moment the deny works, because denying is the part with the security story attached — and that bias is exactly what produces self-inflicted outages. The mechanism: the author's attention follows the threat model, so the allow-set gets enumerated from memory rather than from the system's real traffic, and the first launch discovers a legitimate channel nobody listed. Prevent it by inventorying actual legitimate flows before writing the rule — read the code that opens connections, and trace where the control sits relative to wrapping layers (this guard had to bite at the raw socket's connect and bind, before the TLS wrap) — then write the positive-path tests first. A useful self-check for any restrictive control you ship: can you name everything it must still allow, and does a test fail if one of those stops working?

Act VLesson 22core pathunit 67 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Fail-closed is not fail-shut: a deny-by-default control must still enumerate — and prove — the legitimate-allow set.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)OECD AI Principles — robustness, security and safety

NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function is the home of TEVV — test, evaluation, verification, and validation — and this lesson is TEVV applied to a security control itself: verifying the deny fires is half the work; validating that the system still does its real job under the control is the other half. The arc also exemplifies the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety, where safety includes the system's own availability: a control that bricks the system it protects is not a safe state.

AIGP takeaway Deny-by-default requires a proven allow-set: test the positive path as rigorously as the deny, or the control becomes the outage.
Act VLesson 23unit 68 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A capability proven is not a control enforced — a finding closes when production points at the strong path and the weak one is gone.

The failure

The TPM signing mechanism — the Policy Agent minting its JSON Web Tokens with a non-exportable key inside the TPM (Trusted Platform Module, a dedicated security chip) — was built and proven on the real silicon a full increment before the audit's single Critical finding actually closed. In between, nothing an auditor would credit had changed: the config still named the cleartext key, the key still sat in git, and a software-key fallback still existed to silently regress to. Closing the finding was the unglamorous half: repoint the production entrypoint from from_key_file to from_tpm, move the three validators to the chip's exported public key, delete certs/pa_private.pem by git rm (history kept at 95c4f19 — the rotation makes the old key worthless, so no destructive rewrite was needed), and remove the fallback so preflight is fail-closed-until-provisioned with nothing weaker left to choose.

Evidence: 2026-06-03 — Closing the Critical: the signing key the model trusts is no longer in git · commit 23b2802
Before you turn the page

The strong mechanism works — but if you audited production today, which path would the config actually choose, and what could it still fall back to?

Act VLesson 23unit 68 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A capability proven is not a control enforced — a finding closes when production points at the strong path and the weak one is gone.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VLesson 23unit 68 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A capability proven is not a control enforced — a finding closes when production points at the strong path and the weak one is gone.

The lesson

Building the strong path is the satisfying half; a security finding stays open until production points at the strong path and the weak one is gone — config repointed, the old secret removed, the fallback deleted. Two governance calls ride with it: key provisioning is a deliberate, witnessed human ceremony, and a machine-bound public key is documented by its fingerprint, not committed as one chip's bytes.

Growing from it

The mechanism behind half-closed findings is that engineering effort and risk reduction peak at different moments: the interesting work is the new mechanism, but the risk only moves when the old path stops being reachable. Between those two moments the system has both paths, and 'both' means an attacker — or a careless config — still gets the weak one. Train yourself to define closure from the auditor's chair before you start: what evidence would convince a skeptic that the weak path is unreachable? Usually three concrete artifacts — production configuration naming only the strong path, the old credential gone or worthless, and a test that fails if a fallback reappears. Then sequence the unglamorous rotation work as part of the same deliverable, not a follow-up ticket, because follow-up tickets are where weak paths retire to live forever.

Act VLesson 23unit 68 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A capability proven is not a control enforced — a finding closes when production points at the strong path and the weak one is gone.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MANAGEOECD AI Principles — accountability

NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function covers prioritizing and responding to risks — treatment plans and their implementation — and this lesson is the difference between a treatment designed and a treatment implemented: residual risk is unchanged until the weak path is gone. The witnessed provisioning ceremony and fingerprint-not-key documentation exemplify the accountability the OECD principles ask for: a human can say who created the trust anchor, when, and how to re-verify it.

AIGP takeaway A finding closes when production uses the strong path and the weak one is unreachable — mechanism-built is not risk-moved.
Act VLesson 34unit 69 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A prefix or allow/deny check on a path you did not canonicalize is a bypass waiting to be typed.

The failure

The Policy Agent's deterministic gate denied restricted file paths with startswith on the raw resource string — deny /etc/, another user's /home/, /internal/. The #5 quality benchmark found the hole: a request for /home/user/../../etc/passwd walked straight through, because the string literally starts with the allowed /home/user/ and does not start with /etc/ — yet the .. segments ('go up one directory') resolve it to /etc/passwd, the very file the rule exists to deny. The deterministic gate returned None and handed the decision to the LLM (large language model) classifier — exactly the layer the pre-filter exists not to depend on. The fix normalized with posixpath, not os.path — which on the Windows host would rewrite / to \ and break the very check being hardened — and checks BOTH the raw and normalized forms so the hardening is provably additive. Four traversal tests were proven red against the pre-fix source.

Evidence: 2026-06-04 — The bypass that started with the allowed prefix
Before you turn the page

Your check denies the string '/etc/' — what does the system that finally opens the file do to that string after your check has passed it?

Act VLesson 34unit 69 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A prefix or allow/deny check on a path you did not canonicalize is a bypass waiting to be typed.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VLesson 34unit 69 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A prefix or allow/deny check on a path you did not canonicalize is a bypass waiting to be typed.

The lesson

The string a caller types and the file the kernel opens are different objects; a security comparison must run on the canonical form, not the typed one. Normalize an input to the grammar it was written in, and prefer an additive fix — check both raw and normalized forms — over a tidier rewrite that might open a new hole while closing this one.

Growing from it

Path-traversal bypasses keep recurring because string checks feel like file checks: the rule reads as 'deny /etc/' but executes as 'deny strings beginning with those five characters,' and those are different predicates the moment the input language allows aliases — .. segments, doubled slashes, symlinks, percent-encoding, case folding. Any time you compare attacker-influenced input against an allow or deny list, ask what transformations the consumer applies after your check, because every one of them is an alias an attacker can type. Canonicalize first, compare second — and canonicalize in the input's own grammar, since normalizing POSIX paths with Windows rules silently corrupts the comparison. Then prove the fix with adversarial cases run red against the pre-fix code, plus regression cases showing the old catches still catch: a hardening you cannot show is additive may be a hole you just relocated.

Act VLesson 34unit 69 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A prefix or allow/deny check on a path you did not canonicalize is a bypass waiting to be typed.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)OECD AI Principles — robustness, security and safety

The benchmark that surfaced this — an adversarial corpus run against the gate — is what NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function and its TEVV discipline (test, evaluation, verification, and validation) ask for: probing a control with the inputs an attacker would choose, not the inputs the author imagined. The bypass itself is a robustness failure in the OECD sense of robustness, security and safety: a guardrail that holds only for well-formed input is not a guardrail. The additive raw-plus-normalized fix models risk treatment that cannot regress what it hardens.

AIGP takeaway Canonicalize before you compare: a prefix check on a raw path is theatre, and an adversarial benchmark is what exposes it.
Act VLesson 36core pathunit 70 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

An over-eager control is a regression wearing a security badge — re-enabling a disabled control is not automatically safe.

The failure

The 2026-06-03 security audit said 'wire the inert leakage detector,' and the Tier-1 wave did, with passing tests. Hours later the live screen earned its keep: the operator loaded two documents, asked for a summary, watched an accurate summary stream onto the screen — and then watched it vanish, replaced by a yellow box reading 'Response held by the output validator: LEAKAGE_DETECTED.' Nothing had leaked. The detector measures cosine similarity (a closeness score between texts) between the model's output and the grounded content, flagging matches above 0.85 as verbatim leakage — but a summary of a document is highly similar to that document by construction, so the control fires on exactly the cases the assistant exists to serve. The mocked tests had verified the wiring, not the behaviour; only the real model with real documents caught it. The fix was a revert to inert: off-and-redesign beats on-and-wrong.

Evidence: 2026-06-04 — The detector that flagged the assistant for doing its job
Before you turn the page

If this control were perfectly enforced tomorrow, what fraction of the system's normal, correct behaviour would it block?

Act VLesson 36core pathunit 70 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

An over-eager control is a regression wearing a security badge — re-enabling a disabled control is not automatically safe.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
0.85similarity threshold that flagged summaries
Act VLesson 36core pathunit 70 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

An over-eager control is a regression wearing a security badge — re-enabling a disabled control is not automatically safe.

The lesson

Before enabling a dormant control, verify its precision against the system doing its normal job, not just its presence — 'turn on the thing that ships off' is a claim to be tested, not a safe default. A control that suppresses correct behaviour is a regression wearing a security badge, and off-and-correct beats on-and-wrong.

Growing from it

The mechanism is a category error: enabling a control feels like restoring intended behaviour, so the only test anyone writes is 'does it now run' — but a detector has two failure modes, and the audit finding only named one. Misses are the security failure; false positives are a product failure that arrives wearing the security team's badge, which makes it politically harder to roll back. Whenever you activate something dormant — a detector, a stricter threshold, an old feature flag — treat it as shipping a brand-new control: measure its false-positive rate on the system's normal, legitimate workload before enforcement, and stage it observe-only first if you can. And notice the deeper design question a high false-positive rate points at: this detector could not distinguish 'answered from the content you gave it' from 'leaked content you did not ask for' — no threshold fixes a control measuring the wrong property.

Act VLesson 36core pathunit 70 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

An over-eager control is a regression wearing a security badge — re-enabling a disabled control is not automatically safe.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)NIST AI RMF — valid and reliable (trustworthy-AI characteristic)

This is the verification-versus-validation distinction inside NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function and its TEVV discipline (test, evaluation, verification, and validation): the wiring was verified — built to spec, the detector runs — but never validated — does the right thing in the real context, where it flags the assistant's core job. The NIST trustworthy-AI characteristic 'valid and reliable' applies to controls as much as models: a control wrong on normal inputs degrades the system it guards, which the OECD robustness, security and safety principle counts as a safety failure.

AIGP takeaway Validate a control's precision on normal workload before enforcing it; re-enabling a dormant control is shipping a new one.
Act VLesson 44core pathunit 71 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A safety control that depends on a human remembering to do the right thing is not a control.

The failure

dev-mode — the relaxed posture with ephemeral keys and no hardware trust — shipped as BlarAI's silent default. Every test run and every 'BlarAI works' report therefore validated a configuration that would never ship: green on dev-mode while the production posture failed, which is why agents kept reporting success the operator could not reproduce at his own screen. The Sprint 13 decision walkthrough named the shape: the only thing standing between the project and a false green was someone remembering to test in production posture — and the operator does not run the tests. The first structural fix landed in the same wave (commit ea879ed): the silent default became loud — a boot banner nobody can miss — plus a fail-closed interlock that refuses to run dev-mode while network-facing, built ahead of the risk it guards, with the secure-by-default flip itself staged for the later increment that could land it without breaking the daily launch.

Evidence: 2026-06-05 — The anti-pattern the sprint was built to kill, caught inside the sprint · commit ea879ed
Before you turn the page

Walk through your verification pipeline: at which step would anything fail if everyone simply forgot the rule existed?

Act VLesson 44core pathunit 71 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A safety control that depends on a human remembering to do the right thing is not a control.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VLesson 44core pathunit 71 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A safety control that depends on a human remembering to do the right thing is not a control.

The lesson

If the only thing between you and a false green is someone's memory, you have no control. The fix cannot be 'remember to test in production posture'; it must be structural — make the production posture the one the suite verifies by default, and make the wrong posture fail loudly where automation sees it.

Growing from it

Memory-dependent controls fail by a predictable mechanism: the person who knows the rule is not the person at the keyboard when it matters, and nothing in the system carries the rule across that gap. Defaults are the strongest policy instrument you have, because the default is what every actor who never thought about the question gets — so when a weak posture is the default, every routine action silently votes for it, and the evidence stream (green tests, success reports) describes a system that will never exist in production. Audit your own projects for verbs like 'remember to,' 'make sure you,' 'don't forget' in runbooks and review checklists; each one is a control specification waiting to be made structural. Move the rule into the default, the automated gate, or the refuse-to-start check — places that fire without anyone recalling them.

Act VLesson 44core pathunit 71 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A safety control that depends on a human remembering to do the right thing is not a control.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceNIST AI RMF — GOVERNISO/IEC 42001 — AIMS (plan-do-check-act management-system discipline)

AIGP Domain I's competency on establishing policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle is about exactly this: a policy that lives in human memory is not yet a procedure. The structural fix — secure default, loud banner, fail-closed interlock — exemplifies NIST AI RMF's GOVERN function, which seats risk management in organizational structures and processes rather than individual diligence, and ISO/IEC 42001's management-system logic, where controls are operated and checked on the plan-do-check-act cycle instead of recalled.

AIGP takeaway A control that depends on someone remembering is no control; encode it as a default, gate, or refuse-to-start check.
Act VLesson 47unit 72 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A comment that claims a property the code does not enforce is governance debt that surfaces later as an audit finding.

The failure

The PII (personally identifiable information) credit-card detector's pattern carried the comment '(Luhn-plausible)' — implying the mod-10 checksum that distinguishes plausible card numbers from arbitrary digit runs. The code ran no checksum at all: it matched any 13-to-19-digit run, so order numbers and account IDs would have been redacted as card numbers the moment the redact-at-egress posture switched on. The gap sat between the comment's claim and the code's behaviour — exactly where false confidence lives, because a reader trusts a validation that is not there until an auditor finds it. Sprint 13's first execution agent made the comment true (commit d910739): a real mod-10 gate on both the block and redact paths, a teeth-test reconstructing the exact order-number false positive the old code produced, and the validator registered in a named per-label table so 'what validation actually runs' is visible code, not a parenthetical promise.

Evidence: 2026-06-05 — The anti-pattern the sprint was built to kill, caught inside the sprint · commit d910739
Before you turn the page

Pick any property your code's comments claim — what would actually fail today if that property quietly stopped being true?

Act VLesson 47unit 72 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A comment that claims a property the code does not enforce is governance debt that surfaces later as an audit finding.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VLesson 47unit 72 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A comment that claims a property the code does not enforce is governance debt that surfaces later as an audit finding.

The lesson

A comment that claims a property the code does not enforce is governance debt: a reader trusts a validation that is not there, and an auditor eventually finds the gap. Encode the secondary gate a detector needs as something the reader can watch fail — a registered validator, a test — not as a parenthetical promise.

Growing from it

Comments drift from code because nothing executes them: the interpreter checks the pattern, the suite checks the behaviour, and the claim in the parenthesis is checked by nobody until an audit reads it against the implementation. The deeper mechanism is that documentation often records intent at design time — 'this should be Luhn-gated' — and intent quietly hardens into assertion as readers inherit it. When you review code, treat every property claim in a comment, docstring, or design document as a test that has not been written yet: either the property is enforced somewhere you can point to, or the claim should be deleted or downgraded to a TODO. The same discipline pays at system scale — security postures described in architecture documents deserve a verification trail, because the documents are what assessors, auditors, and your future self will read first and believe.

Act VLesson 47unit 72 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A comment that claims a property the code does not enforce is governance debt that surfaces later as an audit finding.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentEU AI Act — technical documentation (high-risk obligation concept)NIST AI RMF — accountable and transparent (trustworthy-AI characteristic)

The EU AI Act's high-risk obligations include technical documentation that accurately describes the system, and the NIST AI RMF trustworthy-AI characteristic 'accountable and transparent' depends on artifacts that say what the system does, not what its authors intended. A comment claiming an unenforced validation is the small-scale failure of both: documentation an assessor would rely on and be misled by. Making the claim executable — a real checksum, a registered validator, a test — turns the documentation back into evidence.

AIGP takeaway Documentation that claims an unenforced property is misleading evidence; make the claim executable or remove it.
Act VLesson 50unit 73 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Encrypt the derived representations, not just the raw text — a stored embedding is an invertible shadow of its source.

The failure

Wiring the AES-256-GCM field cipher (authenticated encryption) into the substrate store, the first instinct was to treat embeddings — the numeric vectors that represent text by meaning — as non-sensitive metadata; they are not human-readable, after all. The operator's kickoff decision was sharper: embeddings are invertible. The vec2text literature shows a 384-dimensional semantic embedding can reconstruct enough of its source text to be a real privacy leak from a stolen disk — encrypting the house while leaving the blueprint in the mailbox. The cost objection dissolved on measurement: decrypt all embeddings once at unlock into an in-RAM matrix, run vector search over plaintext in memory, decrypt only the top-k matched text per query — measured at +0.124 ms per query (0.397 to 0.521 ms median) and 1.45 ms one-time at boot for 107 embeddings, below measurement noise against time-to-first-token on this hardware.

Evidence: 2026-06-06 — Encrypt the derived representations, not just the raw text · commit de78cb2
Before you turn the page

List everything your system derives from its most sensitive data — which of those artifacts could reconstruct the source, and are they protected like it?

Act VLesson 50unit 73 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Encrypt the derived representations, not just the raw text — a stored embedding is an invertible shadow of its source.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
0.397 ms→0.521 msmedian query under encryption1.45 msone-time boot decrypt, 107 embeddings
Act VLesson 50unit 73 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Encrypt the derived representations, not just the raw text — a stored embedding is an invertible shadow of its source.

The lesson

A derived representation can carry the sensitivity of its source: a stored embedding is an invertible shadow of the text it encodes, so encrypting the text while leaving the vectors plaintext is an internally inconsistent posture. Price the protection before rejecting it — here a boot-time cache made the overhead immaterial at single-user scale, paid in proportion to corpus size, not per query.

Growing from it

The mechanism is an intuition mismatch: 'sensitive' gets read as 'human-readable,' so anything that looks like opaque numbers feels safe. But sensitivity follows information content, not legibility — embeddings, search indexes, caches, logs of prompts, even similarity scores can each reconstruct or reveal slices of the data they were derived from. When you classify what needs protection, trace the data-flow graph downstream from every sensitive source and ask of each derived artifact: with this artifact alone plus public tools, what could a motivated attacker recover? If the answer is 'some of the source,' it inherits the source's protection class. Then do the engineering honestly: measure the cost of protecting it rather than assuming it is prohibitive, because the assumed-expensive control is often cheap once you choose where to pay — at boot rather than per query, here.

Act VLesson 50unit 73 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Encrypt the derived representations, not just the raw text — a stored embedding is an invertible shadow of its source.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — privacy-enhanced (trustworthy-AI characteristic)NIST AI RMF — MANAGE

This is the NIST AI RMF trustworthy-AI characteristic 'privacy-enhanced' applied with technical depth: privacy protection that covers raw text but not its derived representations is incomplete by the data's own information content. It also exemplifies AIGP Domain III's data-governance competency — governing the use of data includes the artifacts the system manufactures from it — and the accepted, documented residual (identical filenames share a keyed hash, the price of deduplication) models the honest residual-risk naming NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function expects.

AIGP takeaway Derived representations inherit their source's sensitivity: an embedding can invert to its text, so encrypt the shadow with the substance.
Act VLesson 51unit 74 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Tamper-evident is not non-forgeable, and the difference belongs in a test, not a comment.

The failure

Sprint 13 shipped a hash-chained audit stream for Policy-Agent decisions — tamper-evident by construction — with the signer deliberately left a software HMAC stub whose key was recomputable from filesystem state. The hash chain proves records were not altered after the fact; only a signature proves who wrote them — and the system had only the first property while a comment implied the second. The gap closed with a TPM-sealed (Trusted Platform Module, a dedicated security chip) ECDSA signer on a dedicated key, BlarAI-Audit-Signing-Key-v1, deliberately separated from the Policy Agent's JWT (JSON Web Token) key so an audit compromise does not also compromise token issuance. The portfolio-valuable part is the contrast test: one test actually forges a record and asserts the weak stub accepts it; its mirror asserts an adversary holding only the key name cannot produce a signature the TPM signer accepts. The follow-up hardened the failure branches: an unprovisioned key now refuses to start rather than authorizing with no audit trail.

Evidence: 2026-06-05 — Closing the door between tamper-evident and non-forgeable — then refusing to run blind · commit 871ced7 · commit 4c71aad
Before you turn the page

Your audit log is 'protected' — protected against alteration, against forgery, or both, and which test would tell you?

Act VLesson 51unit 74 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Tamper-evident is not non-forgeable, and the difference belongs in a test, not a comment.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VLesson 51unit 74 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Tamper-evident is not non-forgeable, and the difference belongs in a test, not a comment.

The lesson

Tamper-evident and non-forgeable are different properties — a hash chain proves records were not altered afterward; only a signature proves who wrote them — and a system can hold one while a comment implies both. Make the security property executable: write the test that forges against the weak path and the mirror that fails to forge against the strong one.

Growing from it

Security properties blur together because their everyday descriptions overlap — 'the log is protected' covers integrity, authenticity, and availability, three different guarantees with different mechanisms and different attackers. The failure mechanism here was a deliberate stub plus an ambiguous description: the staged plan was sound, but the words around it claimed the destination while the code stood at the waypoint. When you build security in stages, name precisely which property each stage delivers, and prove the boundary in both directions: a test demonstrating what the current stage still permits (the stub accepts a forged record) is as valuable as the test of what it prevents, because it stops anyone — including you in six months — from believing the stronger claim early. A second habit worth stealing: separate keys by duty, so compromising one control never silently compromises its neighbour.

Act VLesson 51unit 74 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Tamper-evident is not non-forgeable, and the difference belongs in a test, not a comment.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentEU AI Act — record-keeping/logging (high-risk obligation concept)NIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)

Record-keeping and logging are among the EU AI Act's named high-risk obligation concepts precisely because the audit trail is what accountability rests on — and an audit trail that is tamper-evident but forgeable gives accountability with an asterisk an adversary can exploit. The contrast tests are NIST AI RMF MEASURE-function TEVV (test, evaluation, verification, and validation) applied to the control itself: the claimed security property is demonstrated in code, in both its weak and strong states, rather than asserted in prose.

AIGP takeaway Integrity and authenticity are separate guarantees: hash chains prove no alteration; signatures prove authorship. Test the difference, both directions.
Act VLesson 53core pathunit 75 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A break-glass recovery path must be correct by design and proven against a dead chip, never correct by accident.

The failure

The DEK (data encryption key) recovery flow — unseal the key with the offline recovery key when the TPM (Trusted Platform Module) security chip is gone, then re-seal to a new machine — passed its first review green. But it passed by accident: the dev sealer stub happened to raise an exception the code happened to catch, and the real recovery logic leaned on a private helper, _recovery_wrap, that no public, supported entry point exercised. The merge gate sent it back. Round two made the path correct by construction: public unseal_via_recovery and reseal_dek entry points, and a DeadSealer test fixture whose unseal() always raises — driving the recovery code exactly as a genuinely destroyed TPM would, not as a stub that incidentally errors. The tests now assert both ends of the contract: the wrong-recovery-key case fails closed, and the dead-chip-with-correct-key case succeeds end-to-end into a re-sealed keystore.

Evidence: 2026-06-06 — One command for the whole encryption spine — and a recovery path that was right only by accident · commit 6017e5c
Before you turn the page

If your hardware key store died tonight, has the exact sequence the recovery runbook prescribes ever been executed end-to-end — against a chip that is actually dead?

Act VLesson 53core pathunit 75 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A break-glass recovery path must be correct by design and proven against a dead chip, never correct by accident.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VLesson 53core pathunit 75 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A break-glass recovery path must be correct by design and proven against a dead chip, never correct by accident.

The lesson

A break-glass path is the one path you cannot rehearse in production and the one you most need to work the first time, so 'passes today' is not the bar — correct by construction, proven against a simulated dead chip, is. Assert both ends: the wrong key fails closed, and the genuine catastrophe with the right key recovers real data.

Growing from it

Recovery paths rot because nothing exercises them: the happy path runs daily and self-verifies; the catastrophe path runs only on the day everything else has already failed, in front of the person least equipped to debug it. 'Correct by accident' is the signature failure — a stub's incidental exception happens to route execution the right way, so the green test certifies a coincidence. When you build any disaster control — key recovery, backup restore, failover — construct a fixture that IS the disaster (a sealer that always raises, a region that is simply gone) rather than a mock that imitates a symptom, and drive the path through its public, supported entry points, because the private helper your test reaches into is not the code a panicked operator will run. Then keep the rehearsal alive: a recovery proven once and never re-run decays with every refactor.

Act VLesson 53core pathunit 75 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A break-glass recovery path must be correct by design and proven against a dead chip, never correct by accident.

The AIGP connection
Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and UseNIST AI RMF — MANAGENIST AI RMF — secure and resilient (trustworthy-AI characteristic)
