NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function includes planning and implementing responses to risks — and a recovery plan whose enabling path has never been driven as the catastrophe would drive it is a treatment that exists on paper. The trustworthy-AI characteristic 'secure and resilient' makes resilience a tested property: the system's ability to recover from a dead trust anchor is demonstrated — fail-closed on the wrong key, successful on the right one — not presumed from a green suite.

AIGP takeaway Prove break-glass recovery against a simulated dead chip through public entry points; a green test can certify a coincidence.
Act VLesson 54core pathunit 76 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A factory that reads missing configuration as "dev mode" turns a misconfiguration into a silent downgrade to the weakest posture.

The failure

The independent sprint audit's MINOR-3 finding named a latent failure in the at-rest encryption: the store factories' dev path was not an explicit opt-in but the implicit default for any misconfiguration. With the BLARAI_DEK_KEYSTORE environment variable absent — the pointer to the DEK (data encryption key) keystore — both the session and substrate builders would quietly construct a SoftwareSealer store encrypting under the hard-coded key b"SOFTWARE-SEALER-NOT-A-SECRET-KEY": effectively-plaintext data that looks encrypted. No data was exposed on the live system, because the production boot had set the variable — but the posture was 'missing config reads as dev mode' rather than 'missing config is a misconfiguration,' one accidental env-var drop from a production boot that writes pseudo-plaintext while appearing to encrypt. The fix: the load-bearing session store now refuses the whole boot with StoreProvisioningError; the non-load-bearing substrate degrades loudly with memory disabled; the dev path requires an explicit dev_mode=True from the caller.

Evidence: 2026-06-05 — Closing the silent key-substitution door · commit e16450e
Before you turn the page

If the one environment variable your encryption depends on were silently dropped, would the next boot refuse — or run, looking encrypted?

Act VLesson 54core pathunit 76 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A factory that reads missing configuration as "dev mode" turns a misconfiguration into a silent downgrade to the weakest posture.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VLesson 54core pathunit 76 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A factory that reads missing configuration as "dev mode" turns a misconfiguration into a silent downgrade to the weakest posture.

The lesson

Make the safe posture the enforced default and require an explicit caller signal to unlock the weak one. The weak posture must be the thing you opt into, never the thing you fall back to — and the right refusal is sized to accountability: a load-bearing store refuses the whole boot; a non-load-bearing one degrades loudly.

Growing from it

This failure shape is born of developer convenience compounding silently: the dev path exists so work can proceed without provisioning, and the cheapest way to wire it is as the else-branch — which makes every possible misconfiguration route to it. The danger is camouflage: a system that falls back to weak crypto still runs, still 'encrypts,' still passes a glance, so the downgrade produces no signal anywhere. When you design any control with a strong and a weak mode, draw the decision table for every input state — set, unset, malformed, empty — and check which mode each row lands in; any row that lands weak without an explicit, logged opt-in is a latent downgrade. The general principle: absence of configuration is information about a mistake, not a preference — treat it as one, and fail or degrade loudly enough that the mistake gets fixed.

Act VLesson 54core pathunit 76 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A factory that reads missing configuration as "dev mode" turns a misconfiguration into a silent downgrade to the weakest posture.

The AIGP connection
Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and UseOECD AI Principles — robustness, security and safetyNIST AI RMF — MANAGE

Secure-by-default is the operational face of the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety: the system's weakest posture must be deliberately chosen, never accidentally inherited. The fix also exemplifies NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function in how it treats degraded states — the response to a missing prerequisite is sized to accountability, refusing the load-bearing path and loudly degrading the optional one — and the finding-to-fix arc itself, an independent review surfacing a latent fail-open the builder missed, is the review loop the framework's risk cycle expects.

AIGP takeaway Missing configuration is a misconfiguration, not a dev-mode vote: the weak posture must be explicit opt-in, never fallback.
Act VLesson 57unit 77 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A control built but never issued its key material is half-armed, which is to say off.

The failure

The mTLS path (mutual TLS — both ends of a connection prove identity with certificates) in shared/ipc/vsock.py had been production-ready for many sprints: the server SSL context, CERT_REQUIRED, the handshake, all built. What broke the chain was that the certificate files it needed did not exist — certs/pa_server.pem, certs/pa_server_key.pem, and certs/ca.pem were unprovisioned placeholders — and the gateway client at transport.py:416-436 passed no cert paths at all, so even a valid server would have rejected it. A door with all the hardware installed and no key ever cut. The fix (commit bcda777) co-issues the control and its material at one point in startup: per-boot certificates minted fail-closed before any service constructs, an in-memory certificate authority whose private key dies with the process, a 24-hour lifetime, and a production-wiring regression lock that fails if the certs are minted but never handed to the gateway.

Evidence: 2026-06-06 — The door that was built but never had a lock · commit bcda777
Before you turn the page

For each certificate or key your security design names: which code path creates it on a fresh machine, and what happens on the boot where it does not exist?

Act VLesson 57unit 77 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A control built but never issued its key material is half-armed, which is to say off.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VLesson 57unit 77 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A control built but never issued its key material is half-armed, which is to say off.

The lesson

When a security control depends on a provisioning step, the provisioning must fire automatically as part of the same startup path, never sit as a manual prerequisite no code enforces. Co-issue the control and its key material at one point, so the chain can never be half-armed: either both are present, or the boot refuses.

Growing from it

Half-armed controls accumulate because building and provisioning live in different workstreams: code review sees the mechanism, tests exercise it with fixture material, and the real key, certificate, or token is 'an ops step' that no automated path owns. The system then carries a fully-specified control that has never once operated — and everything still works, because the unprovisioned branch quietly skips it. Hunt for these by asking, for every credentialed control: where does the material come from on a fresh machine, which code path creates it, and what happens on the boot where it is missing? If the answers are 'a runbook,' 'nothing,' and 'it proceeds,' the control is off. The strong pattern is co-issuance — the same startup sequence that constructs the control provisions its material or refuses — plus a wiring test asserting the consumer actually received it.

Act VLesson 57unit 77 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A control built but never issued its key material is half-armed, which is to say off.

The AIGP connection
Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and UseISO/IEC 42001 — AIMS (controls operated, not merely documented)NIST AI RMF — MANAGE

ISO/IEC 42001's management-system framing distinguishes documenting a control from operating it: an AIMS (AI management system) expects controls to be implemented and checked in operation, and mTLS code with no issued certificates is a control documented but inoperative. The co-issuance fix exemplifies NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function — risk treatment implemented all the way into the running system — and the OECD robustness, security and safety principle's demand that protective mechanisms actually function in the deployed configuration.

AIGP takeaway A credentialed control with no issued material is off: co-issue keys with the control in one fail-closed startup path.
Act VLesson 59unit 78 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Build the mechanism and pin the sentinel before you throw the switch — an unactivated mechanism is a deferred bug, and the test suite is what defines "safe" before you flip the bit.

The failure

Flipping BlarAI's default from dev-mode to production posture was a breaking change too risky to fire blind: landing it before the signed manifest and the TPM ceremony keys existed would have bricked the daily boot. The staging pattern carried it. One increment (commit cbe5bb7) built the capability and laid four regression locks asserting the current pre-flip behaviour — lock (a) explicitly asserted resolve_dev_mode(HOST) still returns True — so a premature activation would fail the automated suite loudly. The activation increment (commit 94bc828) then threw the switch: the HOST default flipped from True to False in three lines of runtime code, and the same sentinel lock was updated to assert the new behaviour, with new locks covering the explicit BLARAI_DEV_MODE opt-in and its loud INSECURE banner. The activation was safe precisely because the suite had said what safe meant before the bit moved.

Evidence: 2026-06-06 — Building the door without opening it · 2026-06-06 — Throwing the switch: production becomes the HOST default · commit cbe5bb7 · commit 94bc828
Before you turn the page

For the switch you are afraid to flip: what test today would fail if someone flipped it prematurely — and what list defines when flipping becomes safe?

Act VLesson 59unit 78 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Build the mechanism and pin the sentinel before you throw the switch — an unactivated mechanism is a deferred bug, and the test suite is what defines "safe" before you flip the bit.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
three linesthe entire runtime delta
Act VLesson 59unit 78 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Build the mechanism and pin the sentinel before you throw the switch — an unactivated mechanism is a deferred bug, and the test suite is what defines "safe" before you flip the bit.

The lesson

A breaking change too risky to attempt cleanly tempts you to ship it unguarded or never ship it; both fail. The third path is staging: one increment builds the capability and pins a sentinel asserting the current behaviour, so premature activation fails loudly; a later increment throws the switch and updates the same sentinel. A mechanism built but never made safe to activate is a bug deferred and dressed as caution.

Growing from it

Risky flips go wrong in two mirrored ways: shipped eagerly, they fire before their prerequisites exist; deferred indefinitely, the unactivated mechanism rots while everyone believes the capability is done. The mechanism underneath is that 'safe to activate' is usually nobody's explicit definition — it lives as a feeling, so neither the eager nor the cautious party can prove their position. The staging pattern converts the feeling into executable state: the sentinel test asserts which side of the switch the system is on, the prerequisite list becomes the activation increment's checklist, and the flip itself shrinks to a small, reviewable diff that updates the sentinel in the same commit. Use it for any default change, posture change, or migration: build, pin the current state, prepare the prerequisites, then flip with the sentinel — and treat any mechanism that has waited unactivated for months as a finding, not a comfort.

Act VLesson 59unit 78 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Build the mechanism and pin the sentinel before you throw the switch — an unactivated mechanism is a deferred bug, and the test suite is what defines "safe" before you flip the bit.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)ISO/IEC 42001 — AIMS (plan-do-check-act management-system discipline)

Staged activation with a pinned sentinel is release governance in AIGP Domain III's sense — governing the release of the model and system as a controlled, evidenced act rather than an event. The sentinel is NIST AI RMF MEASURE-function TEVV (test, evaluation, verification, and validation) used as a change-control instrument: the suite defines and enforces the system's intended state on each side of the change, and ISO/IEC 42001's plan-do-check-act discipline shows in the shape — plan the flip, pin the check, act, re-check.

AIGP takeaway Stage risky flips: build the mechanism, pin a sentinel asserting current state, then activate by updating the sentinel deliberately.
Act VLesson 60unit 79 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Off-chip structural proofs and on-chip ceremony verification are distinct work with distinct value — stub data does not cheapen the structural proof.

The failure

Before the production-posture activation, the Policy Agent would refuse to start at dev_mode=false for three distinct reasons: no Known-Good Manifest path, no TPM (Trusted Platform Module) key name, no JWT (JSON Web Token) certificate-authority path — a cascade wired in config but never verified end-to-end. There were no real Qwen3-14B model weights at build time, and the temptation was to treat that as a deficiency blocking the harness. The staging increment (commit e1e0c1c) rejected the framing: the cascade to de-risk was structural — the manifest exists, the digests dict parses, the value is 64 hex characters, the validation gate accepts the format — provable with a stub manifest and a harness dummy binary whose digest is genuinely computable. Seven tests went green in 0.57 seconds, plus four fail-closed regression locks. What remained for the operator's on-chip ceremony was exactly what cannot be faked: the chip-bound key provisioning and the real-model digest match.

Evidence: 2026-06-06 — Testing the door before handing over the key · commit e1e0c1c
Before you turn the page

Of everything the expensive on-hardware session is scheduled to verify, how much could a stub have proven this afternoon?

Act VLesson 60unit 79 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Off-chip structural proofs and on-chip ceremony verification are distinct work with distinct value — stub data does not cheapen the structural proof.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
7 teststhe structural cascade proven0.57show long that proof took
Act VLesson 60unit 79 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Off-chip structural proofs and on-chip ceremony verification are distinct work with distinct value — stub data does not cheapen the structural proof.

The lesson

Off-chip structural proofs with stubs and on-chip ceremony verification are distinct work with distinct value. The instinct to dismiss stubbed proof as fake is wrong: it de-risks every structural surprise before the operator spends scarce ceremony time, so the human-on-hardware session narrows to exactly the part only the silicon can prove.

Growing from it

The underlying confusion is between fidelity and coverage: a stubbed environment has low fidelity to production, so it feels like it proves nothing — but most of what breaks in a security cascade is structure (paths, formats, parsing, ordering, fail-closed branches), and structure is exactly what stubs prove completely. Meanwhile the scarce resource — a human at the real hardware, a production key ceremony, a maintenance window — should be spent only on what is irreducibly bound to it. When you plan verification for anything that ends in a privileged or physical step, partition the claims first: which would a stub prove identically, and which can only the real environment answer? Push everything in the first set into cheap automated proof, and write down the second set as the ceremony's entire agenda. The day the human sits down, every failure they hit should be one only that day could have found.

Act VLesson 60unit 79 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Off-chip structural proofs and on-chip ceremony verification are distinct work with distinct value — stub data does not cheapen the structural proof.

The AIGP connection
Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and UseNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)OECD AI Principles — accountability

This is TEVV planning under NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function: test, evaluation, verification, and validation work is partitioned by what each environment can actually evidence, so assessment effort lands where it discriminates. It maps to AIGP Domain IV's competency of performing key activities to assess the AI model and system before deployment — and the deliberate division of machine-provable structure from human-witnessed ceremony reflects the accountability emphasis in the OECD principles: the human act is reserved for what genuinely requires a witness.

AIGP takeaway Partition verification: stubs prove structure cheaply; spend the scarce on-hardware ceremony only on what silicon alone can prove.
Act VLesson 61unit 80 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Transport topology and security mechanism are orthogonal decisions — conflating them leaves the real production path unbuilt behind a fully-specified one.

The failure

The first real production boot (dev_mode=False, HOST mode) failed at the transport layer: the vsock channel tried to create an AF_HYPERV socket — the virtual-machine-boundary transport — with a Linux-style (cid, port) address Windows rejects with bind(): bad family. The deeper problem was topological: in HOST mode there is no VM boundary; every service runs on the same Windows machine, and the correct transport is what a signed sprint acceptance criterion had described all along — loopback (127.0.0.1) plus mTLS (mutual TLS, certificate-proven identity on both ends), a path already verified in isolation earlier in the sprint. It was simply never wired into the boot, because 'production' had been conflated with 'AF_HYPERV': the boot's own step message had said 'real AF_HYPERV path,' which had been wrong for months. The fix (commit c92d06d) threaded an explicit host_mode parameter through the stack, with nine new tests locking family, address, and the end-to-end loopback mTLS handshake.

Evidence: 2026-06-06 — The transport path that was designed but never wired · commit c92d06d
Before you turn the page

The boot log says 'production path' — which independent decisions (topology, transport, security, posture) did that one word silently make for you?

Act VLesson 61unit 80 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Transport topology and security mechanism are orthogonal decisions — conflating them leaves the real production path unbuilt behind a fully-specified one.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VLesson 61unit 80 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Transport topology and security mechanism are orthogonal decisions — conflating them leaves the real production path unbuilt behind a fully-specified one.

The lesson

Transport topology and security mechanism are orthogonal: AF_HYPERV belongs to the VM-boundary topology; mTLS is a security property every production channel needs regardless of topology. The right diagnostic for a well-specified, isolation-verified path the boot never invokes is 'built into nothing,' not 'needs redesign' — wire the proven path in rather than redesigning around the wrong default.

Growing from it

Conflation bugs grow from shorthand: 'production' starts as a bundle of properties — real model, real certificates, real transport, hardened posture — and the bundle hardens into a single mental switch, so choosing one property silently chooses all the others. Here 'production means the VM transport' survived even though the deployed topology had no VM, leaving the actual production path fully specified, separately verified, and connected to nothing. The protection is to factor your configuration space explicitly: list the independent axes (where services run; how they talk; how the channel is secured; which posture is enforced) and check which combinations the system can really express — every axis collapsed into a mode flag is a place where the real deployment can differ from the imagined one. And when you find a verified-but-uninvoked path, resist the redesign reflex: the cheapest correct fix is usually wiring in what was already proven.

Act VLesson 61unit 80 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Transport topology and security mechanism are orthogonal decisions — conflating them leaves the real production path unbuilt behind a fully-specified one.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MAPOECD AI Principles — robustness, security and safety

NIST AI RMF's MAP function is about establishing context — what the system is, where it actually runs, which deployment environment its risks live in — and this failure was a context error carried in a word: the security architecture was specified for a topology the deployment did not have. Untangling the axes so the real production path got built exemplifies MAP feeding MANAGE, and keeping mTLS on either topology reflects the OECD robustness, security and safety expectation that protection follows the system, not the diagram.

AIGP takeaway Topology and security are separate axes: a security mechanism must ride whatever transport the real deployment uses.
Act VLesson 62unit 81 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Config-to-provisioner correspondence must be tested as a unit, or the gap surfaces only at production boot.

The failure

The per-boot certificate provisioner had been written with two services in mind — the Policy Agent server and the gateway client. But the Assistant Orchestrator's config named a third cert pair, certs/orch_client.pem and its key, that the mint had never been taught to produce — and the gap surfaced exactly where such gaps do: the Orchestrator's listener failed at live production boot with 'Failed to create server SSL context: [Errno 2] No such file or directory.' The systematic audit that followed — reading every [ipc] section across all three services' default.toml files — found a fourth unminted pair, certs/router_client.pem, waiting to produce the identical failure the moment the Semantic Router connected. The fix (commit 681b5b6) taught the mint all nine per-boot files and added seven tests, including a direct regression lock on the exact load_cert_chain call that failed.

Evidence: 2026-06-06 — The config and the mint were speaking different languages · commit 681b5b6
Before you turn the page

Name a file, queue, or key your config references — what test would fail if the component responsible for creating it never did?

Act VLesson 62unit 81 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Config-to-provisioner correspondence must be tested as a unit, or the gap surfaces only at production boot.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
two services→nine per-boot fileswhat the mint produces
Act VLesson 62unit 81 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Config-to-provisioner correspondence must be tested as a unit, or the gap surfaces only at production boot.

The lesson

Any config that names a file another component must produce needs a test that walks from the config key to that component's output. The durable safety net is not a per-service regression test added each time a gap bites, but a single test traversing every config section, extracting each named path, and asserting the provisioner covers it.

Growing from it

The mechanism is parallel evolution without a reconciliation step: configs and provisioners are written by different hands at different times, each correct against its own author's mental inventory, and nothing in the build ever holds the two lists side by side. Such correspondence gaps are invisible to component tests — the provisioner provably writes what it writes, the config provably parses — because the defect lives in the relationship, not in either artifact. Hunt for the pattern wherever one part of a system names resources another part creates: certificate paths, queue names, database migrations versus schema expectations, infrastructure outputs versus application settings. For each, write the closure test: enumerate every consumer-side reference mechanically — do not hand-maintain the list, which recreates the same divergence one level up — and assert the producer covers the set. One enumerating test outlives any number of per-incident patches.

Act VLesson 62unit 81 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Config-to-provisioner correspondence must be tested as a unit, or the gap surfaces only at production boot.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)ISO/IEC 42001 — AIMS (continual improvement on nonconformity)

This exemplifies the verification side of TEVV (test, evaluation, verification, and validation) under NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function, applied to system integration: each component was built to its own spec, but the composed system — the unit the operator actually boots — was verified by nothing until production ran it. The class-level fix, auditing every config against the provisioner and locking the seam with a test, also models the ISO/IEC 42001 management-system habit of correcting the process that produced a nonconformity, not just the instance that surfaced.

AIGP takeaway Test config-to-producer correspondence as a unit: enumerate every referenced resource and assert its producer actually produces it.
Act VLesson 63unit 82 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Fail-closed is a property of a call site relative to its unit of accountability, not a uniform property of a component.

The failure

After the dev-to-production key transition, practice-era rows in sessions.db sat encrypted under the old dev key, and the healthy production TPM-sealed key refused them with AES-GCM InvalidTag — the cipher's tamper-and-wrong-key rejection doing its job. But all three bulk readers — list_sessions, get_session_turns, _backfill_empty_titles — called leaf decrypt helpers that raise on failure inside their loops, so the first legacy row aborted the entire sweep and the whole app bricked: every session denied, including the perfectly healthy ones written under the production key. Two Sprint-14 regression tests had locked exactly this behaviour as 'fail-closed.' The fix (commit 145f2b1): bulk loops quarantine the bad row — omit it, log the stable greppable event SESSION_ROW_DECRYPT_QUARANTINE, continue — while single-record paths keep hard-failing; no plaintext returned, no tampered data trusted, only the blast radius changed. The governing decision record, ADR-025 §2.7, was amended to say what the code now does.

Evidence: 2026-06-06 — When "fail closed" becomes a door that locks you out of your own house · commit 145f2b1
Before you turn the page

When one record in ten thousand fails its integrity check, what should the user lose — that record, or the application?

Act VLesson 63unit 82 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Fail-closed is a property of a call site relative to its unit of accountability, not a uniform property of a component.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VLesson 63unit 82 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Fail-closed is a property of a call site relative to its unit of accountability, not a uniform property of a component.

The lesson

Fail-closed is a property of a call site relative to its unit of accountability. A leaf decryptor is accountable for one row and must raise when that row will not decrypt; a bulk reader is accountable for the health of the whole store and must not let one sick row deny every healthy one. Applying the leaf posture uniformly converts a confidentiality control into an availability attack surface.

Growing from it

The mechanism is that 'fail-closed' reads like a single virtue, so it gets applied as a blanket — but the CIA triad (confidentiality, integrity, availability) names three properties, and a control tuned only for the first two can become the attack on the third: here a self-inflicted denial of service, the app refusing its owner's own healthy data. When you set a failure posture, first name the unit of accountability at each call site: what did the caller actually ask for, and what does the caller need to stay healthy? A caller that named one record should hear the failure loudly; a caller sweeping everything on behalf of startup should isolate the casualty, log it observably, and serve the rest. And when a passing test locks the bricking behaviour, recognize the test as part of the defect — rewrite it to assert the synthesis, both the quarantine and the still-hard leaf.

Act VLesson 63unit 82 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Fail-closed is a property of a call site relative to its unit of accountability, not a uniform property of a component.

The AIGP connection
Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and UseOECD AI Principles — robustness, security and safetyNIST AI RMF — secure and resilient (trustworthy-AI characteristic)

The availability leg of the CIA triad names this failure as the security failure it is, and the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety carries the same expectation: a safe system keeps serving its legitimate user under partial failure. The NIST AI RMF trustworthy-AI characteristic 'secure and resilient' is the synthesis the fix lands on — confidentiality and integrity untouched, no plaintext and no trusted tampered data, while resilience bounds the blast radius of one bad row to that row.

AIGP takeaway Size failure posture to accountability: leaf calls fail hard; bulk sweeps quarantine — or confidentiality controls become availability attacks.
Act VLesson 67unit 83 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

An integrity check on a multi-file artifact must cover the whole manifest — and reject whatever the manifest omits.

The failure

BlarAI's weight-integrity gate had guarded the Policy Agent and the Assistant Orchestrator since Sprint 12 — and had verified only the primary model binary, openvino_model.bin, against the manifest the whole time. Every other .bin in the model directory — the draft-model weights, tokenizer embeddings, any future shard — loaded unverified, and nothing at all checked for files present on disk but absent from the manifest. The attack path was clean: swap the primary binary to a new filename and drop a payload under the original name, and the boot gate passes without complaint. The fix (commit 59fa496) added verify_all_manifest_entries: hash and compare every entry in the manifest's digests dict, then a second pass rejecting any .bin on disk that the manifest does not name. Eight new tests cover the four paths — all-pass, swapped digest, extra .bin on disk, missing manifest entry.

Evidence: 2026-06-07 — Closing the single-file blind spot in weight integrity · commit 59fa496
Before you turn the page

Your integrity gate is green — which files in the artifact did it actually hash, and what happens to a file it has never heard of?

Act VLesson 67unit 83 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

An integrity check on a multi-file artifact must cover the whole manifest — and reject whatever the manifest omits.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VLesson 67unit 83 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

An integrity check on a multi-file artifact must cover the whole manifest — and reject whatever the manifest omits.

The lesson

A manifest defines a set: the integrity check must verify every member of that set and assert no extra members exist. Checking less than the manifest claims is a false assurance; checking less than the directory actually contains is a silent injection window.

Growing from it

Partial integrity checks survive because the first file checked is the one everybody thinks of as 'the model,' and the green check radiates assurance over the whole artifact — the classic gap between what a control covers and what its consumers believe it covers. The logic to internalize is set equality: an integrity claim over a multi-part artifact must hold in both directions — everything claimed must be present and correct (no member unverified), and everything present must be claimed (no stowaway an attacker added). Audit your own gates with two questions: enumerate what the artifact actually contains versus what the check actually reads — any difference is unverified surface; then ask what happens to a file the inventory does not mention — if the answer is 'nothing notices,' you have the injection window. The same discipline applies beyond model weights: container images, dependency trees, plugin directories, backup sets.

Act VLesson 67unit 83 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

An integrity check on a multi-file artifact must cover the whole manifest — and reject whatever the manifest omits.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentEU AI Act — accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity (high-risk obligation concept)OECD AI Principles — robustness, security and safety

Model weights are the deployed system's most direct integrity surface, and the EU AI Act's high-risk obligations bundle accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity for good reason: an attacker who can swap a weight shard owns the model's behaviour. The whole-manifest sweep exemplifies the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety applied at model-load time, and the four fail-closed test paths are NIST AI RMF MEASURE-function TEVV evidence (test, evaluation, verification, and validation) that the gate enforces the set in both directions.

AIGP takeaway Integrity over a multi-file artifact is set equality: verify every manifest member and reject every file the manifest omits.
Act VLesson 71unit 84 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Version pins are containment, not supply-chain integrity.

The failure

Sprint 16's dependency stream found the security-critical dependencies wide open: cryptography>=41.0 allowed a five-major-version window, PyJWT>=2.8 permitted a future 3.x with unknown signing semantics, and openvino>=2024.0 allowed a three-year span of the model-loading runtime to change silently underneath the system. The root pyproject.toml had made the call correctly for cryptography back in Sprint 14, but the five service files had never caught up; commit 639c9b0 closed the gap uniformly, tiered by how directly each dependency sits on the trust boundary. The honest part came at the boundary of the fix itself: PEP 508 — the dependency-specification format pyproject.toml uses — simply does not support hash syntax; the hash=sha256 annotations live in pip-tools and uv lock files. So the pins contain version jumps; they cannot stop a malicious wheel served at the same version number. The rationale doc names that gap instead of implying coverage.

Evidence: 2026-06-07 — Closing the unbounded upper-bounds on the security surface · commit 639c9b0
Before you turn the page

Your dependencies are pinned — what, precisely, stops a tampered package arriving under the exact version number you pinned?

Act VLesson 71unit 84 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Version pins are containment, not supply-chain integrity.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VLesson 71unit 84 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Version pins are containment, not supply-chain integrity.

The lesson

Version pins are containment, not supply-chain integrity: bounding a dependency stops a silent major-version jump, but a version specifier cannot stop a malicious package served at the same version number — only a hashed lock file closes that gap. The honest posture names the boundary the control actually buys instead of letting a pin masquerade as a guarantee.

Growing from it

Controls overclaim through vocabulary drift: 'we pinned our dependencies' sounds like supply-chain security, and unless someone prices exactly which attack each measure stops, the team's belief expands to fill the reassuring phrase. The practice to build is threat-by-threat accounting: for each control, write down the attacks it defeats (an upstream publishing a breaking new major version), the attacks it does not (a compromised registry serving altered bytes at the pinned version), and the named next step that would close the gap (a lock file generated with hashes, verified at install). That residual list is not an admission of weakness — it is the most valuable artifact the exercise produces, because the unnamed residual is the one nobody is watching. Apply the same accounting to backups, encryption, and scanning: every control has a boundary, and the danger concentrates wherever the boundary is assumed rather than written.

Act VLesson 71unit 84 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Version pins are containment, not supply-chain integrity.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MANAGENIST AI RMF — accountable and transparent (trustworthy-AI characteristic)

Third-party components are part of the AI system an organization must govern, and NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function explicitly includes residual-risk monitoring and communication — which is what naming 'version-containment now, hash-verification as the explicit next step' performs: the treatment's boundary is on the record, not implied away. The NIST trustworthy-AI characteristic 'accountable and transparent' shows in the documentation choice — the rationale states what the control does not buy, so the next reader inherits the true posture.

AIGP takeaway A version pin contains drift; only hash verification defeats substitution. Name the boundary every control actually buys.
Act VLesson 93unit 85 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Wiring a guard onto a hot path means asking what legitimate traffic it will see, not just what it is meant to catch.

The failure

#634 asked for one line: register the exfil-screen — the filter that scans outbound data for secrets — onto the armed egress guard. Taken literally, it would have welded a noose. The guard screens every guarded send, and BlarAI's internal traffic (the loopback gateway hop, the Hyper-V vsock channel) legitimately carries the runtime's own capability tokens and the user's personal data — exactly what the screen flags. The first internal message would have tripped the kill-switch and cut all egress: a self-inflicted denial of service dressed as a security control. A second trap: the screen returns a Detection object, in Python always 'truthy' (it counts as true in a yes/no test), so the verdict normaliser would have read a clean Detection(blocked=False) as a positive hit and blocked the first clean external send too. Two independent ways the obvious wiring would have hung the system. Merged 651ef4a; gate green, 2372 passed.

Evidence: 2026-06-09 — The screen that would have strangled the host it guards · commit 651ef4a
Before you turn the page

Before a detector gets enforcement power over a live channel, which two populations of traffic must you enumerate — and which one decides whether the control survives its first day?

Act VLesson 93unit 85 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Wiring a guard onto a hot path means asking what legitimate traffic it will see, not just what it is meant to catch.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VLesson 93unit 85 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Wiring a guard onto a hot path means asking what legitimate traffic it will see, not just what it is meant to catch.

The lesson

A content screen pointed at a channel that carries the system's own secrets will fire on them; a verdict contract read by truthiness will misread an always-truthy 'clean' result. Scope the guard to the traffic that actually crosses the boundary — here, only sockets that connect to an external-allowlisted endpoint are tagged for screening — and read verdicts by their explicit flag, never by truthiness.

Growing from it

The mechanism: a detector is specified by what it should catch, but it is deployed onto a channel — and the channel's legitimate traffic decides its false-positive rate. A screen that flags credentials, pointed at the path that legitimately transports credentials, is an outage by design. Before arming any guard, enumerate two populations: what the boundary-crossing traffic looks like, and what flows on a normal day that must never trip it — then prove the allow side as rigorously as the deny side (this sharpens lessons 22 and 36). The second mechanism is contract reading: when one component interprets another's result, use the explicit named field, never an incidental property like truthiness — an object can be 'true' and mean 'clean.' Note that the existing seam tests had locked in the old broken behaviour; a passing test can guard a defect.

Act VLesson 93unit 85 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Wiring a guard onto a hot path means asking what legitimate traffic it will see, not just what it is meant to catch.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MAPOECD AI Principles — robustness, security and safety

NIST AI RMF names 'safe' and 'secure and resilient' among its trustworthy-AI characteristics, and a control that denies the system's own legitimate operation fails the first while wearing the second. The MAP function asks for context: what the operating environment actually carries, not only what the threat model fears. Scoping the screen to boundary-crossing traffic — and proving it inert until an endpoint is allowlisted — exemplifies Domain III.A, governing the designing and building of the system so a protection cannot become the outage.

AIGP takeaway Scope guards to traffic that crosses the boundary and read verdicts by explicit flags; an unscoped screen is an outage wearing a security badge.
Act VLesson 94core pathunit 86 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A merged capability is not an activated one - say which it is.

The failure

The last two Lead-Architect-added gate items came back from their builders together: #637 rewrites how sensitive on-disk files get their permissions (Windows access-control lists); #639 wires a new branch into the authorization core — the ESCALATE consumer, the path that pauses a risky action for explicit operator approval. The merge-gate WAS the control: the review read the actual code and confirmed #639 fail-closed on every path (no verifier, an exception, a timeout, a malformed result all resolve to DENY) and dormant-safe (nothing registers at import). Then the honest framing for the gate record: the merge MERGED the consumer; it did not ACTIVATE it. An ESCALATE actually prompting the operator still requires registering a verifier at startup — a real behaviour change wanting a live end-to-end verify. Merged 2d82f69 and 494ebb8; the session's four merges took the standing gate from 2358 to 2444, each re-run on clean main.

Evidence: 2026-06-09 — Merge-gating the last two Policy-Agent gate items · commit 2d82f69 · commit 494ebb8
Before you turn the page

A security control merged today with all tests green: what single question tells you whether production is actually better protected tonight than it was last night?

Act VLesson 94core pathunit 86 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A merged capability is not an activated one - say which it is.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
2358→2444standing gate across four merges
Act VLesson 94core pathunit 86 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A merged capability is not an activated one - say which it is.

The lesson

A control can be fully built, tested, and merged and still change nothing in production until a deliberate wiring step turns it on — the egress screen, the ESCALATE consumer. The honest gate record distinguishes 'the path exists and is proven' from 'the path is live,' because conflating the two is how a system gets credited for a protection it is not yet running.

Growing from it

The conflation happens because the artifacts of 'done' look identical for both states: green tests, a merged branch, a closed ticket. A status vocabulary with only built and not-built loses the dimension that matters operationally — what runs. This sharpens lessons 23 and 46: a capability proven is not a control enforced, and tests prove a mechanism in isolation, never that the live system invokes it. The recovering question is cheap: for every merged control, ask what you would observe in production today that differs from yesterday; if the answer is nothing, write merged-dormant and schedule activation as its own deliberate, verifiable act. Notice also what made merging without a live-verify safe here: #637's permission helper grants the running account full access first, then locks others out — a control that can only tighten for others cannot brick the app. Find the invariant that bounds the blast radius.

Act VLesson 94core pathunit 86 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A merged capability is not an activated one - say which it is.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MANAGEEU AI Act — record-keeping (concept level)

NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function covers implementing risk responses and communicating residual risk — and a gate record that writes merged-dormant instead of done is precisely that communication: the risk treatment exists but is not yet operating. The EU AI Act attaches record-keeping obligations to high-risk systems; the concept-level point is that records must reflect operational truth, not build status. Saying which exemplifies Domain III.C — governing the release, monitoring and maintenance of the model and system.

AIGP takeaway Merged is a build status; active is an operational status. Gate records must say which, or protection gets credited before it runs.
Act VLesson 95unit 87 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

An activated control is not an exercised one — verify it against the real producers, not assumed ones.

The failure

#639 had a longer tail than its merge. The consumer went in dormant, and activating it was framed as a fresh decision to escalate — over-caution the Lead Architect punctured: 'why haven't you activated it?' Activation on the terminal surface turned out to be one line, because the orchestrator and the terminal app share a process. Then the live-verify 'didn't work,' and the failure was the real finding: none of BlarAI's four shipped tools (time, date, day-of-week, calculate) can produce an ESCALATE — the verdict that pauses a risky action for operator approval — at all. The seven ESCALATE rules key on file paths, large writes, and flags only a future file or network tool will carry. 'ESCALATE fires for seven rule classes today' was true of the rule logic, false of the current toolset. A deterministic demo (scripts/demo_escalation_modal.py) let the operator watch the real approval dialog approve and deny.

Evidence: 2026-06-09 — The consumer that worked the moment it had something to consume
Before you turn the page

The control is wired, activated, and just demonstrated live in front of the operator — what fact about today's toolset could still make 'it protects production' false?

Act VLesson 95unit 87 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

An activated control is not an exercised one — verify it against the real producers, not assumed ones.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VLesson 95unit 87 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

An activated control is not an exercised one — verify it against the real producers, not assumed ones.

The lesson

A consumer can be built, wired, activated, and even live-demonstrated and still never fire in production, because nothing yet produces its input. The honest record distinguishes 'the path works' from 'the path is exercised by real traffic,' and a live-verify must use the actual toolset — an assumed trigger, like a filesystem tool that does not exist, proves nothing.

Growing from it

Controls form producer-consumer chains, and every link can be independently green while the chain has never carried a real event end-to-end. You verified the consumer with a synthetic trigger; production's job is to produce the trigger, and today it cannot. The recognition move: for any alarm, monitor, or approval path, ask what in the current system generates its input — and answer from the code, not the design document. Trace one real event from producer to consumer before claiming coverage; where no producer exists yet, record the control as live-but-dormant and name the event that will first exercise it. This sharpens lesson 46's 'built but wired into nothing' a full notch further: wired, activated, demonstrated — and still dormant in practice. The companion lesson is calibration: finishing a decision the operator already made is completion, not a new decision to hand back.

Act VLesson 95unit 87 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

An activated control is not an exercised one — verify it against the real producers, not assumed ones.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV: verification vs validation)

This is the verification-versus-validation distinction inside NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function: the demo verified the mechanism was built to spec; validation asks whether it does the real job in the real context, and the real context contains no producer yet. Recording 'mechanism-verified, dormant until an escalating tool ships' exemplifies Domain III.C — governing release, monitoring and maintenance — where claims about a control's coverage are scoped to the traffic that actually exists.

AIGP takeaway Activated is not exercised: trace one real event from producer to consumer before claiming a control covers production.
Act VLesson 97core pathunit 88 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A security control must not claim more than it delivers.

The failure

A #611 live-memory feasibility study, grounding itself in the real cryptography code, found a control that lies about itself. The DEK break-glass recovery ceremony — the emergency path for recovering the data-encryption key (provision_dek_keystore.py) — 'zeroizes' recovered key material after use, except it doesn't. Every recovery_key = bytes(len(recovery_key)) rebinds the name to a fresh all-zero buffer; the original secret bytes object is immutable in Python and merely dereferenced, left in memory until the garbage collector reaps it. The comments ('overwrite', 'Zero out … in memory') asserted a residency control the code never delivered. The fix was deliberately the small one: correct the comments to state the real, weak guarantee — best-effort reference-drop, not in-RAM erasure — and point at #611 where the genuine improvement is tracked. The behaviour was deliberately NOT changed: making the code look like it zeroizes would re-assert the same false claim with more confidence.

Evidence: 2026-06-09 — The zeroization that never cleared
Before you turn the page

A comment says the recovery key is 'zeroed out in memory' — what does the next maintainer now never check, and what happens if the claim is false?

Act VLesson 97core pathunit 88 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A security control must not claim more than it delivers.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VLesson 97core pathunit 88 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A security control must not claim more than it delivers.

The lesson

When code documents a protection — zeroization, isolation, validation — the comment is a claim a future reader will trust. If the implementation cannot honour it (here, Python's immutability prevents true in-memory erasure), say so plainly and point at where the real mitigation is tracked. An overclaiming control is worse than an absent one, because it buys false confidence.

Growing from it

Documentation and implementation drift independently, and security comments are the highest-stakes drift, because a reader's response to 'this is handled' is to stop checking — the overclaim converts directly into someone else's unexamined assumption. Note the second-layer trap this fix dodged: 'improving' the code cosmetically, with more elaborate-looking zeroization that still cannot erase immutable memory, would have re-asserted the false claim more convincingly. Sometimes the honest comment IS the fix, and the behaviour change belongs elsewhere, tracked. This sharpens lesson 47 (the comment that claimed a checksum the code never ran) and pairs with lesson 51 (make the property executable): where a property can be tested, encode it as a test that can fail; where the platform genuinely cannot deliver it, document the modest truth. Audit your comments as claims — for each 'this prevents, erases, or validates X,' ask what would make it false.

Act VLesson 97core pathunit 88 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A security control must not claim more than it delivers.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — trustworthy characteristics (accountable and transparent)EU AI Act — technical documentation (concept level)

Among NIST AI RMF's trustworthy-AI characteristics, 'accountable and transparent' covers the claims a system's builders make about their own controls — and an overclaiming comment is a transparency failure inside the codebase itself. The EU AI Act's high-risk obligations include technical documentation; the concept-level point is that documentation must match delivered behaviour. Correcting the claim to the modest truth, with the real mitigation tracked openly, exemplifies Domain III.A — governing how the system is built and described.

AIGP takeaway An overclaiming control buys false confidence; document the guarantee actually delivered and track the real mitigation where reviewers can see it.
Act VLesson 101unit 89 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Strip authority with an allowlist, not a denylist — and know which lever you're pulling.

The failure

BlarAI's launcher must run elevated because Hyper-V, Windows' virtual-machine layer, demands it — but "elevated" is not one bit. It is a bundle of ~twenty privileges stamped into the process token, Windows' record of what a running program may do: SeDebug, SeLoadDriver, SeTcb and more. The launcher is not just a launcher: it hosts the Policy Agent, the Assistant Orchestrator and the model as in-process threads, so every privilege it holds, the LLM (large language model) tool loop holds. The most security-sensitive component ran with all of admin by inheritance, chosen by no one. #652 strips the token down to a tiny keep-allowlist using SE_PRIVILEGE_REMOVED — deleted for the lifetime of the process, unrecoverable from inside — applied before any worker thread starts, so spawned children inherit the smaller token. The tempting design, a remove-list of known-dangerous privileges, was rejected: it fails open. On the elevated launcher the strip removes ~15 privileges.

Evidence: 2026-06-10 — Taking away the keys the launcher never uses
Before you turn the page

When a capability you never anticipated shows up in the process, does your control drop it or keep it — and who chose that default?

Act VLesson 101unit 89 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Strip authority with an allowlist, not a denylist — and know which lever you're pulling.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
~20 privileges, all of admin→~15 removed for the process lifetimethe elevated launcher token under #6522 + 2keep-set: two hard requirements, two keep-on-doubt judgment calls
Act VLesson 101unit 89 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Strip authority with an allowlist, not a denylist — and know which lever you're pulling.

The lesson

Reduce a process's power by removing everything not explicitly needed, so an unanticipated capability is dropped by default instead of surviving a hard-coded remove-list: a denylist fails open, an allowlist fails safe, and "unknown means denied" is the only defensible default. And know which lever you are pulling: integrity-lowering (Windows' per-process trust ranking) cannot reach in-process threads; privilege-stripping shrinks what the High-integrity process may do without changing the integrity it runs at.

Growing from it

Two mechanisms. A denylist encodes yesterday's threat inventory: it can only name the dangers its author knew, and the platform keeps minting capabilities — a new Windows build, a different elevation path — each surviving the list silently. An allowlist encodes need — small, stable, auditable; the unknown drops by default. Second, every control acts at a specific scope, and a control applied at the wrong scope does nothing while looking like hardening: lowering integrity is per-process, so it cannot touch workers living as threads inside a process that must stay High. When you shrink authority anywhere — permissions, API scopes, firewall rules — start from what is provably needed and drop the rest, then check what scope your lever actually acts at. Before each removal, ask which sibling control could fail silently without it: a fail-safe that skips looks exactly like one that ran, so keep on doubt and write the doubt down.

Act VLesson 101unit 89 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Strip authority with an allowlist, not a denylist — and know which lever you're pulling.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — trustworthy characteristic: secure and resilientOECD AI Principles — robustness, security and safety

Designing what authority an AI system inherits is part of governing how the system is built — AIGP Domain III.A. The stake is agentic: the AIGP Body of Knowledge v2.1 adds agentic architectures as deployment examples, and here the model's tool loop holds whatever the hosting process holds, so deny-by-default privilege design is model-and-system governance, not IT housekeeping. The allowlist posture exemplifies the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's "secure and resilient" trustworthy-AI characteristic and the OECD AI Principle of robustness, security and safety.

AIGP takeaway Authority an agentic system inherits is a governed design choice: allowlist what is needed, drop the unknown by default.
Act VLesson 104unit 90 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

An audit log is the one at-rest store where signed-plaintext beats encryption.

The failure

BlarAI's standing posture is encrypt-content-at-rest: the session and memory stores, sessions.db and substrate.db, are sealed under the DEK (data encryption key) per ADR-025, the at-rest encryption decision record (an ADR is an architecture decision record). The audit-retention work (#607) surfaced a question its builder was right to escalate rather than answer: the new sealed audit-log segments inherited the active log's on-disk shape — TPM-signed (Trusted Platform Module, a dedicated security chip), owner-only-DACL'd (a discretionary access control list, Windows file permissions), but plaintext. The reflex, and the project's standing lean, is encrypt-everything-at-rest; the builder had been overridden before for proposing a plaintext residual, so the honest move was to escalate with a recommendation rather than quietly ship either default. The operator ratified the recommendation as ADR-029: keep the audit log signed-and-locked, not encrypted — a genuine exception to a good default, not a lapse from it.

Evidence: 2026-06-10 — The one store I chose not to encrypt
Before you turn the page

After the very key your system depends on is lost or compromised, which of your records could a reviewer still verify — and which died with it?

Act VLesson 104unit 90 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

An audit log is the one at-rest store where signed-plaintext beats encryption.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VLesson 104unit 90 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

An audit log is the one at-rest store where signed-plaintext beats encryption.

The lesson

An audit trail's value is independent verifiability: a reviewer — or a future operator — must verify it without holding the data-encryption key, and the trail must survive the loss or compromise of the very key whose use it records. Encrypting it couples the witness to the thing it witnesses. The exception holds because the records are metadata, not content — and it carries a named expiry: re-weigh when tools start writing sensitive paths or URLs into the log.

Growing from it

Good defaults fail quietly when applied by category instead of by property. Encryption buys confidentiality; an audit log earns its keep through different properties — integrity, authorship, and the ability of someone without your keys to verify it after things go wrong — so the default optimizes the one property this store barely needs while taxing the ones it exists for. When you meet a store that resists your default, name the properties that store is for and check each control against them; a witness must never depend on the thing it witnesses. And handle the deviation as governance, not convenience: escalate it to the accountable owner with a recommendation, record the reasoning where the next reader will find it, and attach the condition that would reopen the question — an exception without an expiry quietly becomes policy.

Act VLesson 104unit 90 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

An audit log is the one at-rest store where signed-plaintext beats encryption.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentEU AI Act — record-keeping/logging (high-risk obligation concept)NIST AI RMF — MANAGE

Record-keeping and logging are named high-risk obligation concepts in the EU AI Act because accountability rests on the trail — and a trail that dies with the key it monitors cannot support accountability when it matters most. The ratified deviation also exemplifies NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function: a risk treatment chosen deliberately, its residual confidentiality exposure named rather than implied away, and a revisit trigger — sensitive identifiers entering the log — placed on the record as part of the decision.

AIGP takeaway An audit log must outlive the key it witnesses: sign and lock it, do not encrypt it — and record the exception's revisit trigger.
Act VLesson 106unit 91 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

The only secret you can truly erase is a mutable one.

The failure

ADR-025 gave BlarAI at-rest encryption and honestly recorded the limit it could not cross: while the process runs, plaintext-derived secrets live in RAM. The #611 feasibility study walked that surface and named the worst offender — not the DEK (data encryption key) but the substrate's embedding cache: _load_embed_cache decrypts every document and turn vector (embeddings — numeric representations of text by meaning) at store construction and holds the lot for the store's lifetime. Embeddings are semantically invertible — vec2text-class attacks reconstruct text from them — making this the largest, longest-lived sensitive blob the running system held. It also exposed the existing key 'zeroization' as ineffective: rebinding a Python bytes name to zeros dereferences the secret but leaves its bytes in the heap until the garbage collector maybe reaps them. The fix (commit 295cb69) zeroes each cached numpy vector in place after an idle window, re-decrypting lazily on the next retrieval.

Evidence: 2026-06-10 — The secret you can actually erase · commit 295cb69
Before you turn the page

When your code 'zeroizes' a secret by assigning zeros to the variable, what happens to the bytes the variable used to point at?

Act VLesson 106unit 91 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

The only secret you can truly erase is a mutable one.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
900 sdefault idle window before the cache is zeroed — a labelled placeholder until the on-box reload latency is measured+18synthetic-embedding tests, including the captured-reference all-zero assertion and the concurrency storm
Act VLesson 106unit 91 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

The only secret you can truly erase is a mutable one.

The lesson

The only secret you can truly erase is a mutable one. Python bytes are immutable — rebinding the name to zeros dereferences the secret and leaves its bytes in the heap, so 'zeroization' of a bytes key is a comment, not a control. A numpy array is mutable, so an in-place overwrite genuinely scrubs the buffer. The honest test of any memory-residency claim: a captured reference to the buffer reads zero afterward — not merely that the container dropped it.

Growing from it

Ineffective scrubbing survives because the code reads as if it worked: the variable now holds zeros, a test confirms the dictionary is empty, and nothing in the language's surface shows you the old bytes still sitting in process memory. Garbage-collected languages deliberately hide the storage layer, so 'I overwrote the secret' is a claim about objects, while attackers read memory. Before you claim erasure, ask two questions of the concrete type holding the secret: can its storage be overwritten in place at all, and what copies exist that you do not own — runtime internals, native libraries — that no in-place write reaches? Then write the test the property deserves: capture a reference to the buffer before the scrub and assert it reads zero after. And keep the claim honest about windows: erasure shrinks how long a secret is exposed; it cannot protect the moment it is in use.

Act VLesson 106unit 91 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

The only secret you can truly erase is a mutable one.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — privacy-enhanced (trustworthy-AI characteristic)NIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)

The NIST AI RMF trustworthy-AI characteristic 'privacy-enhanced' has to hold in RAM as well as on disk: an invertible embedding cache is personal data in everything but format, and shrinking its resident window is privacy engineering at the memory layer. The captured-reference assertion is MEASURE-function TEVV (test, evaluation, verification, and validation) applied to the control itself — verifying the property actually claimed, erasure, rather than a proxy, emptiness — and the named irreducible in-use window models the honest residual-risk naming the framework expects.

AIGP takeaway Immutable secrets cannot be overwritten, only dereferenced; claim zeroization only where a captured reference provably reads zero afterward.
Act VLesson 109unit 92 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Sanitization order is a verdict-integrity property, not a style choice

The failure

Stage B of the #655 ingest program needed what BlarAI had avoided its entire life: a real HTML parser. The Lead Architect chose trafilatura — a library that extracts article text from web pages — at the cost of 9 hash-pinned packages (pinned to cryptographic checksums so only vetted bytes install); the package's own fetch functions were forbidden on arrival, enforced by a scan that fails the standing test gate on any reference. One ordering subtlety inside the pipeline earned its own test. The injection scan — the check for text crafted to smuggle instructions to the model — runs on normalized text BEFORE the delimiter strip. Strip first, and a forged <|GROUNDED_CONTEXT_BEGIN|> marker vanishes before the scanner sees it: the verdict gets laundered, and the operator reviews a 'clean' document that arrived armed. Normalization must precede both, because a zero-width space — an invisible character — inside 'ignore previous instructions' defeats a naive phrase scan.

Evidence: 2026-06-10 — The biggest dependency since OpenVINO, and the door we welded shut on arrival · commits 9121653, 7531abe
Before you turn the page

If the delimiter strip runs before the injection scan, what artifact is the 'clean' verdict actually describing — and who downstream still believes it describes the document as submitted?

Act VLesson 109unit 92 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Sanitization order is a verdict-integrity property, not a style choice

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
9 packageshash-pinned for trafilatura — the biggest dependency since OpenVINO46 new tests29 corpus + 15 text-path + 2 security locks, all green
Act VLesson 109unit 92 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Sanitization order is a verdict-integrity property, not a style choice

The lesson

Sanitization order is a verdict-integrity property, not a style choice. Detect on what was actually submitted, then strip: strip-then-detect launders the quarantine verdict, because the evidence is destroyed before the detector runs. Normalize before detecting, because invisible characters are a scanner-evasion vector. Lock the ordering with tests that attack it — an evasion test proves the order does real work.

Growing from it

A cleaning pipeline is a chain of transforms, and every transform destroys information; a detector placed downstream of a destructive transform can only judge what survived, so its verdict silently becomes a statement about the sanitized artifact, not the submitted one. The attacker controls the original; the reviewer's trust attaches to the verdict; ordering is the only thing connecting the two. You will find the same shape far beyond HTML: redaction before anomaly detection, markup stripping before moderation, decompression and resizing before forensic checks. The discipline: for every detector in a pipeline, name the artifact it must judge — usually as-submitted, after only the canonical normalization that removes disguises without removing evidence — then write an adversarial test that plants evidence exactly where a wrong order would destroy it, and keep that test as a permanent lock so a future refactor cannot quietly reorder the stages.

Act VLesson 109unit 92 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Sanitization order is a verdict-integrity property, not a style choice

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)OECD AI Principles — robustness, security and safety

A quarantine verdict is an evaluation a human relies on to approve content into a decades-horizon store, and TEVV — test, evaluation, verification, and validation inside NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function — requires an evaluation to measure the artifact it claims to measure; strip-then-detect quietly substitutes a different artifact. Locking the order with adversarial tests, and keeping quarantined text visible so the operator's review stays meaningful, exemplifies Domain III.A — governing the designing and building of the system — and the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety.

AIGP takeaway Detectors judge only what survives upstream transforms: normalize, then detect, then strip — and lock the order with tests that attack it.
Act VLesson 110unit 93 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A deterministic hash of secret content is content metadata, not a label

The failure

An independent Lead-Architect-session verification of the knowledge-bank branch caught what its builder shipped without seeing. The knowledge_docs table stored the SHA-256 — a deterministic fingerprint: same input, same output, for anyone — of the full cleaned plaintext as a plaintext content_sha256 column, directly beside the AES-256-GCM ciphertext it fingerprints, and the signed-plaintext ingest audit log carried the same digest as car_hash. Every piece had a justification (the digest powers a staging integrity cross-check; the audit chain wants a content reference; 'it's only a hash'), and the composition was still wrong. Under the stolen-database threat model an attacker holding knowledge.db runs any public article through the deterministic in-repo cleaner, hashes the output, and tests membership — exactly the attack the keyed source_hash two columns away was designed to deny. The store denied 'did he ingest this URL' while answering 'did he ingest this article' for free.

Evidence: 2026-06-10 — The encrypted store that kept a plaintext fingerprint of its own contents
Before you turn the page

The column is 'only a hash,' and hashes cannot be reversed — so what can an attacker who can fetch or guess candidate documents still learn from it, without reversing anything?

Act VLesson 110unit 93 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A deterministic hash of secret content is content metadata, not a label

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
zeroverification-capability cost of keying the digest — the RAM-side integrity cross-check unchanged
Act VLesson 110unit 93 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A deterministic hash of secret content is content metadata, not a label

The lesson

A deterministic hash of secret content is content metadata, not a label. Anywhere ciphertext is the design — at rest, or in a signed plaintext log — content-derived hashes must be keyed: an HMAC, a fingerprint that also requires a secret key, keeps equality checkable for the system and unguessable for a thief. 'It's only a hash' is the warning sign to check for an oracle; here the right pattern already sat two columns away, in the same CREATE TABLE.

Growing from it

Why does this slip through? Each surface is judged against its own purpose — the integrity check, the audit reference — and each justification is locally true; the leak lives in the composition, plus an attacker capability the design never priced. Hashes feel safe because they are irreversible, but irreversibility only protects unguessable inputs: when the content is public or generatable, an attacker does not need to reverse your hash — only to recompute it and compare. Hunt for deterministic derivatives of protected data everywhere a system stores or logs them: digests, lengths, sort orders. For each, ask what an outsider who can generate candidate inputs could learn. The fixes are cheap when the read path never needs the raw value back: key the derivation — here the RAM-side integrity check was unchanged, cost zero — and add a regression lock that scans the raw stored bytes for the plaintext digest of a known item.

Act VLesson 110unit 93 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A deterministic hash of secret content is content metadata, not a label

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — trustworthy characteristics (privacy-enhanced)EU AI Act — data governance (concept level)

Membership inference — learning whether a specific item is inside a store — is a recognized privacy attack, and NIST AI RMF's privacy-enhanced trustworthy characteristic is judged against the composed system, not against each column's intent. The same verdict forced the data map honest: the claim of one deliberate plaintext metadata column was false, and the row now enumerates every plaintext column with a rationale. Pricing the attacker's recompute capability and documenting what is actually exposed exemplifies Domain III.A and the data-governance obligations the EU AI Act sketches for high-risk systems.

AIGP takeaway Deterministic derivatives of protected content answer membership questions; key them with a secret, and audit every 'only a hash' for an oracle.
Act VLesson 111unit 94 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When you add the first instance of a capability a system was built to forbid, build the seam before the feature

The failure

BlarAI was air-gapped by absence — no runtime module imported a network client — and the egress_guard, a socket-layer watchdog, welded that shut: any stray connect tripped a latched kill-switch. Then the Lead Architect decided to remove the air-gap, and the first feature wanting out was UC-003: read a URL, hand back cleaned text. The temptation was to let that feature open its socket; the build did the opposite — one door, guarded_fetch.fetch_external, four ordered fail-closed rules: an SSRF (server-side request forgery) check, the Policy Agent's per-URL verdict, a fetch inside a one-fetch, always-auto-revoked widening of the allowlist (the permitted-destination list), and an injection scan. Two choices carried the weight. The door ships with no adjudicator (verdict-giver) wired, so today every fetch DENIES. And consent is reserved for the ESCALATE tier — a fingerprint on every ALLOW trains rubber-stamping — recorded as an ADR-027 amendment, because it moves where the human gate sits.

Evidence: 2026-06-10 — The door we cut into the wall · commits b7471dd, 663c7fa
Before you turn the page

The air-gap is coming down and the first network feature is ready to open its own socket — what must exist before that feature ships, and what does every later feature inherit from it?

Act VLesson 111unit 94 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When you add the first instance of a capability a system was built to forbid, build the seam before the feature

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VLesson 111unit 94 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When you add the first instance of a capability a system was built to forbid, build the seam before the feature

The lesson

When you add the first instance of a capability a system was built to forbid, build the seam before the feature: one guarded door every future caller must pass through, never a socket per feature — and when the change moves where a human gate sits, encode it as an ADR (Architecture Decision Record) amendment, not code alone. Ship the seam honest: with no real authority to consult, register nothing and fail closed — a documented TODO, never a fabricated verdict.

Growing from it

A first instance sets the pattern: without a mandatory seam, every later feature opens its own socket, and the once-forbidden capability proliferates beyond any single point of policy, audit, or revocation. Build your choke point before the feature that needs it, so every future caller inherits the same ordered checks. Two corollaries travel with it. First, when no honest in-process authority exists to consult, wire nothing — a stub that answers ALLOW when nobody asked is a silent open door; the documented TODO is the fail-closed shape. Second, expect the fail-closed control to fight the legitimate use it must now permit: a real HTTP client connects to the numeric address a name resolved to, never the allowlisted name, which would have tripped the kill-switch on the first genuine fetch. Teach the control the legitimate path narrowly — pin the resolved address, at the allowlisted port, dropped on revoke — without loosening deny-by-default for anything else.

Act VLesson 111unit 94 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When you add the first instance of a capability a system was built to forbid, build the seam before the feature

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — GOVERNEU AI Act — human oversight (concept level)
