NIST AI RMF's GOVERN function is the cross-cutting one — policies, accountability structures, risk culture — and a single sanctioned door is GOVERN made structural: one point where policy, audit, and revocation apply to every future network caller. Recording the consent posture as an ADR amendment, with the rejected blanket-consent alternative on the record, is the documentation the EU AI Act's human-oversight concept assumes: the record says where the human gate sits, and why. The work exemplifies Domain III.A — governing the designing and building of the AI system.

AIGP takeaway Introduce a forbidden capability through one governed door, fail closed where no authority exists, and record the human-gate placement as policy.
Act VLesson 112unit 95 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Audit the request side of every interception seam, not just the reply side

The failure

The #655 ingest surface let the operator paste a web article into chat with /ingest, and the downstream pipeline did everything right: cleaned it, quarantined it, approval-gated it, encrypted it, and labeled it UNTRUSTED_EXTERNAL. An independent Lead-Architect-session verification then found the channel built around every one of those defenses: the gateway (the component that parses chat commands) persisted the operator's full /ingest message — up to ~40 KB of raw, pre-cleaning web text — as a role='user' turn (a stored chat message attributed to the operator) in sessions.db, and the prompt-history filter forwards all user turns verbatim into later prompts. An identical uncleaned copy rode straight into the model's context as a trusted user turn on the next message — not a bypass of the defenses, a parallel path that never met them. The assistant-side preview was already correctly excluded: the reply was defended, the request left wide open.

Evidence: 2026-06-10 — The paste that walked in through the front door
Before you turn the page

The pipeline cleans, quarantines, encrypts, and labels the pasted article as untrusted — what happens to the chat message that delivered it, and what reads that message next?

Act VLesson 112unit 95 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Audit the request side of every interception seam, not just the reply side

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
~40 KBraw, pre-cleaning web text persisted per /ingest as a trusted user turn
Act VLesson 112unit 95 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Audit the request side of every interception seam, not just the reply side

The lesson

Audit the request side of every interception seam, not just the reply side: a command surface that persists its own raw input as a user turn re-injects that input into future prompts with user-level trust, no matter how well the downstream pipeline guards the same content. The fix persists a labels-only stub — /ingest <article: {N} words, doc {uuid8}> — because absence cannot be forwarded; content-bearing markers rot and scope-creep.

Growing from it

The mechanism: a new feature's defenses are built along its own forward pipeline, while the act of invoking the feature also rides a pre-existing channel — here the chat transcript — whose forwarding semantics were decided long before the feature existed. Every interception seam has two sides: the reply you return, and the residue the request itself leaves behind. When you add a command that carries content into a conversational system, trace where a copy of the raw input lands, what re-reads that store later, and with what trust level it re-enters. Then chase derived forms one level further: here even a fallback label leaked the content's full plaintext digest (its content fingerprint) into the transcript through the approval message. The durable lock is a dump-scan test — prove the content is absent from a full dump of the store, raw and cleaned forms both.

Act VLesson 112unit 95 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Audit the request side of every interception seam, not just the reply side

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MAPNIST AI RMF — trustworthy characteristics (privacy-enhanced)

NIST AI RMF's MAP function establishes context — the system, its data flows, and their risks — and this failure is a mapping gap: a copy of untrusted external content flowed on a path the risk treatment never covered. The trustworthy-AI characteristic at stake is privacy-enhanced; a trust label means little while an unlabeled copy travels in parallel. Auditing both sides of the seam and locking the absence with a dump-scan test exemplifies Domain III.A — governing the designing and building of the AI system across every path the data takes.

AIGP takeaway Map every path data takes, not just the feature's pipeline; an ungoverned parallel copy defeats the governed one.
Act VLesson 121unit 96 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

At a security boundary, prefer a wire format with exactly one legal encoding per payload

The failure

BlarAI's design rule ADR-030 §3 promises that hostile fetched HTML is parsed inside the Hyper-V guest — an isolated virtual machine — never in the host process that holds the unsealed DEK (data-encryption key). The plumbing hit a hard number: one vsock frame (the host-to-VM socket transport) carries at most 65,536 bytes, while a fetched page is allowed 262,144 — four times the frame. Pages must cross as chunks, and the tempting scheme was the loose one: sender picks any chunk sizes, receiver accumulates until the declared total. Rejected — a loose receiver cannot distinguish a malformed sender from a hostile one. What shipped in shared/ipc/parse_channel.py is deterministic framing: every chunk except the last carries exactly 45,000 raw bytes (60,000 after base64, the bytes-as-text encoding), and the receiver validates each frame against its single legal size. Even 45,000 was earned: the 48,000 first budget overflowed on paper at the worst legal frame.

Evidence: 2026-06-11 — The boundary earns its plumbing: a wire contract for hostile bytes · commits 16323cb, 23f667e
Before you turn the page

A receiver that tolerantly accepts whatever chunk sizes the sender chose — what can it no longer distinguish, and what does that cost at a hostile boundary?

Act VLesson 121unit 96 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

At a security boundary, prefer a wire format with exactly one legal encoding per payload

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
48,000→45,000raw bytes per chunk — the first budget overflowed the worst legal frame on paper65,536vsock frame cap in bytes; a fetched page is allowed 262,144 — four times the frame
Act VLesson 121unit 96 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

At a security boundary, prefer a wire format with exactly one legal encoding per payload

The lesson

When the receiver can compute the single valid shape of every frame from the first frame's declaration, validation degenerates to equality checks, and every attack or corruption class becomes a first-frame or per-frame rejection. 'Tolerant reader' is a virtue in friendly protocols; at a security boundary it is a euphemism for unbounded trust. The 64 KB cap looked like an obstacle; it was the design pressure that made nothing about a message negotiable after its first frame.

Growing from it

Why does looseness fail at a boundary? Every degree of freedom you grant a sender is state the receiver must tolerate without being able to judge — a loose accumulator cannot tell a malformed peer from a hostile one, so it extends trust it cannot verify, and accumulate-until-done is how unbounded-buffer bugs are born. When you design any interface that untrusted data crosses, remove degrees of freedom until exactly one legal encoding per payload remains: validation becomes equality, and rejection happens at the earliest possible frame — here, an oversize declaration dies on chunk 0, before any buffering. Two companion habits: do the worst-case arithmetic on paper, where the 48,000 budget failed before production ever could, then lock that arithmetic with a test asserting the worst legal payload fits the envelope. Constraints like a 64 KB frame cap are not obstacles; they are design pressure — use them.

Act VLesson 121unit 96 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

At a security boundary, prefer a wire format with exactly one legal encoding per payload

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentOECD AI Principles — robustness, security and safetyEU AI Act — robustness and cybersecurity (concept level)

Designing the channel that hostile content crosses is Domain III.A work — governing the designing and building of the AI system — and the deterministic wire contract exemplifies the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety: the boundary fails predictably, at the first bad frame, instead of tolerantly. The EU AI Act's high-risk obligations pair robustness with cybersecurity at concept level; a one-legal-encoding contract, documented so a later builder cannot mis-assume it, is that idea expressed as engineering.

AIGP takeaway One legal encoding per payload turns boundary validation into equality checks; a tolerant reader at a hostile boundary is unbounded trust.
Act VI of 8 · 5 lessons · lessons 14–83 (thematic, not contiguous) · 1 on the core path

Judgment and diagnosis — verify before you conclude

Evidence first: probing premises, instruments and alarming conclusions before acting on them

Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and UseDomain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development

Five lessons, one discipline: a conclusion is not evidence, however strongly it feels like one. The act trains diagnostic judgment — probe an inherited plan before building on it, read the instrument before convicting a suspect, measure a premise before deciding on it, price an alarm against the evidence you already hold, instrument an invisible surface before theorising about it. The AIGP home is Domain IV: evaluating key factors and risks relevant to the decision to deploy (IV.A) and performing the key activities that assess the AI model and system (IV.B); one lesson sits in Domain III.C's monitoring-and-maintenance territory. The framework throughline is NIST AI RMF's MAP function — establish the real context, not the described one — and MEASURE, home of TEVV (test, evaluation, verification and validation).

Act VILesson 14unit 97 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A handoff brief is a map, not proof the terrain is passable — probe the load-bearing unknowns before building on them.

The failure

The voice mission opened with a generous handoff brief — a predecessor session's written plan — naming Whisper-small for hearing, Kokoro-82M for speaking, the wire format, the integration points file-by-file. The temptation was to trust it and start writing the architecture decision record. The operator's correction landed first: the brief had been leaned on as a map without confirming the terrain was buildable. So before any feature code, the three load-bearing unknowns ran on the real Arc 140V GPU: `WhisperPipeline` existed and transcribed a 2.7-second clip in 0.53s; Kokoro did not run on the GPU at all — the installed onnxruntime library had no GPU execution provider — yet measured a real-time factor of 0.30, three-and-a-bit times faster than playback: the brief was wrong in mechanism, right in outcome. The probe also flushed out a pre-existing broken `optimum` exporter — a three-way `transformers 5.3.0`/`optimum 2.1.0.dev0`/`optimum-intel` version mismatch — that the brief never mentioned.

Evidence: 2026-06-02 — Voice, the backend half: measuring the terrain before laying track
Before you turn the page

The brief named the models, the package, the wire format, even the integration points file-by-file. What did all that detail actually prove about whether the plan was buildable?

Act VILesson 14unit 97 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A handoff brief is a map, not proof the terrain is passable — probe the load-bearing unknowns before building on them.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
0.53sWhisper transcribes a 2.7s clip0.30Kokoro real-time factor, CPU not GPU
Act VILesson 14unit 97 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A handoff brief is a map, not proof the terrain is passable — probe the load-bearing unknowns before building on them.

The lesson

A plan inherited from a predecessor — or a vendor, or last quarter's you — carries assumptions that were true then, there, or never. A handoff brief is a map, not proof the terrain is passable: before building on it, probe the load-bearing unknowns on the real system. Verifying an assumption always costs less than building a phase on one that has quietly rotted. Write down the number you measured, not the one you were handed.

Growing from it

The mechanism is misplaced confidence transfer: the better a document reads, the more its detail feels like verification — but detail only proves the author was thorough about what they believed, in their environment, at their time. Hardware, library versions and provider availability drift underneath a written plan, so its claims age silently. You prevent this by triaging before you build: separate the assumptions you could cheaply survive being wrong about from the load-bearing ones an entire phase stands on, then spend the first hour probing only the load-bearing set on the actual target system. Expect a probe to pay twice — it corrects the premise you tested, and it flushes out hazards the plan never mentioned, because touching the real system exposes what reading about it cannot. Inheriting a plan is fine; inheriting its unverified physics is not.

Act VILesson 14unit 97 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A handoff brief is a map, not proof the terrain is passable — probe the load-bearing unknowns before building on them.

The AIGP connection
Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and UseNIST AI RMF — MAPNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)

This is the pre-adoption assessment AIGP Domain IV trains: competency IV.B, performing key activities to assess the AI model and system. Probing Whisper and Kokoro on the target hardware before committing to them exemplifies NIST AI RMF's MAP function — establishing the system's real context, capabilities and deployment environment rather than an inherited description of one — and the measurements taken are MEASURE-function TEVV evidence (test, evaluation, verification and validation) feeding the build decision.

AIGP takeaway Inherited plans are claims, not evidence; assess the model and system in the real deployment context before building on them.
Act VILesson 24unit 98 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A problem the live boot surfaces next to your change makes the change a suspect, not a culprit — let the instrument decide before you reach for the fix.

The failure

Tier 0's egress kill-switch — a fail-closed guard that denies outbound traffic by default — had never run live until the first real boot, and that boot was unusably slow: a photo attach sat for minutes, a voice reply queued five. The guard was the obvious suspect; a deny-by-default control starving a legitimate channel is exactly the self-inflicted denial-of-service its own architecture decision record had warned about. The pull to reach in and loosen it was real. The launcher log said otherwise: the guard armed at 22:05:59 and not a single `EgressDenied` line appeared anywhere in the file — it had refused nothing. The real bottleneck was image grounding — vision analysis running eagerly the moment a photo attaches — monopolising the app's single serialised work lane, so a voice clip captured at 22:10:39 was not handed to the transcriber until 22:15:27, then transcribed in under a second.

Evidence: 2026-06-04 — The slowdown the live boot found, and the log that said it wasn't mine · 2026-06-04 — Off the one lane: the freeze was structural, so the fix had to be
Before you turn the page

If the egress guard really had been the bottleneck, what specific evidence would the launcher log have had to contain — and what did it actually show?

Act VILesson 24unit 98 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A problem the live boot surfaces next to your change makes the change a suspect, not a culprit — let the instrument decide before you reach for the fix.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
22:10:39→22:15:27voice clip capture to handoff22:05:59guard armed; zero denials logged
Act VILesson 24unit 98 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A problem the live boot surfaces next to your change makes the change a suspect, not a culprit — let the instrument decide before you reach for the fix.

The lesson

A problem the live boot surfaces next to your change makes the change a suspect, not a culprit. Suspicion — even well-founded, design-anticipated suspicion — is a hypothesis; the instrument holds the evidence. Read the log before reaching for the fix: had suspicion been trusted over the instrument, an innocent guard would have been "fixed" and the real jam shipped untouched. The live boot tells you that something is wrong; only the log tells you what.

Growing from it

The mechanism is proximity bias: your newest change is the most available explanation for any co-occurring symptom, and when that change is a deny-by-default security control, the story "my guard is starving the system" is so plausible it feels like a completed diagnosis. Plausibility is not causation — it only nominates a suspect. Train the reflex: before modifying anything under suspicion, ask what evidence would have to exist if the suspicion were true — here, denial lines in the log — then go look. Finding that evidence convicts; its absence acquits in minutes, and either way you acted on the instrument rather than the story. Design for this moment in advance, too: a control that records every refusal can be exonerated later; one that refuses silently can never clear its own name. Reading a log costs minutes; weakening a security control on a hunch costs the control.

Act VILesson 24unit 98 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A problem the live boot surfaces next to your change makes the change a suspect, not a culprit — let the instrument decide before you reach for the fix.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASUREEU AI Act — record-keeping and logging (high-risk obligation)

The log that acquitted the guard is what record-keeping exists to enable: the EU AI Act, at concept level, includes record-keeping and logging among its high-risk obligations precisely so behaviour in operation can be reconstructed and faults attributed — a discipline this incident exemplifies. Diagnosing a live system from its instruments rather than its suspects is NIST AI RMF MEASURE-function work — analyzing and tracking risk with evidence — applied to Domain III.C, governing the release, monitoring and maintenance of the model and system.

AIGP takeaway Logs exist so operating faults can be attributed on evidence; a suspected control is convicted or acquitted by its records.
Act VILesson 48core pathunit 99 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A load-bearing factual premise gets verified on disk, not inherited through the review chain.

The failure

The urgency case for the at-rest encryption sprint rested on one claim: that BlarAI's two SQLite stores already held "decades of the user's private data" sitting exposed in plaintext. The architecture decision record asserted it, the sprint's Strategic Design Vision asserted it, the security roadmap asserted it, the design-gate presentation asserted it — and none had checked. The Lead Architect, flipping the ADR to ACCEPTED, finally looked at the files: `substrate.db` held 107 chunks (~400 KB), `sessions.db` held 59 sessions / 376 turns (~250 KB) — all build-phase test scaffolding. Nothing real had ever been stored. The premise was false, inherited author-to-author across four documents because it was plausible, repeated, and motivating. The honest framing was the opposite of urgency — encrypt now, before first real use, so real data is born encrypted — stronger and more defensible than the claim it replaced.

Evidence: 2026-06-05 — The premise nobody checked: there were never "decades of data on disk" · commit 804a0ef
Before you turn the page

Four authoritative documents asserted the same premise about the data on disk. How many independent verifications does that repetition amount to?

Act VILesson 48core pathunit 99 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A load-bearing factual premise gets verified on disk, not inherited through the review chain.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
decades of the user's private data→107 chunks (~400 KB)the claim vs substrate.db59 sessions / 376 turnsall of sessions.db (~250 KB)
Act VILesson 48core pathunit 99 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A load-bearing factual premise gets verified on disk, not inherited through the review chain.

The lesson

A load-bearing factual premise gets verified on disk, not inherited through the review chain. When a claim is doing real work in a decision, its truth is a row count or a file size — something you check — not something you carry forward because the previous document said it. Plausible-and-repeated reads as settled fact; each author inherits it from the last, and repetition manufactures authority that verification never conferred.

Growing from it

Review chains diffuse verification: every reader assumes someone upstream checked, so a claim no one ever tested accrues authority with each repetition — and the more motivating the claim, the safer it feels, because it justifies work everyone already wants to do. Notice the tell in yourself: when a fact is carrying urgency, scope or risk in your argument, ask where in the chain it was measured. If you cannot point at the measurement, run it — load-bearing facts about a system are usually one command away: a row count, a file size, a directory listing. Then keep the second half of this story: the corrected premise was stronger, not weaker. Honest framings tend to be, because they survive the scrutiny the inherited claim was never given. The check costs a minute; a decision premised on fiction costs its credibility.

Act VILesson 48core pathunit 99 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A load-bearing factual premise gets verified on disk, not inherited through the review chain.

The AIGP connection
Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and UseNIST AI RMF — MAPISO/IEC 42005 — AI system impact assessment

Domain IV.A asks the practitioner to evaluate the key factors and risks relevant to a deployment decision — here, the central factor (what data was actually exposed) had been asserted through four documents and evaluated by none. Checking the stores is NIST AI RMF MAP-function work: establishing the system's real context and the risks specific to it before acting. And grounding exposure claims in the actual system is the discipline ISO/IEC 42005's AI system impact assessment formalizes — impact statements drawn from evidence, not narrative.

AIGP takeaway Repetition across documents is not verification; a premise feeding a governance decision gets measured against the actual system.
Act VILesson 82unit 100 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Verify an alarming conclusion against the evidence you already have before you escalate it — a symptom read correctly can still yield a wrong conclusion.

The failure

The #615 guest-boundary test — proving the host can reach the isolation VM over Hyper-V sockets — failed `connect(): bad family`. The symptom was read correctly: this virtual environment's Python has no `socket.AF_HYPERV`. The conclusion escalated off it was wrong: that the approach was broken, wanted a ctypes rewrite (a low-level workaround), maybe a re-scope of the #598 air-gap gate. The Lead Architect pushed back in four words — "didn't we test AF_HYPERV already?" — and he was right: the Phase-2 evidence held a real host-to-Alpine AF_HYPERV round-trip. The failure was a mundane interpreter-version gap: Python 3.11.9 predates CPython's `AF_HYPERV`; the system's 3.14 has it. The design had been sound the whole time. The same instinct, applied a beat later, paid correctly: the C2 tier's `CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED` was called test-tier — not a real mutual-TLS gap — because the stubbed-GPU tier ran the identical handshake and passed.

Evidence: 2026-06-07 — The on-chip session: the alarm I withdrew, and the tiers the chip locked · commit 09c62b1 · commit f4bdea4
Before you turn the page

The symptom was real — that Python truly had no AF_HYPERV. What already-existing evidence did the "design is broken" conclusion fail to explain?

Act VILesson 82unit 100 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Verify an alarming conclusion against the evidence you already have before you escalate it — a symptom read correctly can still yield a wrong conclusion.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Python 3.11.9→3.14venv vs system interpreterfour wordsthe pushback that re-priced it
Act VILesson 82unit 100 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Verify an alarming conclusion against the evidence you already have before you escalate it — a symptom read correctly can still yield a wrong conclusion.

The lesson

An alarming red is a hypothesis about the system, not a finding. Before escalating, price it against evidence you already have — a prior validation artifact, a passing sibling tier, a one-line probe. A symptom read correctly can still yield a wrong conclusion, and an unconfirmed alarm can manufacture an architecture crisis that is not there. Proactive fixing is for confirmed defects; escalation spends the decision-maker's attention and your credibility, so verify the conclusion before spending either.

Growing from it

The mechanism: under an alarming symptom, severity transfers from the feeling to the conclusion — "this fails here" becomes "this can never work" — and a correctly-read symptom makes the wrong conclusion feel verified, because part of your chain genuinely was. Before escalating, run two checks. First: what existing evidence bears on this — has the thing ever worked anywhere (an earlier phase's validation artifact, a passing sibling test)? If it has, your conclusion must explain that evidence too, and "the design is broken" usually cannot. Second: what one-line probe discriminates between broken design and broken environment? Run it before you write the escalation. The discipline cuts both ways — the same check, applied a beat later, correctly confirmed a different alarming failure was environmental. You owe the decision-maker a verified conclusion, not a verified symptom.

Act VILesson 82unit 100 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Verify an alarming conclusion against the evidence you already have before you escalate it — a symptom read correctly can still yield a wrong conclusion.

The AIGP connection
Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and UseNIST AI RMF — MANAGENIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)

Escalating a risk is NIST AI RMF MANAGE-function work — responding to and communicating risk — and MANAGE is only as good as the MEASURE work beneath it. The Phase-2 round-trip artifact was existing TEVV evidence (test, evaluation, verification and validation) that should have priced the alarm before it reached the decision-maker. Domain IV.A's evaluation of factors and risks relevant to the deployment decision demands exactly this: conclusions that account for the validation evidence already on record before they reshape a gate.

AIGP takeaway Communicate confirmed risks, not alarmed hypotheses — check the conclusion against existing validation evidence before escalating.
Act VILesson 83unit 101 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A WinUI element is in the UI-Automation tree only when THREE separate conditions hold — and when one is missing, instrument the tree before theorising.

The failure

A folder of graphical-interface tests had collected green and run never — marked `winui`+`slow`, they skip whenever `BlarAI.Desktop.exe` is absent, i.e. every headless run. The first time the screen drove them, six of thirteen failed: all in the harness, none in the app. The fix took three foreground runs, because Windows's UI Automation (UIA) tree — the structure automated tests search for controls — is a projection gated on three preconditions a hand-driven session hides: realization (window foreground, render pass run); peer existence (only Controls get an AutomationPeer, the adapter that makes an element findable — layout panels like `Grid` get none); and non-empty/visible projection (an empty or Collapsed `ListView` is not in the tree at all). Each run taught one precondition, mistaken for the whole; twice the screen failed a "fix" just shipped — a re-anchor onto the peer-less `RootGrid`, a self-contradictory `MessagesList.exists()` on the empty-state greeting. Runs went 7→9, 9→11, then 13/13.

Evidence: 2026-06-07 — The harness that had never met the window it was driving · commit d3fe427
Before you turn the page

Three runs each fixed one precondition and were declared complete. What single diagnostic, added at the start, would have replaced the theorising?

Act VILesson 83unit 101 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A WinUI element is in the UI-Automation tree only when THREE separate conditions hold — and when one is missing, instrument the tree before theorising.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
six failed→13/13first foreground run to third
Act VILesson 83unit 101 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A WinUI element is in the UI-Automation tree only when THREE separate conditions hold — and when one is missing, instrument the tree before theorising.

The lesson

A WinUI element is in the UI-Automation tree only when three separate conditions hold — realization, peer existence, and non-empty/visible projection — because the tree is a projection, not the visual layout or the markup you wrote. When an element is missing, instrument the tree before theorising: dump the AutomationId-bearing descendants and the item count. Printing what the framework actually exposes is the cheapest way to stop guessing at preconditions your own hands-on sessions never let you see.

Growing from it

The trap is hidden preconditions: the harness author's own sessions satisfy them invisibly — the window is foreground, in hand — so the mental model gets built from the markup that was written, not from the projection the automation interface actually exposes. Worse, each newly discovered precondition arrives feeling complete, so you fix one and re-offend on the next; here the screen rejected two just-shipped fixes, each itself an instance of the bug being fixed. The portable counter-move: when automation cannot find what your eyes can see, make instrumentation the first act — print what the interface exposes, and the next failure becomes reading an answer instead of theorising another guess. The wider habit: a test that has never executed against its real subject is a hypothesis, and counting it as coverage is self-deception — verify fixes with the instrument, never with your model of the framework.

Act VILesson 83unit 101 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A WinUI element is in the UI-Automation tree only when THREE separate conditions hold — and when one is missing, instrument the tree before theorising.

The AIGP connection
Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and UseNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)

NIST's trustworthy-AI characteristic "valid and reliable" rests on assessment that actually executes — a test that collects green but never runs is assurance no one has. Driving the real window and instrumenting what the interface exposes is MEASURE-function TEVV work (test, evaluation, verification and validation), and the gap exposed is TEVV's own distinction: the harness was verified against the markup as written, but only the screen could validate it against the system doing its real job in its real context. That assessment activity is Domain IV.B's competency.

AIGP takeaway Never-executed tests are hypotheses, not assurance; validate against the real system and instrument it rather than theorise.
Act VII of 8 · 19 lessons · lessons 9–116 (thematic, not contiguous)

Operating at fleet scale — process and systems maturity

Multi-agent coordination, state that crosses boundaries, and the mechanics that bite at scale

Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceDomain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development

This act trains the governance of the build system itself: what happens when one builder becomes several concurrent agents sharing a machine, a repository, an event loop, and a memory ceiling. Its lessons pair the IAPP AIGP Body of Knowledge's Domain I competencies — establishing and communicating organizational expectations (I.B) and policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle (I.C) — with Domain III's governance of designing-and-building and of release, monitoring and maintenance (III.A, III.C). The cross-cutting anchor is the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's GOVERN function: accountability structures, processes, and risk culture that hold under scale. ISO/IEC 42001's plan-do-check-act management-system pattern recurs wherever an observed process failure fed a structural redesign of the process — this act's signature move.

Act VIILesson 9unit 102 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Two Claudes on one machine share one HEAD.

The failure

The model-sharing build went out as a second Claude session in a separate window, treated as fully independent. It was not: both sessions shared one physical working tree, and therefore one HEAD — git's pointer to whichever branch a checkout currently sits on. While the successor's Phase 1 commit was finishing on its feature branch, the predecessor ran `git checkout main` on the shared tree to commit a journal entry, and the successor's commit landed on `main` during the roughly thirty-second window that checkout opened. The tell was a benchmark partially failing: the second run launched after the branch flipped back, and the on-disk script no longer carried the flag the first run had used. Recovery traced the reflog and merged `main` into the feature branch — non-destructive, and cleaner than cherry-picking, because the same commit reaches both branches.

Evidence: 2026-05-22 — Sharing the 14B (the second me, picking up the brief) · 2026-05-22 — The instrument, and a hand-off to a second me
Before you turn the page

Two sessions in two windows look fully independent — what single piece of state did they still share, and at which layer does parallel work actually collide?

Act VIILesson 9unit 102 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Two Claudes on one machine share one HEAD.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
thirty-second windowall it took to collide
Act VIILesson 9unit 102 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Two Claudes on one machine share one HEAD.

The lesson

Two sessions can share a hard disk; they cannot share a checkout. Whenever a second agent must commit on the same repository, give it its own working directory with `git worktree add` — the fix is isolation, not 'be careful.' The companion rule: the predecessor stays off the GPU while the successor benchmarks, because a busy machine poisons the data the other session is paying for.

Growing from it

The failure happened because the isolation you perceived (two windows, two conversations) lived at a different layer than the coupling that mattered (one filesystem, one HEAD). Concurrency bugs are almost never visible at the layer where you think about the actors; they live in the shared mutable state underneath them. Before you parallelize anything — agents, scripts, teammates, CI jobs — enumerate what they share: a checkout, a port, a database file, a cache directory, a GPU. For each shared thing, either give every actor an isolated copy or accept explicit serialization. And notice how the failure announced itself: not at the collision, but later, as a confusing downstream symptom — a benchmark missing its flag. When a tool misbehaves inexplicably during parallel work, ask what invisible state another actor may have flipped under you.

Act VIILesson 9unit 102 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Two Claudes on one machine share one HEAD.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceNIST AI RMF — GOVERN

This maps to AIGP Domain I.C — establish policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle: a multi-agent development operation needs written procedures that isolate shared infrastructure, because vigilance does not scale across actors. It exemplifies the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's GOVERN function — the cross-cutting home of processes, roles and accountability structures — where the worktree rule became standing procedure rather than a habit held by one agent.

AIGP takeaway Parallel actors collide in shared state, not in plain view; isolate the substrate by standing procedure, not by carefulness.
Act VIILesson 10unit 103 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Continuity of intent across agents is a file-system property, not a memory property.

The failure

The journal discipline that compounds the operator's portfolio — failures kept in, trade-offs named, lessons distilled into a numbered list — had been paid for by the Layer 3 over-build and a near-quit, and it worked exactly as long as it lived in the head of whichever agent was carrying the work. The moment a fresh session opened without it, it stopped working. When the operator asked whether the agents to come would automatically write entries in the shape his IAPP AIGP certification track needs, the honest answer was that nothing guaranteed it. So the discipline was codified as a CLAUDE.md section — 'Build-Journal Discipline (NON-NEGOTIABLE)' — that every session reads at startup: when an entry is required, the form, the content, the anti-patterns. The entry called the codification 'the apology for assuming continuity that the file system did not actually provide.'

Evidence: 2026-06-02 — Making the journal discipline standing instruction
Before you turn the page

If every rule your project relies on lived only in the head of whoever worked yesterday, what survives the first morning they are not in the room?

Act VIILesson 10unit 103 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Continuity of intent across agents is a file-system property, not a memory property.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VIILesson 10unit 103 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Continuity of intent across agents is a file-system property, not a memory property.

The lesson

If you want the next session to do the thing this one learned, write it down where the next session will read it — not where you will remember it. Standing instructions belong in the file every agent inherits at startup; the journal entry beneath them is the evidence the instruction earned its standing. A rule held in one place should be held in two.

Growing from it

The mechanism is turnover: every fresh agent — and every new hire, contractor, or future-you — starts from the written substrate, not from anyone's memory, so a rule that exists only in a head has a lifetime of exactly one session. You can recognize the gap whenever you hear 'we always do X' about something written nowhere an inheritor actually reads. The prevention is to identify the startup surface in any project — the onboarding document, the configuration file, the checklist that genuinely gets read first — and put standing rules there, then treat every repeated verbal correction as a signal that a written rule is missing. Culture you cannot point to in a file is culture that resets to zero at the next handoff.

Act VIILesson 10unit 103 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Continuity of intent across agents is a file-system property, not a memory property.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceNIST AI RMF — GOVERNISO/IEC 42001 — AIMS documented management system

This is AIGP Domain I.B — establish and communicate organizational expectations for AI governance: an expectation is established only when it reaches every actor by default, in writing. It exemplifies the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's GOVERN function, which locates risk culture and policies as the enabling foundation, and ISO/IEC 42001, whose artificial-intelligence management system (AIMS) rests on documented expectations rather than individual memory.

AIGP takeaway Expectations bind only when documented where every actor reads them by default; memory does not carry governance across handoffs.
Act VIILesson 11unit 104 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A setting that must cross a privilege boundary has to travel by a channel that crosses it.

The failure

Wiring the new native window into the launcher, the obvious switch was an environment variable — `BLARAI_UI=winui`. But the launcher self-elevates: it relaunches itself through a UAC (User Account Control) prompt to gain the Administrator rights Hyper-V needs, and a Windows elevation relaunch begins with a fresh environment. A variable set in the un-elevated shell simply does not exist in the elevated process, so the selection would have silently fallen back to the terminal. Worse, no test could ever catch it: the elevated, GPU-bound path cannot run on a headless machine, so every available check would pass while the real path quietly did the wrong thing — only the operator at the live screen would see it. Reading `request_elevation` instead of trusting a mental model showed it forwards `sys.argv` but not the environment; the choice became a `--winui` command-line flag, which rides across.

Evidence: 2026-06-02 — Phase 3: a real model behind the window, and a flag that survives elevation · 2026-06-02 — The same privilege boundary, the other thing that doesn't cross it
Before you turn the page

An elevated relaunch starts the new process deliberately clean — of everything your code relies on, what is explicitly forwarded across, and what silently vanishes?

Act VIILesson 11unit 104 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A setting that must cross a privilege boundary has to travel by a channel that crosses it.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VIILesson 11unit 104 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A setting that must cross a privilege boundary has to travel by a channel that crosses it.

The lesson

When a value must survive a trust or process boundary — UAC, sudo, a container edge, a service account — it has to travel by a channel defined to cross that boundary, not one that merely happens to work on the same side of it. Read the crossing code to learn what is actually forwarded; do not trust the mental model.

Growing from it

Boundaries drop ambient state on purpose — that is their security function: a fresh environment, a default working directory, a clean import context are how an elevated process avoids inheriting an attacker's influence. Your code, though, leans on ambient state constantly, which is why this same boundary bit the project three more times in other costumes — package imports, drag-and-drop, file dialogs. The portable skill: whenever your value must cross an elevation, a sandbox, a container, or a remote hop, list what the boundary's contract explicitly forwards (here, the argument list) versus what it deliberately discards (the environment), and route through the forwarded channel. Then make the crossing fail loudly when the value is absent — the silent fallback was what made this bug invisible to every test.

Act VIILesson 11unit 104 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A setting that must cross a privilege boundary has to travel by a channel that crosses it.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — secure and resilient (trustworthy characteristic)EU AI Act — cybersecurity obligation (concept level)

This maps to AIGP Domain III.A — govern the designing and building of the AI model and system: how configuration crosses privilege boundaries is a design decision with security consequences. It exemplifies the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's secure-and-resilient trustworthy characteristic, and, at concept level, the EU AI Act's cybersecurity expectation for high-risk systems — controls must hold across exactly the trust edges they are built upon.

AIGP takeaway Values that must cross a privilege boundary ride channels defined to cross it; ambient state is dropped by design.
Act VIILesson 25unit 105 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

One shared event loop makes any single blocking call a whole-app freeze — fix it structurally (off the loop), not locally (make the blocker faster).

The failure

The backend serves every request through one event loop — the single scheduler all asynchronous work shares — via `loop.run_until_complete(...)` per connection. The voice methods knew this: transcribe and synthesize push their blocking model calls onto worker threads with `asyncio.to_thread`, leaving the loop free. The document methods three functions above them in the same file did not: `_m_load_document` called the blocking loader directly on the loop, and `_m_store_attachment` did it twice. So the moment a photo attach ran its eager vision pass, there was no loop left to service the microphone frame that arrived a minute later. The live boot put numbers on it: a voice clip captured at 22:10:39 was not handed to the transcriber until 22:15:27, then transcribed in under a second. The five-minute voice queue was not slowness — it was a thread starved of its turn.

Evidence: 2026-06-04 — Off the one lane: the freeze was structural, so the fix had to be · 2026-06-04 — The slowdown the live boot found, and the log that said it wasn't mine
Before you turn the page

When everything queues behind one slow operation, is the operation too slow — or is it standing in the only lane the whole application has?

Act VIILesson 25unit 105 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

One shared event loop makes any single blocking call a whole-app freeze — fix it structurally (off the loop), not locally (make the blocker faster).

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
22:10:39→22:15:27voice clip, capture to handoffunder a secondthe transcription itself
Act VIILesson 25unit 105 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

One shared event loop makes any single blocking call a whole-app freeze — fix it structurally (off the loop), not locally (make the blocker faster).

The lesson

When work is slow on a single-loop — or single-threaded, single-lane — system, ask what is holding the lane before asking how to speed the work. Fix it structurally, moving every blocking call off the loop, not locally by making one blocker faster. Then pin the property with a test asserting the work ran on a different thread than the loop, so regression announces itself.

Growing from it

The mechanism is contention on a shared serialized resource: one loop means any single blocking occupant freezes everything behind it, and per-component tests structurally cannot feel it because the contention is the bug. The tell worth training your eye on is asymmetry — the same discipline applied to one set of handlers and not to the set beside them, the gap that opens when a pattern lives in one author's head instead of where the next feature inherits it. In any future system, identify the shared lanes (an event loop, a UI thread, a single worker queue) and make 'nothing blocks the lane' an explicit, written, test-locked invariant. And prefer locking the real invariant — thread identity — over a timing proxy that flakes under load.

Act VIILesson 25unit 105 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

One shared event loop makes any single blocking call a whole-app freeze — fix it structurally (off the loop), not locally (make the blocker faster).

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — valid and reliable (trustworthy characteristic)NIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)

This maps to AIGP Domain III.A — govern the designing and building of the AI system: availability under concurrency is an architectural property, not a tuning knob. The structural fix plus a regression test on the off-loop invariant exemplifies the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's valid-and-reliable trustworthy characteristic and its MEASURE function — home of TEVV (test, evaluation, verification and validation) — verifying the system meets its concurrency contract rather than observing the symptom fade.

AIGP takeaway One blocking call on a shared loop freezes the app; fix structurally off the loop and test-lock the invariant.
Act VIILesson 27unit 106 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A deferred resource risk comes due as a hard failure, not a graceful one — and a fail-closed default can disguise it as another subsystem's bug.

The failure

The vision MVP cached its ~5 GB model in a module global and never freed it. On the integrated Arc 140V GPU there is no separate video memory — that 5 GB lived in the same 32 GB system RAM as the always-resident 14B (~8.7 GB) — so over a multimodal session memory saturated and the host froze at 100%. The risk had been deferred in the design record as the 'open hard problem' — deferred without being bounded. And it wore a disguise: under memory pressure a vision turn exceeded the 120-second IPC (inter-process communication) timeout, the gateway's fail-closed default denied, and the user saw a 'validation error' — naming the policy validator for what was resource exhaustion three layers away. The log proved the disguise: the orchestrator was still working 95 seconds after the gateway gave up, ingesting at 08:28:34 a turn already denied at 08:26:53.

Evidence: 2026-06-04 — The deferred problem came due, wearing another bug's face
Before you turn the page

When the screen says 'validation error,' how would you know whether policy refused the answer — or whether nothing answered before the door closed?

Act VIILesson 27unit 106 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A deferred resource risk comes due as a hard failure, not a graceful one — and a fail-closed default can disguise it as another subsystem's bug.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
~5 GBVLM cached, never freed08:26:53→08:28:34denied, yet still working
Act VIILesson 27unit 106 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A deferred resource risk comes due as a hard failure, not a graceful one — and a fail-closed default can disguise it as another subsystem's bug.

The lesson

Free what you cache on a shared, finite resource — an unfreed cache is a slow leak that ends in a freeze, not a warning — and never defer a resource risk without bounding it. Separately, make a fail-closed control report why it closed: 'denied by policy' and 'denied because nothing answered in time' are different failures with different owners.

Growing from it

Two mechanisms compounded here. First, resource exhaustion degrades nonlinearly — fine, fine, swap-thrash, frozen — so a deferred memory risk does not ramp politely toward you; it arrives as a cliff. Any time you write 'we'll deal with the resource cost later,' attach a bound the same day: an eviction, a cap, an alarm. Second, safety defaults that collapse failure causes manufacture false diagnoses: the deny was correct, but its surface message pointed at the wrong subsystem, and you can lose days debugging the scapegoat. Audit your own fail-closed paths for whether they can say why they closed, and when debugging, trust the layer that names causes — the log distinguished 'default deny (no result)' from a policy denial — over any surface that collapses them.

Act VIILesson 27unit 106 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A deferred resource risk comes due as a hard failure, not a graceful one — and a fail-closed default can disguise it as another subsystem's bug.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MANAGEEU AI Act — record-keeping and logging (concept level)

This maps to AIGP Domain III.C — govern the release, monitoring and maintenance of the model and system: a risk accepted at design time must carry a bound and must surface diagnosably in operation. It exemplifies the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's MANAGE function — residual risks are tracked and monitored, not parked — and, at concept level, the EU AI Act's record-keeping and logging obligation: the log, not the user-facing surface, held the truth.

AIGP takeaway Bound every deferred resource risk; make fail-closed controls name their reason, or they misdirect the response to a scapegoat.
Act VIILesson 35unit 107 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Verify the merge landed where you meant it to — a shell's working directory can drift, and a loop's "OK" echo is not proof.

The failure

The decision-free half of the Tier-1 security wave ran as five specialist agents in parallel, each in its own git worktree (a separate working folder sharing one repository), each branch carrying one audit finding. The merge loop printed 'OK' five times, the commit graph looked clean, and `main` appeared to advance to the integrated commit. It had not: the shell's working directory had silently drifted into one of the agents' worktrees, so all five merges landed on that worktree's branch while `main` never moved. The verify-on-disk habit caught it before a single test ran — grep the merged tree for each of the five control markers (all present), then `git rev-parse HEAD` in the main repository by absolute path, which still sat at the pre-wave commit. The integrated commit was a clean descendant of `main`, so recovery was a fast-forward, not a rewrite.

Evidence: 2026-06-04 — Five locks in parallel, and the merge that landed on the wrong branch · commit 6994b49
Before you turn the page

Your merge loop printed 'OK' five times — what exactly did each 'OK' prove, and relative to which working directory was it true?

Act VIILesson 35unit 107 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Verify the merge landed where you meant it to — a shell's working directory can drift, and a loop's "OK" echo is not proof.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
five timesOK echoed; main never moved
Act VIILesson 35unit 107 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Verify the merge landed where you meant it to — a shell's working directory can drift, and a loop's "OK" echo is not proof.

The lesson

A command that prints success proves the operation ran somewhere — not that it ran where you intended. Confirm the branch, the HEAD, and the working directory by absolute path before trusting any multi-step git sequence, and never trust the loop's own success echo over the repository's own answer. This is the test-as-oracle trap wearing version-control clothes.

Growing from it

The mechanism: the echo and the intent verify different propositions. 'OK' meant each merge succeeded relative to wherever the shell happened to stand — a moving frame of reference you did not know had moved. Any scripted sequence in a stateful environment can succeed against the wrong target: the wrong directory, the wrong branch, the wrong cluster, the wrong account. The discipline that generalizes is postcondition checking from ground truth: after the operation, read back the one fact you actually care about — did MAIN advance? — by absolute reference, from the system of record, not from the tool's progress output. Build that read-back into the loop itself, so the verification cannot be skipped on the day you are tired.

Act VIILesson 35unit 107 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

Verify the merge landed where you meant it to — a shell's working directory can drift, and a loop's "OK" echo is not proof.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)ISO/IEC 42001 — Annex A life-cycle control themes

This maps to AIGP Domain I.C — policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle: integrating parallel work is a lifecycle procedure that needs its own verification step. It exemplifies the verification half of TEVV (test, evaluation, verification and validation) under the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's MEASURE function — confirm the change met its specification rather than accept an activity signal — and ISO/IEC 42001's Annex A control themes on the AI system life cycle: change lands with evidence.

AIGP takeaway Success echoes prove activity, not placement; verify the postcondition from the system of record before trusting multi-step change.
Act VIILesson 41unit 108 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

To land on a branch others are actively pushing to, use an operation that can only fast-forward — it refuses rather than races.

The failure

Landing the web-search work, the shared `main` branch would not hold still: it advanced three times mid-merge (`9e693be → e2fe1bd → 3fbc4d7`), each time moments after 'nothing else is running' had been said in good faith. A plain merge would have raced the moving target, and a drifted working directory had already once landed five merges on the wrong branch. The guard was `git merge --ff-only` — an operation that can only fast-forward, advancing `main` onto a commit that already contains it, and refusing otherwise. When `main` moved, the fast-forward declined; the new `main` was re-integrated — the only conflict, every single time, was the journal, resolved by keeping both sides and renumbering the incoming lesson, 36 to 39 to 40 — and the land retried. Bounded and deterministic: a moving target can never produce a wrong land.

Evidence: 2026-06-05 — The main that wouldn't hold still, and the merge that refused to race · commits 768346a, a3e18f7, 0fd859a
Before you turn the page

If the shared branch moves in the instant between your check and your merge, which protects you: a more careful human, or an operation that refuses?

Act VIILesson 41unit 108 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

To land on a branch others are actively pushing to, use an operation that can only fast-forward — it refuses rather than races.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
9e693be → e2fe1bd → 3fbc4d7main moved mid-merge36 to 39 to 40the lesson number, per retry
Act VIILesson 41unit 108 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

To land on a branch others are actively pushing to, use an operation that can only fast-forward — it refuses rather than races.

The lesson

To land on a branch others are actively advancing, use an operation that structurally cannot be wrong about the race — one that refuses rather than guesses. And treat 'nothing is running' as a claim to verify against the repository's own HEAD by absolute path, not a fact to trust: a verbal all-clear and a parallel commit are simply not synchronized.

Growing from it

The mechanism is the check-then-act gap: between reading the shared state and acting on it, another actor can move it, and no amount of asking more carefully closes that window. The durable fix is choosing primitives whose failure mode is refusal — fast-forward-only merges, compare-and-swap updates, optimistic locking with retry — so a race becomes a clean, bounded retry instead of a silent corruption. Train yourself to spot the shape: anywhere you check a shared thing and then change it (a branch, a record, a config, a booking), ask what happens if it moves between your two steps. If the answer is 'something wrong lands quietly,' replace the careful human with an operation that detects the conflict for you.

Act VIILesson 41unit 108 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

To land on a branch others are actively pushing to, use an operation that can only fast-forward — it refuses rather than races.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceNIST AI RMF — GOVERNISO/IEC 42001 — plan-do-check-act

This maps to AIGP Domain I.C — policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle: change procedures are strongest when the procedure enforces itself. It exemplifies the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's GOVERN function — policy embedded as mechanism rather than reliance on individual care — and ISO/IEC 42001's plan-do-check-act management-system pattern: an observed race fed a structural improvement to how change lands.

AIGP takeaway Land concurrent change with operations that refuse on conflict; verbal all-clears are never synchronized with shared state.
Act VIILesson 42unit 109 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A coordination discipline that works for one actor becomes the bottleneck when many run at once — scale the mechanism, not just the rule.

The failure

With a security session, a substrate session, and a web-search session each running in its own worktree, `BUILD_JOURNAL.md` — the single file the journal discipline appends to on every substantive change — became a shared-append hotspot git cannot auto-merge. It bit three times in one day: the five-branch Tier-1 wave, the web-search branch's merge of `main`, and a spec-decode commit landing a lesson on top. The rule that would have prevented it already existed for sub-agents — 'report your entry text, don't touch the journal' — but was never given to sessions, so the collision simply moved up a layer. The sharpest contention was lesson numbers: two sessions both reaching for '39.' The fix stopped sharing the file: a per-session fragment inbox, `docs/journal_fragments/`, one file per entry, folded into the canonical journal serially by one integrator, with numbers assigned only at fold-in.

Evidence: 2026-06-05 — The journal that conflicted with itself, three times · commit 9a01fc2
Before you turn the page

The journal rule was already fixed for sub-agents — why did the identical collision bite three times in one day, one layer up?

Act VIILesson 42unit 109 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A coordination discipline that works for one actor becomes the bottleneck when many run at once — scale the mechanism, not just the rule.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
three timesjournal collisions in one day
Act VIILesson 42unit 109 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A coordination discipline that works for one actor becomes the bottleneck when many run at once — scale the mechanism, not just the rule.

The lesson

When a single-actor discipline must scale to many concurrent actors, the medium it rides on has to scale with it, or the discipline becomes the friction it was meant to prevent. Stop sharing the contended file: give each actor an exclusive inbox, serialize the fold at a quiet point, and assign the most contended state only at the serial step.

Growing from it

The discipline was sound; its substrate — one append-only file — had a concurrency ceiling of one actor, and the jam was structural, not behavioral. You will meet this shape constantly: the sign-off spreadsheet, the shared changelog, the single approval queue — all fine at one actor, all contention at N. The general fix is partition-then-merge: per-actor exclusive resources, one serialized integrator, and the scarcest shared state (here, the running lesson numbers) allocated only at the serial point so parallel actors never race for it. The second, subtler skill: when you patch a coordination rule at one layer, ask which other layers the same collision can climb to — a fix scoped to sub-agents left sessions to rediscover the identical jam.

Act VIILesson 42unit 109 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A coordination discipline that works for one actor becomes the bottleneck when many run at once — scale the mechanism, not just the rule.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceNIST AI RMF — GOVERNISO/IEC 42001 — continual improvement (plan-do-check-act)

This maps to AIGP Domain I.C — policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle: procedures must be designed for the scale at which they will actually operate. It exemplifies the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's GOVERN function — processes that hold as the organization grows — and ISO/IEC 42001's continual-improvement, plan-do-check-act pattern: an observed process failure produced a structural redesign of the mechanism, not a reminder to try harder.

AIGP takeaway Scale the mechanism a discipline rides on, not just the rule; shared-append state caps coordination at one actor.
Act VIILesson 52unit 110 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

The worktree-isolation quirk can switch the main checkout's branch, so `git -C <main-path>` is not enough — verify the branch before every main-tree commit.

The failure

Launching background worktree builders did not merely move the shell's working directory — it switched the main checkout itself (`C:/Users/mrbla/BlarAI`) onto an agent's feature branch. So a commit made with an explicit `git -C <main-path>` — believed safe precisely because the path was pinned — landed on the feature branch; `main` never advanced; and it was reported as 'on main.' The path and the branch are independent facts: a directory can be the main worktree while its HEAD sits on a feature branch. Recovery stayed inside the never-discard rule, refined by the operator into something cleaner: preserve the stray staged reversal on a throwaway branch (`14d8a7b` on `wip/s14-ea4-staged-reversal-artifact`), switch the checkout back to `main`, cherry-pick the real commit (`4e58a8b`) — no reset, no force, nothing thrown away. The branch-guard added immediately afterward caught the very next recurrence and blocked a wrong-branch merge.

Evidence: 2026-06-05 — Locking the door the sessions come through, and the two gremlins at the gate · commits 4e58a8b (cherry-picked recovery), 14d8a7b (preserved reversal)
Before you turn the page

You pinned the directory with `git -C` — which independent fact about that directory's state did the pin leave completely unverified?

Act VIILesson 52unit 110 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

The worktree-isolation quirk can switch the main checkout's branch, so `git -C <main-path>` is not enough — verify the branch before every main-tree commit.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VIILesson 52unit 110 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

The worktree-isolation quirk can switch the main checkout's branch, so `git -C <main-path>` is not enough — verify the branch before every main-tree commit.

The lesson

Pinning the path does not pin the branch: verify `git branch --show-current == main` before every commit or merge on the main tree, because the checkout's HEAD can be switched under you by tooling you did not watch. And recover non-destructively — preserve stray state on a throwaway branch, switch back, cherry-pick — so the incident leaves evidence, not wreckage.

Growing from it

The deeper failure is guard design: the explicit `-C` path was a guard built against the previous incident — a drifting working directory — and it verified one variable while the environment mutated a different one. A safety argument must name the invariant it protects ('commits land on main'), not the last incident it answers, or each recurrence arrives through the unverified variable. In any environment where tools mutate your context behind you (build agents, background jobs, IDE integrations), re-read the full precondition set at the point of action, every time, cheaply — a one-line check beats a post-hoc forensic. And only call a guard real after you have watched it catch a genuine recurrence; this one did, on the very next merge.

Act VIILesson 52unit 110 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

The worktree-isolation quirk can switch the main checkout's branch, so `git -C <main-path>` is not enough — verify the branch before every main-tree commit.

The AIGP connection
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI GovernanceNIST AI RMF — MANAGE

This maps to AIGP Domain I.C — policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle: a recurring operational hazard answered with a standing checkpoint at the point of action, and an incident recovery that preserved all state for audit. It exemplifies the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's MANAGE function — risk response plus residual-risk monitoring: the treatment was installed, then observed to catch the next recurrence, which is what demonstrates a treatment works.

AIGP takeaway Path and branch are independent facts; guard the invariant at the action point, and trust guards only after they catch.
Act VIILesson 58unit 111 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A side effect placed before the point a process can still fork-and-exit runs more than once.

The failure

The launcher self-elevates through a UAC (User Account Control) prompt: the original un-elevated process fires a relaunch, returns 0, and an elevated child carries on. The per-boot certificate mint — a disk-write side effect that issues fresh mutual-TLS certificates each boot — was placed after the dev-mode interlock but before the elevation block, on a reading of 'after the interlock, before service construction' as one contiguous region. It is not: the elevation's return-0 cuts that span in two, so both processes would run the mint — a double-mint, with the short-lived non-elevated process writing the certificates first. The merge-gate review caught the placement before it shipped. The fix moved the mint to a 'Step 1.5' after `admin_ok` is recorded and before the VM starts, so it fires exactly once, in the elevated process.

Evidence: 2026-06-06 — The door that was built but never had a lock · commits bcda777, 3fa2315; merged 336fcc2
Before you turn the page

Your startup step runs 'once at boot' — how many times does that span execute when the process relaunches itself elevated halfway through it?

Act VIILesson 58unit 111 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A side effect placed before the point a process can still fork-and-exit runs more than once.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Act VIILesson 58unit 111 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A side effect placed before the point a process can still fork-and-exit runs more than once.

The lesson

In a startup sequence that can fork and exit — a UAC relaunch is effectively a fork — the position of a side-effecting step relative to the fork point is load-bearing. Side effects belong after the last point the process can still bail out and hand off to a successor, not merely somewhere in the pre-service span.

Growing from it

Reading startup code linearly hides that control flow can split: 'after X' and 'before Y' can both be true on both sides of a fork, so a step that looks placed once actually executes per process. Map the points where your process can relaunch, re-exec, retry, or be restarted by a supervisor — every one is a multiplier on any side effect upstream of it, and an innocent-looking helper (mint, write, register, send) is still a side effect. Either place effects after the final exit point or make them genuinely idempotent and prove it. Note also what caught this: an independent reviewer reading for placement rather than for the step's own correctness — a second pair of eyes asking a different question than the author asked.

Act VIILesson 58unit 111 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A side effect placed before the point a process can still fork-and-exit runs more than once.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)NIST AI RMF — secure and resilient (trustworthy characteristic)

This maps to AIGP Domain III.A — govern the designing and building of the AI system: where security material is generated in the process lifecycle is a design property, and independent review caught it pre-release. It exemplifies TEVV (test, evaluation, verification and validation) under the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's MEASURE function — the merge gate as a verification activity — and the secure-and-resilient characteristic: duplicate key material written by the wrong process is an integrity hazard.

AIGP takeaway Side effects upstream of a fork-and-exit point run in every process; place them after the last handoff.
Act VIILesson 64unit 112 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When you fix a defect of a particular shape, scan the codebase for the same shape before you close — finding one and fixing only one is a half-fix.

The failure

The session store and the substrate store were built in parallel under the same encryption ADR (Architecture Decision Record), with the same bulk-read pattern — and therefore the same defect: one undecryptable row aborts the entire bulk loop. The session-store fix landed — quarantine the bad row, keep serving the healthy ones — and should have triggered an immediate check of its sibling. It did not. The substrate store sat with the identical fault at two sites: `_load_embed_cache`, which runs at construction time, where a single practice-era row's decrypt failure would stop the store from opening and the Assistant Orchestrator from starting at all; and `_search_kind`, where one bad hit aborts the whole retrieval. The class-audit caught it one boot away from the identical startup brick; the fix was the same shape, applied to the sibling, with the brick-locking test rewritten to assert the quarantine instead.

Evidence: 2026-06-06 — When the fix was already written — applying it to the sibling store · commit 3f61b41, merged 6fe1fcc
Before you turn the page

You just fixed a defect born from a shared pattern — what does that finding tell you about every other component built from the same pattern?

Act VIILesson 64unit 112 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When you fix a defect of a particular shape, scan the codebase for the same shape before you close — finding one and fixing only one is a half-fix.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
Two sitessame defect in the sibling
Act VIILesson 64unit 112 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When you fix a defect of a particular shape, scan the codebase for the same shape before you close — finding one and fixing only one is a half-fix.

The lesson

Two components built from the same template carry the same design defects, so when you fix a defect of a particular shape, scan the codebase for the same shape before you close. Finding one and fixing only one is a half-fix; the reflex that closes the class, not the instance, is what stops the sibling biting in production next.

Growing from it

Defects travel through reuse channels — copied patterns, shared design records, parallel builds from one specification — so a bug found in one instance is evidence about a population, not a point event. At fix time, ask: what else was built from this template, under this decision, by this process, at the same time? Then audit those sites inside the same change, while the defect's shape is sharp in your mind — one grep and an afternoon when systematic, a production outage per sibling when skipped. Make the class-audit part of your definition of 'done' for any defect fix, and notice the special trap here: a passing test had even locked the sibling's wrong behavior, so the audit must re-examine the tests too.

Act VIILesson 64unit 112 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

When you fix a defect of a particular shape, scan the codebase for the same shape before you close — finding one and fixing only one is a half-fix.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentISO/IEC 42001 — continual improvement (plan-do-check-act)NIST AI RMF — MANAGE

This maps to AIGP Domain III.C — govern the release, monitoring and maintenance of the model and system: corrective action in a managed system extends to the class of nonconformity, not the single instance. It exemplifies ISO/IEC 42001's continual-improvement, plan-do-check-act pattern — the check found a defect, the act covered its siblings — and the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's MANAGE function: treat the risk wherever it recurs.

AIGP takeaway A defect with a shape indicts every sibling built from the same template; close the class before closing the ticket.
Act VIILesson 73unit 113 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A long-"deferred" code path is where bugs hide from each other — activating it is a discovery exercise, not a one-liner.

The failure

The #615 guest boundary rode through several sprints as one deferred line item — 'Windows AF_HYPERV addressing fix' — preserved but never exercised. Activating it revealed the single label had hidden two opposite defects that masked each other because nothing in the path had ever run. The shared transport had the right protocol but built a Linux-style `(cid, port)` address, where Windows Hyper-V sockets address by a `(VmId, ServiceId)` pair of GUID strings — the host literally cannot reach a guest with the integer form. The gateway had the right GUID address but omitted the protocol argument, raising `WSAEPROTOTYPE` (WinError 10041) before its address was ever used. Neither had ever thrown. The oracle that settled the correct form was not documentation or memory but `phase2_gates/evidence/vsock_validation.json` — the February validation run on the real hardware, recording the working topology. The evidence file was the spec.

Evidence: 2026-06-07 — The two bugs that hid behind one "deferred" label
Before you turn the page

If a code path has never once executed, how many faults could it contain — and which of its comments has ever been tested against reality?

Act VIILesson 73unit 113 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A long-"deferred" code path is where bugs hide from each other — activating it is a discovery exercise, not a one-liner.

The mechanism, drawn
rendering diagram…
twoopposite defects, one labelWinError 10041raised before the address
Act VIILesson 73unit 113 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A long-"deferred" code path is where bugs hide from each other — activating it is a discovery exercise, not a one-liner.

The lesson

When you finally exercise dormant code, budget for plural defects and find the empirical oracle — the artifact recording the configuration that actually worked — rather than trusting in-code comments that describe intent, not working form. Sibling to 'committed is not done until verified': dormant is not correct until exercised.

Growing from it

Execution is the only force that surfaces a whole class of faults, so a path that never runs accumulates them invisibly — and multiple faults along one dead path produce zero signal, because the first failure never happens in a way that would expose the second. Deferral also rots descriptions: comments record what the author intended at write time, not what ever worked. So treat 'activate the deferred thing' as a discovery exercise — scaffold tests around it, budget time for several faults, and expect the bugs to be of opposite kinds. Before reviving anything dormant, hunt for the empirical record — a validation log, a known-good configuration, a captured working run — and promote it to the specification, because it is the only description guaranteed to have once been true.

Act VIILesson 73unit 113 / 126failuremechanismlesson + growthAIGP

A long-"deferred" code path is where bugs hide from each other — activating it is a discovery exercise, not a one-liner.

The AIGP connection
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI DevelopmentNIST AI RMF — MEASURE (TEVV)
